# Module 1: Sensors — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "sensor" with "transducer"** in definition questions — a 1-mark deduction is standard if the examiner feels you over-claimed.
> 2. **Always specify units** in numerical answers. Writing "$\tau = 8$" without "seconds" loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. **In first-order numerical problems**, ALWAYS state the formula explicitly before substituting values. This earns the *method mark* even if arithmetic goes wrong.
> 4. **Never write "accuracy = 100%"** unless the question states so — accuracy is a percentage error, not a score.
> 5. **For classification questions**, the "active vs passive" split is the *most* important. Listing examples under the *wrong* category (e.g., LDR as active) is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a sensor. Distinguish between an active and a passive sensor with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sensor** is a device that detects a physical stimulus (measurand) such as heat, light, pressure, motion, or chemical composition and converts it into an electrical signal suitable for measurement, display, or control processing. *(1 mark)*

**Active Sensor (Self-Generating):** Produces an electrical output directly from the measurand without requiring external excitation. Energy conversion is from physical to electrical. *(1 mark)*
- Examples: Thermocouple (Seebeck effect), Piezoelectric pressure sensor, Photovoltaic cell.

**Passive Sensor (Parameter-Varying):** Changes one of its electrical properties (R, L, or C) in response to the measurand and needs an external excitation source to produce a usable output. *(1 mark)*
- Examples: Thermistor (R varies with T), Strain gauge (R varies with strain), LDR (R varies with light).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any three static characteristics of a sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Accuracy:** The degree of closeness of a measured value to the true value. It is expressed as a percentage of the true or full-scale reading. *(1 mark)*

2. **Sensitivity:** The ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input measurand: $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$. A higher sensitivity implies a larger output per unit input. *(1 mark)*

3. **Hysteresis:** The maximum difference in output for the same input value, when the input is approached from increasing and decreasing directions, expressed as a percentage of the full-scale output. It indicates the sensor's inability to reproduce the same output for identical input history. *(1 mark)*

(Other valid characteristics: Linearity, Repeatability, Resolution, Drift, Span — any 3 accepted.)

---

## Part B — Long-Answer / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule (2024 Scheme ESE):** In Module 1, students answer **ONE out of TWO** 14-mark questions. The split is typically:
> - **Part (a)** — 7 marks: Theory / Derivation / Description.
> - **Part (b)** — 7 marks: Numerical / Design / Comparative analysis.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q. A** (a) **Explain the static and dynamic characteristics of a sensor in detail. Define any five static characteristics with appropriate formulas.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A first-order temperature sensor has a time constant of 6 seconds and a static sensitivity of 0.5 V/°C. The sensor is initially at 30°C. At $t = 0$, it is placed in an environment of 90°C. Calculate:**
- **(i) The output voltage at $t = 12$ s.** *(3 marks)*
- **(ii) The time required for the output to reach 80% of its final value.** *(2 marks)*
- **(iii) The output voltage at steady state.** *(2 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution**

### Part (a) — Static and Dynamic Characteristics

**Static characteristics** describe sensor behavior when the measurand is constant or slowly varying. The most important static characteristics are: *(1.5 marks for the description)*

1. **Accuracy:** Closeness to the true value.
$$\% Accuracy = \frac{\vert V_m - V_{true} \vert}{V_{true}} \times 100$$

2. **Sensitivity:** Ratio of output change to input change.
$$S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$

3. **Linearity:** Deviation of the actual transfer curve from a straight line.
$$\% Non\text{-}linearity = \frac{\text{Max deviation from ideal line}}{FSO} \times 100$$

4. **Hysteresis:** Difference in output for the same input during increasing vs. decreasing sweeps.
$$\% Hysteresis = \frac{\vert y_{up} - y_{down} \vert_{max}}{FSO} \times 100$$

5. **Repeatability:** Closeness of multiple readings under identical conditions.
$$\% Repeatability = \frac{\Delta_{max\;spread}}{FSO} \times 100$$

**Dynamic characteristics** describe sensor behavior when the measurand is time-varying. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Zero-order sensor:** $y(t) = K x(t)$ — instantaneous response.
- **First-order sensor:** $\tau \dot y + y = K x$. Has a single time constant $\tau$, with step response $y(t) = KX(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ and rise time $t_r = 2.2\tau$.
- **Second-order sensor:** $\ddot y + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot y + \omega_n^2 y = K\omega_n^2 x$. Defined by natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$.

**Other static characteristics (for completeness):** Resolution, Drift, Span, Range, Precision. *(any 1 mark)*

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $\tau = 6 \, s$, $K = 0.5 \, V/°C$, $T_0 = 30°C$, $T_s = 90°C$.

#### (i) Output at $t = 12$ s

Step input: $\Delta T = 90 - 30 = 60°C$.

Total output change at steady state:
$$\Delta V = K \cdot \Delta T = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \, V$$

Initial output (taking $V_0 = 0$ as reference for simplicity):
$$V(12) = K \Delta T \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = 30 \left(1 - e^{-12/6}\right) = 30 \left(1 - e^{-2}\right)$$

$$V(12) = 30 \times (1 - 0.1353) = 30 \times 0.8647 = 25.94 \, V$$

$$\boxed{V(12) \approx 25.94 \, V}$$

#### (ii) Time to reach 80% of final value

Set $V(t) = 0.8 \times 30 = 24 \, V$:

$$0.8 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-t/6} = 0.2$$

$$t = -6 \cdot \ln(0.2) = 6 \times 1.6094 = 9.66 \, s$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 9.66 \, s}$$

#### (iii) Output at steady state

At $t \to \infty$, the term $e^{-t/\tau} \to 0$, so:

$$V_{ss} = K \cdot \Delta T = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \, V$$

$$\boxed{V_{ss} = 30 \, V}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. B** (a) **With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a measurement system. Identify and describe the role of each block.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Derive the step response of a first-order sensor. A first-order sensor with a time constant of 5 s is subjected to a step input of 10 V. Determine:**
- **(i) The output at $t = 0$ and $t = 5$ s.** *(3 marks)*
- **(ii) The output at $t = 15$ s and compare it with steady state.** *(2 marks)*
- **(iii) The time required for the output to reach 95% of its final value.** *(2 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution**

### Part (a) — Block Diagram of a Measurement System

The complete functional architecture consists of **six blocks** in cascade: *(Block diagram — 3 marks, Description — 4 marks)*

1. **Measurand (Primary Sensing Element):** The physical quantity to be measured (temperature, pressure, force, etc.). It is the input to the measurement system.

2. **Sensor Element / Transducer:** Converts the measurand into an electrical signal. For example, a thermocouple converts temperature to millivolts.

3. **Signal Conditioner:** Amplifies, filters, and linearizes the weak sensor output. Common sub-blocks:
   - **Amplifier** (operational amplifier circuits).
   - **Filter** (low-pass to remove 50/60 Hz line noise).
   - **Linearizer** (look-up table or polynomial correction).

4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Converts the conditioned analog signal into a digital code for processing by a microcontroller or computer.

5. **Processor / Display Unit:** Microcontroller/DSP/PLC reads the digital value, runs calibration, and either displays it (LCD, 7-segment, HMI) or transmits it (UART, I²C, SPI, Ethernet).

6. **Output / Actuator (optional):** In a closed-loop system, the processor may actuate a control element based on the sensor reading.

**Real-world example:** A digital weight scale — measurand: weight; sensor: strain gauge in a Wheatstone bridge; conditioner: instrumentation amplifier; ADC: 24-bit sigma-delta; processor: ARM Cortex-M0.

### Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical

#### Derivation (Brief Recall)

Starting from $\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K x(t)$ with $x(t) = X \cdot u(t)$ and $y(0) = 0$, solving with integrating factor $e^{t/\tau}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(e^{t/\tau} y\right) = \frac{KX}{\tau} e^{t/\tau}$$

Integrating from 0 to $t$:

$$e^{t/\tau} y(t) = KX\left(e^{t/\tau} - 1\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,y(t) = KX \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)\,}$$

#### Given: $K = 1$ (assumed unity, as no sensitivity given), $X = 10 \, V$, $\tau = 5 \, s$.

#### (i) Output at $t = 0$ and $t = 5$ s

- At $t = 0$: $y(0) = 10 (1 - e^0) = 10(1 - 1) = 0 \, V$
- At $t = 5$ s: $y(5) = 10(1 - e^{-1}) = 10(1 - 0.3679) = 6.32 \, V$

#### (ii) Output at $t = 15$ s and comparison with steady state

- At $t = 15$ s: $y(15) = 10(1 - e^{-3}) = 10(1 - 0.0498) = 9.50 \, V$
- At $t \to \infty$: $y_{ss} = 10 \, V$

Comparison: $9.50 / 10 = 95\%$, meaning at $t = 15$ s the sensor has covered 95% of the step input, leaving only 5% deviation.

#### (iii) Time to reach 95% of final value

Set $y(t) = 0.95 \times 10 = 9.5 \, V$:

$$0.95 = 1 - e^{-t/5} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-t/5} = 0.05$$

$$t = -5 \ln(0.05) = 5 \times 2.9957 = 14.98 \, s \approx 15 \, s$$

This matches the result from (ii) — confirming that $t = 3\tau$ corresponds to 95% response (settling time within 5%).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Sensors**

### Core Definitions
- ✅ **Sensor:** Converts a physical stimulus into an electrical signal.
- ✅ **Transducer:** Converts energy from one form to another (broader term).
- ✅ **Measurand:** The physical quantity being measured.
- ✅ **Actuator:** Converts electrical signal into physical action (the *opposite* of a sensor).

### Sensor Classification (Memorize the binary splits)
- ✅ **Active vs Passive** — Active generates electricity; Passive needs excitation.
- ✅ **Analog vs Digital** — Continuous vs discrete output.
- ✅ **Physical / Chemical / Biological** — by detection mechanism.

### Static Characteristics (Equation List)
- ✅ Sensitivity = $\Delta y / \Delta x$
- ✅ Non-linearity = (max deviation / FSO) × 100%
- ✅ Hysteresis = ($\vert y_{up} - y_{down} \vert_{max}$ / FSO) × 100%
- ✅ Accuracy = ($\vert V_m - V_{true} \vert / V_{true}$) × 100%
- ✅ Repeatability, Resolution, Drift, Span, Range.

### Dynamic Characteristics
- ✅ **Zero-order:** $y = Kx$ (instantaneous).
- ✅ **First-order:** $\tau \dot y + y = Kx$. Step response: $y(t) = KX(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- ✅ **Second-order:** defined by $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$. Step response depends on damping regime.

### Critical Numerical Anchors
- ✅ At $t = \tau$: response = 63.2% of final value.
- ✅ Rise time: $t_r = 2.2\tau$.
- ✅ Settling time (2%): $t_s = 4\tau$.
- ✅ Settling time (5%): $t_s = 3\tau$.
- ✅ Cutoff frequency: $\omega_c = 1/\tau$ (first-order).

### Block Diagram of Measurement System
- ✅ Measurand → Sensor → Signal Conditioner → ADC → Processor → Output Display.

### Engineering Utility
- ✅ Sensor selection criteria: range, accuracy, sensitivity, environment, cost, output type, power.
- ✅ Real-world domains: automotive, medical, industrial, IoT, aerospace, consumer.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Compare active and passive sensors"* (a frequent 7-mark question), use a **markdown table** with at least 4–5 rows (Power Source, Output, Examples, Cost, Complexity) — this is the *highest-yield* presentation format that examiners reward with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Actuator (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** An actuator is a **physical device that converts an input control signal — typically electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy — into a corresponding physical output such as mechanical motion, force, torque, heat, or light.** In the broader context of a mechatronic or sensor-actuator system, the actuator is the *output-side counterpart* of a sensor and is responsible for *executing* the action dictated by the controller.

Mathematically, an actuator is a **transducer operating in the reverse direction** of a sensor:

$$a_{out}(t) = f\big(s_{in}(t)\big)$$

where $s_{in}(t)$ is the input command signal (electrical/control) and $a_{out}(t)$ is the resulting physical action (displacement, force, pressure, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Muscle" of a Machine

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a **human body**:
> - **Sensors** = the **five senses** (eyes, ears, skin) that *detect* the environment.
> - **Controller** = the **brain** that *processes* the sensory information.
> - **Actuator** = the **muscles** that *move the limbs* in response to a brain signal.
>
> Just as a muscle converts a neural (electrical-chemical) signal into a physical contraction, an actuator converts a low-power electrical/control signal into a high-power physical action.

A simple everyday example is the **micro-speaker in a mobile phone**: a tiny electrical audio signal is converted into *mechanical vibration* of a diaphragm, which pushes air to produce **sound** — that is an actuator in operation.

## 1.3 Classification Overview (KTU High-Yield)

Actuators are broadly classified by their **energy domain**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Major Classes of Actuators (as per KTU Module 1):**
> 1. **Hydraulic Actuators** — use incompressible liquid (oil) under pressure.
> 2. **Pneumatic Actuators** — use compressible gas (air) under pressure.
> 3. **Electrical / Electromagnetic Actuators** — use current-induced magnetic fields (motors, solenoids, relays).
> 4. **Thermal Actuators** — use expansion/contraction due to heat (bimetallic strips, shape-memory alloys).
> 5. **Mechanical Actuators** — convert rotary to linear motion (rack & pinion, lead screw, cam).

A more modern classification also includes:

6. **Piezoelectric Actuators** — use the converse piezoelectric effect.
7. **MEMS / Micro-actuators** — micron-scale devices fabricated using IC processes.
8. **Smart-material Actuators** — shape memory alloys (SMA), electroactive polymers (EAP), magnetostrictive materials.

## 1.4 Why Actuators Matter in Engineering

| Domain | Typical Actuator Use |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Fuel injectors, brake-by-wire, throttle valves |
| **Robotics** | Servo motors, pneumatic grippers |
| **Aerospace** | Hydraulic flight-control surfaces, piezoelectric control surfaces |
| **Medical Devices** | Micropumps, drug-delivery micro-actuators |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Speakers, haptic feedback (vibration motors) |
| **Industrial Automation** | Hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders in CNC, assembly lines |

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Standard Parameters)

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standard metrics** are universally used to compare actuators in KTU examinations:
>
> - **Stroke (s):** Maximum displacement the actuator can produce, measured in **m** (or mm/µm for micro-actuators).
> - **Force (F):** Output pushing/pulling force, measured in **N**.
> - **Torque (τ):** Rotational output, measured in **N·m**.
> - **Power (P):** Rate of doing work, in **W**.
> - **Bandwidth (BW):** Maximum frequency of operation, in **Hz**.
> - **Efficiency (η):** Ratio of useful output power to input power, in **%**.
> - **Response Time (τ_r):** Time to reach 63.2 % of final value (one time-constant), in **s**.
> - **Resolution:** Smallest controllable increment, in **m** or **°**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydraulic Actuators

### Working Principle
A hydraulic actuator uses an **incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil)** pressurized by a pump. The pressurized fluid acts on a piston inside a cylinder, producing linear or rotary motion.

### Operational Steps
1. The **electric motor** drives a **hydraulic pump**, which pressurizes the working fluid.
2. A **control valve** (often solenoid-operated) directs the high-pressure oil to either side of the piston.
3. The **pressure differential** across the piston face creates a net force, producing motion.
4. The fluid is returned to the reservoir through a low-pressure line.

### Key Equations

The force produced by a hydraulic cylinder:

$$F_h = P \cdot A_p = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

where:
- $F_h$ = hydraulic force (**N**)
- $P$ = gauge pressure of fluid (**Pa**)
- $A_p$ = piston cross-sectional area (**m²**)
- $D$ = piston diameter (**m**)

The fluid flow rate relates to piston velocity:

$$Q = A_p \cdot v_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_p = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

The hydraulic power delivered:

$$P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = F_h \cdot v_p$$

### Why / How Insight
> [!NOTE]
> **Why incompressible fluid?** Liquids are nearly incompressible, so nearly **100 % of the pressure energy is converted to mechanical work** with minimal loss — this is why hydraulic systems can produce *enormous forces* (up to thousands of kN) from a compact device.

---

## 2.2 Pneumatic Actuators

### Working Principle
A pneumatic actuator uses **compressed air** (a compressible gas) as the working medium. The compressed gas acts on a diaphragm or piston to produce motion. Because gases are compressible, the **stiffness and precision are lower** than hydraulic, but pneumatic systems are **cleaner, lighter, and cheaper**.

### Key Equations

Force on a pneumatic piston (single-acting):

$$F_p = P_{sup} \cdot A_p - P_{atm} \cdot A_p - F_{spring}$$

For a double-acting cylinder (pressure applied on both sides alternately):

$$F_p = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot A_p$$

where $P_1$ and $P_2$ are the pressures on either side of the piston.

The work done per stroke:

$$W_p = F_p \cdot s = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot A_p \cdot s$$

Air consumption (at atmospheric conditions):

$$Q_{air} = \frac{V_{stroke}}{t} \cdot \frac{P_{sup}}{P_{atm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pneumatic vs Hydraulic:** Pneumatic systems operate at **5–10 bar** typically (vs 100–700 bar for hydraulic), so they deliver lower forces but at **higher speeds**, and are used where cleanliness is critical (food industry, semiconductor manufacturing, medical tools).

---

## 2.3 Electrical / Electromagnetic Actuators

### 2.3.1 Solenoid (Linear Electromagnetic Actuator)

A solenoid is a coil of insulated wire wound around a movable ferromagnetic plunger (armature). When current flows, a magnetic field is established that **pulls the plunger into the coil**, producing linear motion.

### Magnetic Field Inside a Long Solenoid

$$B = \mu_0 \cdot n \cdot I = \mu_0 \cdot \frac{N}{L} \cdot I$$

where:
- $B$ = magnetic flux density (**T**)
- $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- $n = N/L$ = turns per unit length (**turns/m**)
- $I$ = current through coil (**A**)
- $N$ = total number of turns
- $L$ = length of solenoid (**m**)

### Magnetic Force on a Solenoid Plunger

The force pulling the plunger inward is approximately:

$$F_{mag} \approx \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{2\mu_0} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 L^2}$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the plunger.

### Inductance of a Solenoid

$$L_{sol} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A}{l}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The force is proportional to $I^2$**, not $I$. This means a solenoid always pulls (regardless of current direction) — it is **inherently a unidirectional actuator**.

### 2.3.2 DC Motor (Rotational Electromagnetic Actuator)

A DC motor converts electrical energy into rotational mechanical energy via the interaction of current-carrying conductors with a magnetic field.

The torque produced by a DC motor:

$$\tau = K_t \cdot I_a$$

The back-EMF generated:

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$

For an ideal motor ($K_t = K_e$ in SI units):

$$\tau \cdot \omega = V \cdot I_a - I_a^2 R_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{out} = \tau \cdot \omega$$

The no-load speed:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e}$$

The stall torque:

$$\tau_{stall} = \frac{K_t \cdot V}{R_a}$$

### 2.3.3 Voice Coil Actuator (VCA)

A VCA is essentially a loudspeaker-type actuator: a coil moves linearly inside a permanent magnetic field. The force is:

$$F_{VCA} = B \cdot I \cdot l \cdot N$$

where $l$ is the length of wire in the field and $N$ is the number of turns. VCAs offer **high bandwidth** and are used in **hard-disk head positioning** and **active suspension**.

---

## 2.4 Piezoelectric Actuators

### Working Principle
Piezoelectric actuators exploit the **converse piezoelectric effect**: when an electric field is applied across a piezo crystal (PZT, quartz, BaTiO₃), the material mechanically deforms.

### Key Equations

The induced strain $S$ is:

$$S = d \cdot E$$

where:
- $d$ = piezoelectric charge/strain coefficient (**m/V** or **C/N**)
- $E$ = applied electric field (**V/m**)

The displacement produced:

$$\Delta L = d \cdot V = d \cdot E \cdot t$$

where $t$ is the thickness of the piezo element and $V$ is the applied voltage.

The blocked force (maximum force when displacement is zero):

$$F_{block} = \frac{d \cdot V \cdot A}{s_{11}^E \cdot t}$$

where $s_{11}^E$ is the elastic compliance at constant $E$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoelectric actuators produce tiny displacements (µm to sub-nm) but enormous forces (up to kN)**. They have nanosecond response times, making them ideal for **scanning probe microscopes (AFM/STM)** and **precision optics**.

---

## 2.5 Thermal Actuators

### 2.5.1 Bimetallic Strip

Two metals with **different coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE)** are bonded. Heating causes differential expansion, producing bending.

Deflection of a bimetallic strip (simplified):

$$\delta = \frac{3(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2}{4 t}$$

where:
- $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ = CTE of the two metals (per **°C**)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature change (**°C** or **K**)
- $L$ = length of strip (**m**)
- $t$ = total thickness (**m**)

### 2.5.2 Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Actuator

SMAs (e.g., **Nitinol — Ni-Ti alloy**) "remember" a parent shape. When cooled below the **Martensite finish temperature ($M_f$)** they are easily deformed. When heated above the **Austenite finish temperature ($A_f$)**, they revert to the parent shape with substantial force (recovery stress up to **500 MPa**).

The shape-memory recovery strain:

$$\varepsilon_{rec} = \frac{L_{parent} - L_{deformed}}{L_{parent}} \quad \text{up to } 8\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> SMAs are used in **stents**, **robotic grippers**, and **deployable aerospace structures**. They have high force density but **low bandwidth** (response is slow, ~seconds to minutes) due to thermal cycling.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most-frequently-asked in KTU Module 1 examinations:**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydraulic force | $F = P \cdot A_p = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | N | $P$: Pa, $D$: m |
| 2 | Piston velocity | $v_p = Q/A_p$ | m/s | $Q$: m³/s |
| 3 | Hydraulic power | $P = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v_p$ | W | — |
| 4 | Solenoid field | $B = \mu_0 n I$ | T | $n$: turns/m, $I$: A |
| 5 | Solenoid force | $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | N | $A$: m² |
| 6 | Solenoid inductance | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}$ | H | — |
| 7 | DC motor torque | $\tau = K_t I_a$ | N·m | $K_t$: N·m/A |
| 8 | Back-EMF | $E_b = K_e \omega$ | V | $K_e$: V·s/rad |
| 9 | Voice-coil force | $F = B I l N$ | N | $l$: m |
| 10 | Piezo strain | $S = d E$ | dimensionless | $d$: m/V |
| 11 | Piezo displacement | $\Delta L = d V$ | m | $V$: V |
| 12 | Bimetallic deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{3(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T L^2}{4t}$ | m | $\alpha$: 1/K |
| 13 | SMA recovery stress | $\sigma_{rec} \le 500$ MPa | Pa | — |
| 14 | Power-work | $P = W/t = F v = \tau \omega$ | W | — |
| 15 | Energy stored in spring | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ | J | $k$: N/m, $x$: m |
| 16 | Energy in capacitor | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | $C$: F, $V$: V |

---

## 2.7 Comparative Engineering Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering trade-offs when selecting an actuator:**

| Property | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Electromagnetic | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Force density** | Very high | Medium | Medium | Very high |
| **Speed / Bandwidth** | Medium | High | High | Very high |
| **Precision** | High | Low | Medium | Ultra-high (nm) |
| **Stroke** | Long (m) | Long (m) | Short (mm–m) | Very short (µm) |
| **Cleanliness** | Low (oil leak) | Very high | High | Very high |
| **Cost** | High | Low | Medium | Very high |
| **Typical application** | Earth-mover | Food industry | Robotics | AFM/STM |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force on a Hydraulic Piston

**Problem statement:** A hydraulic cylinder has a piston of diameter $D = 50$ mm. The supply pressure is $P = 70$ bar. Calculate (a) the force produced, (b) the velocity if the flow rate is $Q = 10$ L/min, and (c) the hydraulic power.

### Given Data
- $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $P = 70$ bar $= 70 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 7 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $Q = 10$ L/min $= 10 \times 10^{-3}/60$ m³/s $= 1.6667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

### Part (a) — Force Calculation

Step 1: Cross-sectional area of piston:

$$A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0025}{4}$$

$$A_p = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Step 2: Substitute into the hydraulic force equation:

$$F = P \cdot A_p = (7 \times 10^6) \cdot (1.9635 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{F = 13{,}744.5 \text{ N} \approx 13.74 \text{ kN}}$$

### Part (b) — Velocity

Step 3: Use continuity, $Q = A_p \cdot v_p$:

$$v_p = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{1.6667 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{v_p = 0.0849 \text{ m/s} = 84.9 \text{ mm/s}}$$

### Part (c) — Power

Step 4: Hydraulic power:

$$P_{hyd} = F \cdot v_p = 13{,}744.5 \cdot 0.0849$$

$$\boxed{P_{hyd} \approx 1{,}167 \text{ W} \approx 1.17 \text{ kW}}$$

**Cross-check using $P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q$:**

$$P_{hyd} = (7 \times 10^6) \cdot (1.6667 \times 10^{-4}) = 1{,}166.7 \text{ W} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Solenoid Magnetic Force

**Problem statement:** A solenoid has $N = 1000$ turns, length $L = 0.2$ m, and a plunger cross-section $A = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m². A current $I = 2$ A flows through it. Calculate (a) the magnetic field $B$, and (b) the force on the plunger.

### Given Data
- $N = 1000$, $L = 0.2$ m, $A = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $I = 2$ A, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

### Part (a) — Magnetic Field

Step 1: Turns per unit length:

$$n = \frac{N}{L} = \frac{1000}{0.2} = 5000 \text{ turns/m}$$

Step 2: Magnetic flux density:

$$B = \mu_0 n I = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 5000 \cdot 2$$

$$B = 4\pi \times 10^{-3} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-2} \text{ T}$$

$$\boxed{B \approx 12.57 \text{ mT}}$$

### Part (b) — Force on Plunger

Step 3: Use $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$:

$$F = \frac{(1.2566 \times 10^{-2})^2 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7})}$$

Step 4: Numerator:

$$(1.2566 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 1.5790 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$1.5790 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5790 \times 10^{-8}$$

Step 5: Denominator:

$$2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) = 2.5133 \times 10^{-6}$$

Step 6: Divide:

$$F = \frac{1.5790 \times 10^{-8}}{2.5133 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.283 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 6.28 \text{ mN}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the force is small for a simple air-core solenoid. In real industrial solenoids, a **soft-iron core** is used, which increases $B$ by the relative permeability $\mu_r$ (often > 1000), boosting the force to tens of newtons.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Piezoelectric Stack Displacement

**Problem statement:** A PZT piezo stack has $n = 100$ ceramic layers, each of thickness $t = 0.1$ mm and piezo coefficient $d_{33} = 4 \times 10^{-10}$ m/V. A voltage $V = 100$ V is applied. Calculate the total free displacement.

### Given Data
- $n = 100$, $t = 0.1$ mm $= 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $d_{33} = 4 \times 10^{-10}$ m/V, $V = 100$ V

### Step-by-Step

Step 1: Displacement of a single layer:

$$\Delta L_{single} = d_{33} \cdot V = (4 \times 10^{-10}) \cdot 100 = 4 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = 40 \text{ nm}$$

Step 2: Total displacement of $n$ layers in series electrically (parallel mechanically):

$$\Delta L_{total} = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V = 100 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta L_{total} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 4\ \mu\text{m}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the principle of **multilayer piezo stacks** used in **AFM scanners** and **inertial nanopositioning stages** — stacking multiplies the displacement without increasing the drive voltage.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: DC Motor Speed-Torque Curve

**Problem statement:** A DC motor has armature resistance $R_a = 1\ \Omega$, voltage $V = 24$ V, and torque constant $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A. Find (a) the no-load speed (assuming no armature current at no load), and (b) the stall torque.

### Part (a) — No-load speed

Step 1: At no load, back-EMF $\approx$ supply voltage:

$$E_b = V = K_e \omega_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e}$$

Step 2: In SI units $K_e = K_t = 0.05$ V·s/rad:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{24}{0.05} = 480 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 3: Convert to RPM:

$$\omega_0 = 480 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 4587 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_0 \approx 480 \text{ rad/s} \approx 4587 \text{ rpm}}$$

### Part (b) — Stall Torque

Step 4: At stall, $\omega = 0$, so back-EMF = 0, and armature current is maximum:

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{24}{1} = 24 \text{ A}$$

Step 5: Stall torque:

$$\tau_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.05 \cdot 24$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{stall} = 1.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Actuator Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision matrix for KTU problems where you are asked to "select the best actuator for a given application":**

| Application | Required Force | Required Stroke | Required Speed | Required Precision | **Best Choice** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Earth-mover arm lift | Very high (kN) | Long (m) | Slow | Low | **Hydraulic** |
| Food packaging gripper | Low | Short | High | Medium | **Pneumatic** |
| Robotic arm joint | Medium | Medium | High | High | **DC servo motor + gear** |
| AFM scanner | Very low | Nano | Very high | Ultra-high | **Piezoelectric stack** |
| HDD head positioning | Very low | Micro | Very high | Ultra-high | **Voice-coil actuator** |
| Thermal switch (thermostat) | Low | mm | Slow | Low | **Bimetallic strip** |
| Cardiac stent | Medium | mm | Very slow | Medium | **Shape-memory alloy (Nitinol)** |
| Mobile phone speaker | Very low | µm | High (kHz) | Medium | **Voice-coil / piezo** |
| Haptic feedback in a watch | Low | sub-mm | High | Medium | **Piezo / Eccentric rotating mass** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Actuators

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ACTUATORS] --> B[Hydraulic]
    A --> C[Pneumatic]
    A --> D[Electromagnetic]
    A --> E[Thermal]
    A --> F[Mechanical]
    A --> G[Piezoelectric]
    A --> H[Smart Material]
    A --> I[MEMS Microactuator]

    B --> B1[Linear Cylinder]
    B --> B2[Rotary Motor]
    B --> B3[Servo Valve]

    C --> C1[Single-Acting Cylinder]
    C --> C2[Double-Acting Cylinder]
    C --> C3[Rotary Vane]

    D --> D1[Solenoid]
    D --> D2[DC Motor]
    D --> D3[Stepper Motor]
    D --> D4[Voice Coil]
    D --> D5[Relay]

    E --> E1[Bimetallic Strip]
    E --> E2[Shape Memory Alloy]
    E --> E3[Thermopneumatic]

    F --> F1[Rack and Pinion]
    F --> F2[Lead Screw]
    F --> F3[Cam Follower]
    F --> F4[Belt Drive]

    G --> G1[Stack Actuator]
    G --> G2[Tube Actuator]
    G --> G3[Shear Actuator]

    H --> H1[SMA Nitinol]
    H --> H2[Electroactive Polymer]
    H --> H3[Magnetostrictive Terfenol D]

    I --> I1[Electrostatic Comb Drive]
    I --> I2[Electrothermal Microactuator]
    I --> I3[Piezo MEMS]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Position Control with Actuator

```mermaid
graph LR
    CMD[Reference Input] --> SUM((Sum))
    FB[Sensor Feedback] --> SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[Controller PID]
    CTRL --> DRV[Driver Amplifier]
    DRV --> ACT[Actuator]
    ACT --> LOAD[Mechanical Load]
    LOAD --> POS[Position Output]
    POS --> FB2[Position Sensor]
    FB2 --> FB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this block diagram:** The user commands a position. The error (command − actual) is fed to a **PID controller**, which drives an **amplifier**, which powers the **actuator** (e.g., DC motor), which moves the **load**. A **position sensor** (e.g., encoder or LVDT) measures the actual position and feeds it back — completing the loop. This is **exactly the closed-loop architecture used in every CNC machine, robot arm, and autofocus camera**.

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Hydraulic Cylinder Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Electric Motor Starts] --> S2[Step 2 Hydraulic Pump Engaged]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Oil Pressurized to Pbar 70 bar]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Control Valve Directs Flow]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Pressure on Piston Face]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Net Force F Pushes Piston]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Linear Stroke Produced]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Exhaust Oil Returns to Reservoir]
    S8 --> S2
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Solenoid Actuator

```mermaid
graph TD
    COIL[Energized Copper Coil N turns] --> FIELD[Magnetic Field B Lines]
    FIELD --> CORE[Soft Iron Plunger]
    CORE --> FORCE[Axial Pull Force F Generated]
    FIELD --> YOKE[Iron Yoke Completes Magnetic Circuit]
    YOKE --> FIELD
    FORCE --> STROKE[Plunger Displaces by Stroke s]
    STROKE --> WORK[External Mechanical Work W is Done]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The **iron yoke** completes the magnetic circuit with very low reluctance, channeling nearly all the magnetic flux through the plunger — this is what amplifies the force by a factor of $\mu_r$ (relative permeability) compared to an air-core solenoid.

## 4.5 Piezoelectric Stack — Voltage to Displacement

```mermaid
graph LR
    V[Applied Voltage V] --> E[Electric Field E V per m]
    E --> S[Strain S in PZT]
    S --> DL[Single Layer Displacement]
    DL1[Layer 1] --> TS[Stack of n Layers]
    DL2[Layer 2] --> TS
    DL3[Layer 3] --> TS
    DLn[Layer n] --> TS
    TS --> TOT[Total Displacement n times d33 V]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions are concise, definition/derivation-based, and worth 3 marks. Answers should fit in **one page** of the answer booklet.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` Define an actuator. How is it different from a sensor?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **actuator** is a device that converts a **control signal** (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal) into a **physical output** such as motion, force, torque, heat, or light. It is the **output-side counterpart** of a sensor.

| Aspect | Sensor | Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Physical → Electrical | Electrical → Physical |
| **Function** | *Detects / measures* | *Acts / moves* |
| **Power** | Generally low power | Generally high power |
| **Example** | Thermistor, LVDT | Solenoid, DC motor |

> Example: a **thermistor** is a sensor (temperature → resistance), while a **bimetallic strip** is an actuator (heat → mechanical displacement).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` List any four classifications of actuators with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Hydraulic** — e.g., hydraulic cylinder in an earth-mover
2. **Pneumatic** — e.g., air cylinder in a packaging machine
3. **Electromagnetic** — e.g., solenoid valve in a dishwasher
4. **Piezoelectric** — e.g., AFM nanopositioning scanner

(Alternative: Thermal — bimetallic strip; Smart material — SMA stent; MEMS — comb-drive.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks** and have **internal choice** (you answer EITHER Option A OR Option B). Each part typically has two 7-mark sub-questions.

---

### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **classification of actuators** based on the energy domain. List the **advantages and limitations** of hydraulic and pneumatic actuators. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hydraulic cylinder has piston diameter $D = 80$ mm and a stroke length $L = 0.5$ m. The fluid supply pressure is $P = 100$ bar. If the flow rate is $Q = 20$ L/min, calculate:
- (i) The **force** developed by the piston.
- (ii) The **piston velocity**.
- (iii) The **time** taken for one complete stroke.
- (iv) The **hydraulic power** delivered. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Classification (3 marks)**

Actuators are classified as:
1. **Hydraulic** — incompressible fluid, high force
2. **Pneumatic** — compressible gas, clean, low force
3. **Electrical / Electromagnetic** — motor, solenoid, relay
4. **Thermal** — bimetallic, SMA
5. **Mechanical** — lead screw, cam
6. **Piezoelectric / Smart material** — piezo, EAP, SMA
7. **MEMS / Micro-actuator** — comb drive, thermal micro-actuator

[Refer to the **block diagram in Section 4.1** for visual representation — 1 mark]

**Step 2: Hydraulic vs Pneumatic (4 marks)**

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) |
| Pressure | 70–700 bar | 5–10 bar |
| Force density | Very high | Medium |
| Speed | Medium | High |
| Precision | High | Low (compressibility) |
| Cleanliness | Low (oil leak) | Very high |
| Cost | High (pump, seals) | Low (compressor) |
| Application | Earth-movers, presses | Food, pharma, packaging |

> **Limitations of hydraulic:** Oil leaks, environmental hazard, requires filtration, expensive.
> **Limitations of pneumatic:** Compressibility → poor positioning accuracy, noise, lower force.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $D = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m
- $L = 0.5$ m
- $P = 100$ bar $= 100 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 1 \times 10^7$ Pa
- $Q = 20$ L/min $= \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s $= 3.333 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

**Step 1: Piston area (1 mark)**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0064}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Force developed (2 marks)**

$$F = P \cdot A_p = (1 \times 10^7) \cdot (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) = 50{,}265.5 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3: Piston velocity (2 marks)**

$$v_p = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0663 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_p = 66.3 \text{ mm/s}}$$

**Step 4: Stroke time (1 mark)**

$$t = \frac{L}{v_p} = \frac{0.5}{0.0663} = 7.54 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 7.54 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 5: Hydraulic power (1 mark)**

$$P_{hyd} = F \cdot v_p = 50{,}265.5 \cdot 0.0663 = 3{,}332.6 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{hyd} \approx 3.33 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### **QUESTION B** `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, explain the **working of a solenoid actuator**. Derive the expression for the **magnetic field inside a long solenoid** and the **force on the plunger**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A solenoid has $N = 1500$ turns, length $l = 0.3$ m, cross-sectional area $A = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m², and carries a current $I = 3$ A. Compute:
- (i) The **magnetic field** $B$ inside the solenoid.
- (ii) The **inductance** $L$ of the solenoid.
- (iii) The **force on the plunger**.
- (iv) The **energy stored** in the magnetic field. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Construction & Working (3 marks)**

A solenoid consists of a helical coil of insulated copper wire wound on a non-magnetic former. A movable **soft-iron plunger** sits partially inside the coil. When DC current is passed, a magnetic field is established along the axis; the plunger is pulled into the coil with an axial force, producing linear motion. A **return spring** restores the plunger when the current is removed. Solenoids are used in **valves, relays, door locks, and starter motors**.

[Refer to the **schematic in Section 4.4** for diagram — 1 mark]

**Step 2: Magnetic field derivation (2 marks)**

Applying **Ampere's law** to a rectangular loop of length $l$ inside the long solenoid:

$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enclosed}$$

For a long solenoid, $B = 0$ outside and $B = $ const inside:

$$B \cdot l = \mu_0 \cdot (N \cdot I) \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = \mu_0 \cdot \frac{N}{l} \cdot I = \mu_0 n I$$

**Step 3: Force on plunger (2 marks)**

The energy stored in the magnetic field is $U = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot A \cdot x$. The force is $F = \dfrac{dU}{dx}$. For constant current:

$$F = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $N = 1500$, $l = 0.3$ m, $A = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $I = 3$ A, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

**Step 1: Magnetic field (2 marks)**

$$n = \frac{N}{l} = \frac{1500}{0.3} = 5000 \text{ turns/m}$$

$$B = \mu_0 n I = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 5000 \cdot 3$$

$$\boxed{B = 1.885 \times 10^{-2} \text{ T} \approx 18.85 \text{ mT}}$$

**Step 2: Inductance (2 marks)**

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (1500)^2 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4})}{0.3}$$

$$L = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 2.25 \times 10^6 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-4}}{0.3}$$

$$L = \frac{5.6549 \times 10^{-4}}{0.3}$$

$$\boxed{L = 1.885 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H} = 1.885 \text{ mH}}$$

**Step 3: Force on plunger (2 marks)**

$$F = \frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0} = \frac{(1.885 \times 10^{-2})^2 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7})}$$

Numerator: $(3.553 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4}) = 7.106 \times 10^{-8}$
Denominator: $2.513 \times 10^{-6}$

$$F = \frac{7.106 \times 10^{-8}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 0.0283 \text{ N} = 28.3 \text{ mN}}$$

**Step 4: Energy stored (1 mark)**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (1.885 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (3)^2$$

$$\boxed{E = 8.48 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 8.48 \text{ mJ}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit-conversion mistakes:** $1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa. Forgetting this gives a force **wrong by a factor of 100,000**. Always convert bar → Pa first.
> 2. **Flow-rate unit confusion:** L/min must be converted to **m³/s** before substituting into $Q = A_p v_p$.
> 3. **Confusing $K_t$ and $K_e$:** In SI units $K_t = K_e$ — students often use different values and get inconsistent answers.
> 4. **Direction of force in solenoid:** It is always **pulling** the plunger inward, regardless of current direction (force $\propto I^2$).
> 5. **Piezo direction:** A piezo stack's displacement is in the direction of the applied field (along the poling axis) — not transverse.
> 6. **Diagrams:** In 7-mark questions, a **neat labelled diagram is mandatory** (1–2 marks reserved). Skipping it loses guaranteed marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these key points before your KTU exam:**

- ☐ **Actuator** = device that converts a control signal → physical action. The *opposite* of a sensor.
- ☐ **Five major classes:** Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electromagnetic, Thermal, Mechanical. (Plus modern: Piezo, SMA, MEMS.)
- ☐ **Hydraulic force equation:** $F = P \cdot A_p = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ — high force, slow, oily.
- ☐ **Pneumatic force equation:** $F = (P_1 - P_2) A_p$ — clean, low force, compressible.
- ☐ **Solenoid field:** $B = \mu_0 n I$ — long-solenoid approximation.
- ☐ **Solenoid force:** $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$ — proportional to $I^2$, always attractive.
- ☐ **Solenoid inductance:** $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}$ — increases with $N^2$.
- ☐ **DC motor torque:** $\tau = K_t I_a$. **Back-EMF:** $E_b = K_e \omega$.
- ☐ **Piezoelectric strain:** $S = dE$. **Displacement:** $\Delta L = dV$. **Stack multiplies displacement** by $n$ layers.
- ☐ **Bimetallic deflection:** $\delta \propto \Delta T \cdot L^2 / t$ — used in thermostats.
- ☐ **SMA recovery strain** can reach up to **8%** — used in stents and deployable structures.
- ☐ **Hydraulic power:** $P = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$ (all interconvertible).
- ☐ **Energy in inductor:** $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2$.
- ☐ **Performance metrics** to quote: Stroke, Force, Torque, Power, Bandwidth, Efficiency, Response time, Resolution.
- ☐ **Selection rule of thumb:** *Hydraulic for huge force, Pneumatic for clean & fast, Electromagnetic for general purpose, Piezo for nano-precision, SMA for bio-medical deployment.*
- ☐ **Always convert units to SI** (Pa, m, m³/s, A, T) before substituting.
- ☐ **Always draw a labelled diagram** in 7-mark questions (1–2 marks reserved for it).
- ☐ **Examiner's verdict on most-missed concept:** Students forget that the **solenoid force is proportional to $I^2$**, not $I$ — and therefore is **always attractive**, never repulsive.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Design of IoT - Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Design of IoT - Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Physical Design of IoT?

The **Physical Design of IoT** refers to the actual hardware and connectivity architecture that makes an Internet of Things system function in the real world. While the *logical design* deals with the abstract data flow (cloud, services, applications), the **physical design** addresses *what devices exist, what they sense, how they communicate, and which protocols they speak*. In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is treated as the foundation for understanding how sensors, actuators, and gateways are wired (logically) together using standardized communication protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:**
> The Physical Design of IoT is the ensemble of *Things/Devices*, *Resources*, *Controllers*, *Sensors*, and *Actuators* interconnected through wired or wireless **communication protocols** to enable data exchange, actuation, and identification across the network.

## 1.2 The Four Building Blocks of IoT Physical Design

According to the KTU syllabus, the physical design consists of four primary blocks:

| # | Block | KTU Definition | Real-World Example |
|---|-------|----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Things/Sensors** | Devices that sense the environment and collect data | DHT22 temperature sensor, MQ-2 gas sensor |
| 2 | **Actuators** | Devices that perform an action based on data | Servo motor, relay, solenoid valve |
| 3 | **Communication Protocols** | Rules governing data exchange between devices | Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, MQTT |
| 4 | **IoT Gateways/Controllers** | Bridging devices that aggregate data and forward to the cloud | Raspberry Pi, ESP32, Arduino + Wi-Fi shield |

## 1.3 What is a Communication Protocol?

A **communication protocol** is a pre-agreed set of rules that allow two or more entities (sensor → gateway → cloud) to transmit and interpret data correctly. Without a protocol, the receiver would not know *how* the bits are grouped, *when* a message starts/ends, or *how* errors are handled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Two-Way Radio Conversation:**
> Imagine two walkie-talkies. Before speaking, you must agree on: (1) the **channel frequency** (like Wi-Fi at 2.4 GHz), (2) the **language** (like English vs Morse code — this is the *encoding* rule), and (3) a **"over" signal** to indicate you are done speaking (this is the *frame delimiter*). All three together form a *protocol*. IoT protocols work the same way — they define the frequency, the packet structure, and the "handshake" rules.

## 1.4 Why are IoT Protocols Different from Traditional Internet Protocols?

Traditional internet protocols (HTTP, TCP/IP) were designed for *high-bandwidth, mains-powered* devices like PCs and servers. IoT devices, however, are typically:
- **Battery-powered** → low energy
- **Low-cost** → limited processing power (often 8-bit or 32-bit microcontrollers)
- **Transmitting tiny packets** (often <100 bytes)
- **Operating in lossy, unstable networks** (RF interference, long range)

Hence, the **physical design of IoT demands specialized protocols** optimized for low power, low data rate, and scalability.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** Whenever you see the phrase *"Physical Design of IoT"*, immediately visualize the **four-layer stack**:
> 1. **Things** (sensors + actuators)
> 2. **Local Connectivity** (Bluetooth/ZigBee)
> 3. **Gateway/Network** (Wi-Fi/Ethernet)
> 4. **Cloud Protocol** (MQTT/CoAP/HTTP)
>
> Protocols are the *glue* that joins these four layers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of IoT Protocols

The KTU 2024 scheme groups IoT protocols into **two super-families** based on where they operate in the network stack.

### A. IoT Communication Protocols (Network/Transport Layer)
These define *how* data packets travel from the device to the destination (gateway or cloud). They are further split into:

#### (i) Link-Layer / Short-Range Wireless Protocols
Used for **device-to-device** communication within a Personal Area Network (PAN) or Local Area Network (LAN). Typical range: **< 100 m**.

| Protocol | KTU-Standard Frequency | Data Rate | Range | Power Profile |
|----------|------------------------|-----------|-------|----------------|
| **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** | **2.4 GHz / 5 GHz** | 11–600 Mbps | ~50 m | High |
| **Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1)** | **2.4 GHz** | 1–3 Mbps | ~10 m | Medium |
| **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** | 2.4 GHz | 1–2 Mbps | ~50 m | **Very Low** |
| **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** | **2.4 GHz** | 250 kbps | ~100 m | Low |
| **Z-Wave** | 908.42 MHz (sub-GHz) | 100 kbps | ~30 m | Low |
| **NFC (ISO/IEC 14443)** | **13.56 MHz** | 106–424 kbps | <10 cm | Very Low |
| **RFID** | LF/HF/UHF | varies | <10 m | Passive/Active |
| **IR (Infrared)** | Light band | few kbps | line-of-sight | Very Low |

#### (ii) Long-Range / Wide-Area Network (LPWAN) Protocols
Used for **device-to-cloud** communication across kilometers. Typical range: **2–15 km** in rural areas.

| Protocol | Frequency | Data Rate | Range | Modulation |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-------|------------|
| **LoRaWAN** | **868 MHz (EU) / 915 MHz (US) / 433 MHz (Asia)** | 0.3–50 kbps | 2–15 km | CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum) |
| **Sigfox** | Sub-GHz | 100–600 bps | 10–50 km | UNB (Ultra Narrow Band) |
| **NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1)** | Licensed LTE bands | 26 kbps | <15 km | QPSK |
| **LTE-M (LTE Cat-M1)** | Licensed LTE | 300 kbps–1 Mbps | <10 km | QPSK/16-QAM |

#### (iii) Wired / Serial Protocols
Used for **device-to-controller** communication over physical cables.

| Protocol | Use Case | KTU Highlights |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| **UART** | Point-to-point serial | Asynchronous, 2 wires (TX, RX) |
| **SPI** | High-speed sensor interfacing | 4 wires, full-duplex, master-slave |
| **I²C (I2C)** | Multi-sensor low-speed bus | 2 wires (SDA, SCL), 7-bit/10-bit addressing |
| **CAN Bus** | Automotive IoT | Robust differential signalling, message-based |
| **Modbus (RTU/TCP)** | Industrial SCADA | Master-slave, RS-485 physical layer |
| **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)** | Wired LAN | 10/100/1000 Mbps over twisted pair |

---

### B. IoT Data Protocols (Application Layer)
These define *how* the data is structured and exchanged between client and server (or device and cloud). They run on top of TCP/UDP.

| Protocol | Transport | Header Size | Message Pattern | Best Suited For |
|----------|-----------|-------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **HTTP/HTTPS** | TCP | Large (~1 KB) | Request–Response | Web dashboards, REST APIs |
| **MQTT** | TCP | Tiny (2 bytes) | Publish–Subscribe | Battery sensors, telemetry |
| **CoAP** | UDP | 4 bytes | Request–Response | Constrained devices |
| **AMQP** | TCP | 8 bytes | Pub/Sub + Queues | Enterprise messaging |
| **DDS** | UDP/TCP | Variable | Real-time Pub/Sub | Industrial robotics |
| **XMPP** | TCP | Large | Request–Response + Presence | Chatty IoT apps |

---

## 2.2 Detailed Protocol Profiles (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.2.1 MQTT — Message Queuing Telemetry Transport
- **Architecture:** Publish–Subscribe (Pub/Sub) broker model.
- **Components:** *Publisher* (sensor) → **Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto) → *Subscriber* (mobile app/dashboard).
- **Why preferred in IoT:** Lightweight, designed for unreliable networks, supports **QoS levels 0, 1, 2**.
- **Topics:** Hierarchical, e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`.
- **Default Port:** **1883** (plain) / **8883** (TLS).

### 2.2.2 CoAP — Constrained Application Protocol
- **Architecture:** Request–Response (similar to HTTP) but over **UDP** to save energy.
- **Why preferred in IoT:** Tiny header (4 bytes vs HTTP's 1 KB), supports **DTLS** for security.
- **Method codes:** GET, POST, PUT, DELETE (mirror of HTTP verbs).
- **Default Port:** **5683**.

### 2.2.3 HTTP / REST
- Heavyweight, but ubiquitous. Used in IoT only when bandwidth and power are not a concern.
- Default port: **80** (plain) / **443** (HTTPS).

### 2.2.4 ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)
- **Network Topologies:** Star, Mesh, Tree.
- **Max Nodes per Coordinator:** ~65,000.
- **Operates on 16 channels in 2.4 GHz ISM band.**
- **Used in:** Philips Hue lights, smart meters, industrial automation.

### 2.2.5 LoRaWAN
- **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) modulation** — resistant to multipath fading.
- **Spreading Factors (SF7–SF12):** Higher SF = longer range, lower data rate, higher battery life.
- **Device Classes:** A (lowest power, downlink only after uplink), B (scheduled downlink), C (always listening).

### 2.2.6 BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)
- **Stack:** Generic Attribute Profile (GATT) with *Services* and *Characteristics*.
- **Advertising channels:** 37, 38, 39 (2.4 GHz band).
- **Connection interval:** 7.5 ms to 4 s.
- **Used in:** Fitness bands, beacons (iBeacon), health sensors.

---

## 2.3 Protocol Selection Trade-offs (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Design Goal | Recommended Protocol | Why |
|--------------|----------------------|-----|
| Battery-powered sensor to phone | **BLE** | Sub-50 mA peak current |
| Smart home mesh (lights, locks) | **ZigBee** | Mesh networking, low duty cycle |
| City-wide air quality monitoring | **LoRaWAN** | >10 km range, multi-year battery |
| Vehicle telemetry to cloud | **MQTT over LTE-M** | Reliable TCP + cellular coverage |
| Industrial PLC communication | **Modbus/TCP** | Proven, deterministic |
| Payment / card tap | **NFC** | <10 cm secure range |
| Peer-to-peer microcontrollers | **SPI / I²C** | High speed, short distance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cheat-Sheet — Three Magic Numbers to Memorize:**
> - **MQTT default port = 1883**
> - **CoAP default port = 5683**
> - **HTTPS default port = 443**
> These are *guaranteed* to appear as 1-mark Part-A questions.

---

## 2.4 Mathematical Foundations (PHY Layer Metrics)

Although IoT protocols are mostly engineering implementations, three core physical-layer equations govern *how* the radio behaves — and KTU loves to test them.

### 2.4.1 Shannon–Hartley Theorem (Channel Capacity)

$$C = B \cdot \log_2 \left( 1 + \frac{S}{N} \right)$$

Where:
- $C$ = channel capacity in **bits per second (bps)**
- $B$ = bandwidth in **Hz**
- $S$ = signal power in **Watts**
- $N$ = noise power in **Watts**
- $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (**SNR**, dimensionless)

**Engineering Insight:** Doubling the bandwidth doubles the data rate. To double the data rate at constant bandwidth, you must square the SNR (because the log is base 2). This is why LoRaWAN sacrifices data rate (lower $C$) for higher range — it spreads the signal over a narrower effective bandwidth with high spreading factor.

### 2.4.2 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

$$FSPL \, (\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$

Where:
- $d$ = distance in **km**
- $f$ = frequency in **MHz**
- The constant **32.44** assumes $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz.

**Engineering Insight:** Higher frequency signals (e.g., 5 GHz Wi-Fi) lose energy faster than sub-GHz signals (e.g., 868 MHz LoRa). This is why **LPWANs use sub-GHz bands** to reach long distances.

### 2.4.3 Link Budget Equation

$$P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} + G_{RX} - L_{system}$$

Where:
- $P_{TX}$ = transmit power (dBm)
- $G_{TX}, G_{RX}$ = antenna gains (dBi)
- $L_{path}$ = path loss (dB)
- $L_{system}$ = cable + connector + fade margin (dB)
- $P_{RX}$ = received power (dBm) — must exceed receiver sensitivity.

### 2.4.4 Time on Air (for LPWAN like LoRa)

$$T_{packet} = T_{preamble} + T_{payload} + T_{header}$$

For LoRa, payload duration depends on **Spreading Factor (SF)** — every increase in SF by 1 *doubles* the time on air, quadrupling energy consumption.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Consolidated)

| # | Formula | Where Used | Unit |
|---|---------|-----------|------|
| 1 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Channel capacity | bps |
| 2 | $FSPL = 20\log(d) + 20\log(f) + 32.44$ | Range estimation | dB |
| 3 | $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} + G_{RX} - L_{sys}$ | Link budget | dBm |
| 4 | $T_{air} = T_{preamble} + T_{payload} + T_{header}$ | Energy estimation | seconds |
| 5 | $N_{max} \approx 2^{16}$ for ZigBee | Network sizing | nodes |
| 6 | $E_{total} = V \cdot I \cdot T_{air}$ | Battery life | Joules |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering application:** A smart agriculture startup choosing between **ZigBee** and **LoRa** for soil-moisture sensors uses Equation 2.4.4 to estimate that a ZigBee node transmitting every 5 minutes will last ~6 months on 2× AA batteries, whereas a LoRa node with SF=10 will last >3 years — validating the LoRa choice for large farms.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **derivation of link-budget feasibility** for an IoT sensor deployment — a classic 14-mark KTU question style. We will explicitly write every algebraic step.

---

## 3.1 Problem Setup (Model KTU 14-Mark Question)

> A farmer deploys a LoRaWAN soil-moisture sensor at a distance of **5 km** from the gateway. The sensor transmits at **14 dBm** with an antenna gain of **2 dBi**. The gateway antenna has a gain of **6 dBi**. Cable and connector losses total **2 dB**, and we allow a fade margin of **10 dB**. The operating frequency is **868 MHz**. The gateway sensitivity is **−137 dBm**. Determine whether the link is feasible. If not, suggest two remedies.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

### Step 1 — Identify all given quantities and convert units

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 5 \, \text{km} \\
f &= 868 \, \text{MHz} \\
P_{TX} &= 14 \, \text{dBm} \\
G_{TX} &= 2 \, \text{dBi} \\
G_{RX} &= 6 \, \text{dBi} \\
L_{system} &= L_{cable} + L_{fade} = 2 + 10 = 12 \, \text{dB} \\
P_{RX,\text{min}} &= -137 \, \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** We must aggregate all fixed losses (cable + fade) into a single system loss term to plug into the link-budget equation. dBi is a dimensionless antenna gain expressed in dB; dBm is power referenced to 1 mW.

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

$$
\begin{aligned}
FSPL \, (\text{dB}) &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(5) + 20 \log_{10}(868) + 32.44
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute each term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 \log_{10}(5) &= 20 \times 0.69897 = 13.979 \, \text{dB} \\
20 \log_{10}(868) &= 20 \times (\log_{10}(8.68 \times 10^{2})) \\
&= 20 \times (2.9385) = 58.770 \, \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum the three terms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FSPL &= 13.979 + 58.770 + 32.44 \\
&= 105.189 \, \text{dB} \approx 105.2 \, \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Higher frequency and greater distance both increase path loss. At 868 MHz, every doubling of distance adds 6 dB loss (since $20\log(2) = 6$).

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Received Power $P_{RX}$ using Link-Budget Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{RX} &= P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{path} - L_{system} \\
P_{RX} &= 14 + 2 + 6 - 105.2 - 12 \\
P_{RX} &= 22 - 117.2 \\
P_{RX} &= -95.2 \, \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Transmit power and antenna gains add (positive), while path loss and system losses subtract (negative). All quantities are in dB-scale, so simple algebraic addition/subtraction suffices.

---

### Step 4 — Compare $P_{RX}$ with Receiver Sensitivity

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{RX,\text{received}} &= -95.2 \, \text{dBm} \\
P_{RX,\text{sensitivity}} &= -137 \, \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **link margin** is computed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Link Margin} &= P_{RX} - P_{RX,\text{sensitivity}} \\
&= -95.2 - (-137) \\
&= +41.8 \, \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A *positive* link margin means the received signal is **stronger** than the minimum required for decoding. The link is **feasible** with a healthy 41.8 dB margin.

---

### Step 5 — Decision & Remedies (for the second part of the question)

✅ **The link is feasible** (link margin = +41.8 dB).

However, if the margin had been negative, the two standard remedies would be:

1. **Increase the Spreading Factor (SF)** of LoRa — e.g., from SF7 to SF10. This increases the receiver sensitivity (by ~3 dB per SF step) at the cost of longer time-on-air and lower data rate.
2. **Deploy a higher-gain antenna or a repeater/gateway in the middle** to halve the effective path distance — since path loss scales as $20\log(d)$, halving the distance recovers 6 dB of margin.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing Among Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa

The following matrix is the analytical heart of the "Physical Design of IoT" module and is a frequent 7-mark KTU sub-question.

| Evaluation Criterion | Wi-Fi (802.11) | BLE | ZigBee (802.15.4) | LoRaWAN |
|------------------------|----------------|------|-------------------|---------|
| **Frequency Band** | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | Sub-GHz (868/915) |
| **Range** | ~50 m | ~50 m | ~100 m | 2–15 km |
| **Peak Data Rate** | 600 Mbps | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 50 kbps |
| **TX Current (typical)** | 200–400 mA | 7–15 mA | 30 mA | 40–100 mA (burst) |
| **Battery Life (2× AA)** | Hours | Months | Months | **Years** |
| **Network Topology** | Star | Star / Mesh (BLE 5) | Star / Mesh / Tree | Star-of-stars |
| **Max Nodes** | ~32 per AP | ~7 active | ~65,000 | ~1 million / network |
| **IP Stack Support** | Full TCP/IP | Partial (IPv6 over BLE) | Partial (6LoWPAN) | LoRaWAN MAC + IPv6 (via SCHC) |
| **Best Use Case** | High-throughput indoor | Wearables, beacons | Smart home mesh | Long-range outdoor telemetry |
| **Cost per Module** | Medium–High | Low | Low | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut for 7-Mark Comparison Questions:**
> Always frame your answer along **six axes** — *Range, Data Rate, Power, Topology, Nodes, Use Case*. This guarantees full marks because it matches the KTU marking rubric.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — MQTT vs CoAP Selection

Suppose a battery-powered air-quality sensor sends 64-byte readings every 60 seconds over a cellular NB-IoT link.

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|-----------|------|------|
| Transport | TCP (3-way handshake every session) | UDP (stateless) |
| Header overhead | ~2 bytes + TCP ~20 bytes | 4 bytes + UDP ~8 bytes |
| Handshake cost per connection | High (~3 round-trips) | None |
| Energy per packet @ 64 B | High | **Low** |
| Reliability | Built-in QoS 0/1/2 | Requires CoAP confirmable mode |
| Suitability for 1-min telemetry | Medium | **High** |

**Conclusion:** **CoAP over UDP** is the optimal choice for this scenario because:
- It avoids TCP's 3-way handshake overhead during every wake-up.
- The 4-byte CoAP header fits the 64-byte payload efficiently.
- The sensor is "constrained" (low power, low MCU) — exactly CoAP's design target.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the IoT Physical Design with protocols overlaid on the data path. All Mermaid diagrams use strictly alphanumeric node identifiers and clean uppercase labels (no markdown formatting inside quotes).

---

## 4.1 Master Physical Design Architecture with Protocols

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["SENSOR LAYER"] --> nodeB["MICROCONTROLLER MCU"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["WIRED BUS I2C SPI UART"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["WIRELESS RADIO MODULE"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["SHORT RANGE PROTOCOL BLE ZIGBEE WIFI"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["LONG RANGE PROTOCOL LORA SIGFOX NB IOT"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["EDGE GATEWAY"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["APPLICATION LAYER PROTOCOL MQTT COAP HTTP"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["CLOUD BROKER OR SERVER"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["DATA ANALYTICS AND DASHBOARD"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["ACTUATOR COMMAND PATH"]
    nodeK --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeB
```

**Reading Guide for Students:**
- The **sensor** (e.g., DHT22) sends raw data to the **MCU** over a *wired bus* (I²C, SPI, or UART).
- The MCU hands off the data to a *radio module* (e.g., ESP32, SX1276).
- The radio transmits using either a **short-range protocol** (BLE/ZigBee/Wi-Fi) or a **long-range protocol** (LoRa/Sigfox/NB-IoT).
- The **gateway** receives the radio packets and re-encodes them into an *application-layer protocol* (MQTT/CoAP/HTTP) for the cloud.
- The cloud broker fans out data to **dashboards** and triggers **actuator commands** that travel back to the edge.

---

## 4.2 Protocol Stack Comparison — TCP/IP vs IoT Constrained Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["APPLICATION LAYER HTTP REST MQTT COAP"] --> s2["TRANSPORT LAYER TCP UDP"]
    s2 --> s3["NETWORK LAYER IPv4 IPv6 6LOWPAN"]
    s3 --> s4["PHYSICAL LAYER WIFI BLE ZIGBEE LORA"]
```

**Engineering Note:** On a fully-constrained IoT device (e.g., a Class-A LoRa node with 32 KB RAM), the full TCP/IP stack cannot fit. Hence, **6LoWPAN** compresses IPv6 headers, and **CoAP/UDP** replaces TCP+HTTP, allowing a 32-bit MCU to participate directly in the internet.

---

## 4.3 MQTT Pub/Sub Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pub1["SENSOR A PUBLISHER"] --> broker["MQTT BROKER MOSQUITTO"]
    pub2["SENSOR B PUBLISHER"] --> broker
    pub3["SENSOR C PUBLISHER"] --> broker
    broker --> sub1["MOBILE APP SUBSCRIBER"]
    broker --> sub2["CLOUD DASHBOARD SUBSCRIBER"]
    broker --> sub3["ACTUATOR NODE SUBSCRIBER"]
```

**Key Insight:** Decoupling is the magic of MQTT. Sensors never need to know *who* will consume their data; the broker handles fan-out. This is why MQTT scales beautifully for thousands of IoT nodes.

---

## 4.4 LoRaWAN Network Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    end1["END DEVICE CLASS A SOIL SENSOR"] --> gw1["GATEWAY 1"]
    end2["END DEVICE CLASS A WEATHER SENSOR"] --> gw1
    end3["END DEVICE CLASS C SMART METER"] --> gw1
    end1 --> gw2["GATEWAY 2"]
    end2 --> gw2
    gw1 --> ns["NETWORK SERVER"]
    gw2 --> ns
    ns --> app["APPLICATION SERVER"]
    app --> cloud["CLOUD DASHBOARD"]
```

**Key Insight:** LoRaWAN is a **star-of-stars** topology. End devices never speak to each other; they only uplink to *any* gateway in range. Gateways forward the packet to a *Network Server* which deduplicates and routes to the correct Application Server.

---

## 4.5 Selection Decision Tree for KTU Viva

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["CHOOSE IOT PROTOCOL"] --> q1{"RANGE NEEDED OVER 1 KM"}
    q1 -->|YES| q2{"BATTERY LIFE OVER 5 YEARS"}
    q1 -->|NO| q3{"INSIDE A SMART HOME"}
    q2 -->|YES| lora["USE LORAWAN"]
    q2 -->|NO| nbiot["USE NB IOT OR LTE M"]
    q3 -->|YES| zig["USE ZIGBEE MESH"]
    q3 -->|NO| q4{"STREAMING VIDEO OR AUDIO"}
    q4 -->|YES| wifi["USE WIFI"]
    q4 -->|NO| ble["USE BLE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this diagram in the exam:** If a question asks *"Which protocol would you choose for a smart agriculture deployment across 200 hectares?"* — walk through the tree, justifying each branch. This structured reasoning is exactly what examiners reward.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

Below are model questions crafted to the **exact** pattern of the KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination. Each question carries the expected marks and a fully-worked model answer.

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. Define the Physical Design of IoT. List its four main building blocks.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Physical Design of IoT refers to the actual hardware entities and the communication protocols that interconnect them to sense, process, and act upon real-world phenomena. It represents the tangible, deployable layer of the IoT architecture.

**Four Main Building Blocks:**
1. **Things/Devices** — physical sensors (e.g., temperature, humidity, gas sensors).
2. **Actuators** — devices that perform an action (e.g., motors, relays, valves).
3. **Communication Protocols** — rules for data exchange (Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, MQTT, CoAP).
4. **IoT Gateways/Controllers** — devices that aggregate sensor data and route it to the cloud (e.g., Raspberry Pi, ESP32).

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing, 1 mark for relevant example.)**

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between MQTT and CoAP. State the default port numbers.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | MQTT | CoAP |
|---------|------|------|
| Transport Layer | **TCP** | **UDP** |
| Message Pattern | Publish–Subscribe | Request–Response |
| Header Size | **2 bytes** (fixed) | **4 bytes** (fixed) |
| Default Port | **1883** | **5683** |
| Best Suited For | High-latency unreliable cellular | Constrained battery devices |
| Security | TLS (port 8883) | DTLS |

**Default Ports (1 mark each for correctness):** MQTT = **1883**, CoAP = **5683**.

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]`

#### (a) Explain the architecture of MQTT. List its three QoS levels with their guarantees. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**MQTT Architecture (4 Marks):**

MQTT follows the **Publish–Subscribe** pattern and is built around three logical entities:

1. **Publisher (Client)** — The IoT sensor (e.g., a temperature node) that *publishes* data. Each publication is sent under a hierarchical **topic** (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`).
2. **Broker** — A central server (commonly **Mosquitto**, HiveMQ, or EMQX) that receives all messages, filters them by topic, and distributes them to interested subscribers. The broker runs on default **port 1883**.
3. **Subscriber (Client)** — The consuming application (mobile app, dashboard, or actuator) that *subscribes* to one or more topics and receives matching messages automatically.

The publisher and subscriber are **decoupled** — they never know each other's IP address, which is why MQTT scales effortlessly to millions of devices.

**Three QoS Levels (3 Marks):**

| QoS Level | Name | Guarantee | Use Case |
|-----------|------|-----------|----------|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget; no ACK | Non-critical telemetry |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Possible duplicates; ACK required | Standard sensor data |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | Four-step handshake guarantees one delivery | Billing, alarms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why QoS 2 is rare in IoT:** The four-step handshake (PUBLIB → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP) doubles the number of packet exchanges, consuming precious battery on a constrained node.

---

#### (b) With a neat diagram, explain the LoRaWAN network topology. Mention all three device classes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**LoRaWAN Topology (4 Marks):**

LoRaWAN uses a **Star-of-Stars** topology. The hierarchy is:

1. **End Devices (EDs)** — Battery-powered sensors that transmit using **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** modulation on sub-GHz frequencies (e.g., 868 MHz in EU, 915 MHz in US).
2. **Gateways (GWs)** — Receive uplinks from any ED in range and forward via IP backhaul (Ethernet, 4G, or satellite) to the Network Server. A single ED can be heard by multiple gateways.
3. **Network Server (NS)** — De-duplicates packets received by multiple gateways, performs security checks, and routes the message to the correct Application Server.
4. **Application Server (AS)** — Owns the data, presents it to the end user, and issues downlinks if needed.

**Three Device Classes (3 Marks):**

| Class | Behavior | Power Profile | Use Case |
|-------|----------|---------------|----------|
| **Class A** | Uplink anytime; **two downlinks only immediately after uplink** | **Lowest** | Battery sensors |
| **Class B** | Uplink anytime + **scheduled downlink windows** | Medium | Actuated valves, smart meters |
| **Class C** | **Continuously listening** for downlink except during TX | Highest | Mains-powered devices |

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.4 — refer to it during the exam).**

---

### ⭐ **Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

#### (a) Compare Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, and LoRaWAN across six evaluation parameters. Justify which is best for a *smart agriculture* deployment. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | Wi-Fi | BLE | ZigBee | LoRaWAN |
|-----------|-------|-----|--------|---------|
| **Range** | ~50 m | ~50 m | ~100 m | **2–15 km** |
| **Data Rate** | 600 Mbps | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 50 kbps |
| **Power** | High | Low | Low | **Very Low** |
| **Topology** | Star | Star | Mesh | Star-of-stars |
| **Nodes** | ~32 | ~7 | ~65 K | ~1 M |
| **Frequency** | 2.4/5 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | Sub-GHz |

**Justification for Smart Agriculture (3 Marks):**

For a 200-hectare farm, the deployment area exceeds the range of Wi-Fi, BLE, and ZigBee by orders of magnitude. **LoRaWAN is the optimal choice** because:

1. **Range:** A single gateway at the farmhouse can cover the entire farm, whereas hundreds of ZigBee routers would be needed to relay a mesh network — economically and energetically infeasible.
2. **Battery Life:** LoRa end-devices last 5–10 years on a single battery, essential for sensors buried in soil.
3. **Data Rate Sufficiency:** Soil moisture and weather telemetry packets are tiny (<20 bytes); the 50 kbps LoRa capacity is more than adequate.
4. **Cost per Node:** A LoRa node (e.g., STM32 + SX1276) costs ~$8, much cheaper than a Wi-Fi node with its power-hungry radio.

---

#### (b) Derive the link-budget feasibility for a Wi-Fi node transmitting at 20 dBm over 100 m at 2.4 GHz, given $G_{TX}$ = 2 dBi, $G_{RX}$ = 2 dBi, system loss = 5 dB, and receiver sensitivity = −90 dBm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{TX} &= 20 \, \text{dBm} \\
G_{TX} &= 2 \, \text{dBi} \\
G_{RX} &= 2 \, \text{dBi} \\
L_{system} &= 5 \, \text{dB} \\
d &= 0.1 \, \text{km} \quad (\text{since } 100 \, \text{m} = 0.1 \, \text{km}) \\
f &= 2400 \, \text{MHz} \\
P_{RX,\text{min}} &= -90 \, \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute Free-Space Path Loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FSPL &= 20 \log_{10}(0.1) + 20 \log_{10}(2400) + 32.44 \\
&= 20 \times (-1) + 20 \times (3.3802) + 32.44 \\
&= -20 + 67.604 + 32.44 \\
&= 80.044 \, \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Received Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{RX} &= P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - FSPL - L_{system} \\
&= 20 + 2 + 2 - 80.044 - 5 \\
&= 24 - 85.044 \\
&= -61.044 \, \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Link Margin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Link Margin} &= P_{RX} - P_{RX,\text{sensitivity}} \\
&= -61.044 - (-90) \\
&= +28.956 \, \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The link is **feasible** with a comfortable margin of ~29 dB, meaning the Wi-Fi signal is 28.956 dB *stronger* than the minimum required for decoding. This validates a robust indoor Wi-Fi link over 100 m.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks Per Question:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in FSPL formula** — KTU examiners deduct marks if you plug $d$ in meters instead of **km**, or $f$ in GHz instead of **MHz**. Memorize: *d in km, f in MHz, constant = 32.44*.
> 2. **Confusing CoAP port (5683) with HTTP (80)** — A single-digit slip and you lose the mark.
> 3. **Forgetting antenna gains in the link budget** — Many students write $P_{RX} = P_{TX} - FSPL$ only. Always add $G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{system}$.
> 4. **Writing "ZigBee is for long range"** — ZigBee is **short-range mesh (<100 m)**. LoRaWAN is the long-range one.
> 5. **Drawing the Pub/Sub diagram backwards** — Sensors are *publishers*, not subscribers. Reversed arrows = 0 marks on the diagram.
> 6. **Claiming MQTT uses UDP** — It uses **TCP**. Only CoAP and DDS use UDP.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:**

- **Physical Design of IoT = Sensors + Actuators + Protocols + Gateways.**
- **Protocols are split into Communication (link/transport) and Data (application).**
- **MQTT = TCP, Pub/Sub, Broker, port 1883, 2-byte header, QoS 0/1/2.**
- **CoAP = UDP, Request-Response, port 5683, 4-byte header.**
- **HTTP/HTTPS = port 80/443, heavy, not for constrained nodes.**
- **Wi-Fi = high speed, short range, high power, 2.4/5 GHz.**
- **BLE = short range, very low power, used in wearables, advertising channels 37/38/39.**
- **ZigBee = IEEE 802.15.4, 2.4 GHz, mesh, ~65 K nodes, smart home.**
- **LoRaWAN = Sub-GHz (868/915 MHz), 2–15 km, CSS modulation, 3 device classes (A, B, C).**
- **NB-IoT = Licensed cellular, ~26 kbps, deep indoor coverage.**
- **NFC = 13.56 MHz, <10 cm, contactless payments.**
- **RFID = identification, passive/active, supply chain.**
- **I²C = 2 wires, multi-master, 7-bit addressing.**
- **SPI = 4 wires, full-duplex, faster than I²C.**
- **FSPL formula uses d in km, f in MHz, constant 32.44.**
- **Link budget = $P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{path} - L_{system}$; must exceed sensitivity.**
- **Positive Link Margin = feasible; negative = infeasible.**
- **Shannon Capacity: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — bandwidth and SNR both raise capacity logarithmically.**
- **LoRa Spreading Factor: SF7 → SF12 increases range but doubles time-on-air per step.**
- **MQTT broker decouples publishers from subscribers — the secret of its scalability.**
- **CoAP confirmable mode (CON) provides reliability over UDP using ACK + token matching.**
- **ZigBee supports Star, Mesh, and Tree topologies — Mesh is the most common.**
- **Long range + low power = LPWAN family (LoRa, Sigfox, NB-IoT).**
- **Short range + low power = PAN family (BLE, ZigBee, NFC).**
- **Long range + high speed = Cellular LTE-M, 5G.**
- **Short range + high speed = Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11).**

✅ You are now fully equipped to answer any 3-mark, 7-mark, or 14-mark KTU question on the **Physical Design of IoT — Protocols**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Communication Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Communication Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
