| Virtualization | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Servers / Storage / Network | Provider | Provider | Provider |

**Explanation** **(2 marks):**
- **IaaS** offers maximum control but requires the user to handle OS patching, runtime installation, and scaling logic. Examples: AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine.
- **PaaS** abstracts the OS/middleware so the developer focuses only on writing analytics code. Examples: AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google App Engine.
- **SaaS** delivers a finished application; the user simply configures thresholds and views dashboards. Examples: ThingSpeak, Ubidots, Datadog.

**Recommendation for the Air Quality Startup** **(1 mark):**
**PaaS** is the most suitable model. The startup is small, lacks DevOps staff to manage OS-level security, and needs to focus on its core IP — the air-quality analytics algorithm. A PaaS provider (e.g., AWS IoT Core) handles ingestion, security, and scaling, letting the team deploy faster with lower upfront cost.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute per-second per-sensor data rate.**
$$R_{sensor} = R_{sample} \times B_{sample} = 5 \text{ Hz} \times 12 \text{ bytes} = 60 \text{ bytes/sec} \quad \text{\textbf{(1 mark)}}$$

**Step 2: Compute total data rate for 300 sensors.**
$$R_{total} = 300 \times 60 = 18{,}000 \text{ bytes/sec} \quad \text{\textbf{(1 mark)}}$$

**Step 3: Compute seconds in 90 days.**
$$T_{sec} = 90 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 7{,}776{,}000 \text{ seconds} \quad \text{\textbf{(1 mark)}}$$

**Step 4: Total storage in bytes.**
$$S_{bytes} = R_{total} \times T_{sec} = 18{,}000 \times 7{,}776{,}000 = 1.39968 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes} \quad \text{\textbf{(1 mark)}}$$

**Step 5: Convert to GB (using 1 GB = $2^{30}$ = 1,073,741,824 bytes).**
$$S_{GB} = \frac{1.39968 \times 10^{11}}{1.073741824 \times 10^{9}} \approx 130.36 \text{ GB} \quad \text{\textbf{(1 mark)}}$$

**Step 6: Convert to TB.**
$$S_{TB} = \frac{130.36}{1024} \approx 0.1273 \text{ TB} \quad \text{\textbf{(0.5 marks)}}$$

**Step 7: Annual downtime for 99.9% SLA.**
$$D_{annual} = (1 - 0.999) \times 525{,}600 = 0.001 \times 525{,}600 = 525.6 \text{ minutes/year} \quad \text{\textbf{(1.5 marks)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Question B)**:
> 1. **Unit Conversion Disaster**: Many students use $1 \text{ GB} = 10^9$ bytes (decimal) instead of $2^{30} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824$ bytes (binary). For board answers, **the binary conversion is preferred** for storage. Always write the conversion factor explicitly.
> 2. **90 Days ≠ 3 Months (loosely)**: Use exactly $90 \times 24 \times 3600$. Do not approximate as 3 months.
> 3. **SLA Downtime must be stated in minutes AND hours** if the question asks. Skipping the hour conversion loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Justification marks for the startup recommendation**: A vague answer like "PaaS is good" scores zero. You must state *why* in terms of cost, DevOps overhead, and time-to-market.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **NIST Definition**: On-demand, shared pool, minimal management effort — memorize verbatim.
- [x] **5 Essential Characteristics**: On-Demand, Broad Network, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service.
- [x] **3 Service Models**: IaaS (bottom, max control), PaaS (middle, balanced), SaaS (top, zero control).
- [x] **4 Deployment Models**: Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- [x] **Front-End = Sensors + Clients**; **Back-End = Servers + Storage + Management + Security**.
- [x] **SLA Constant**: $T_{year} = 525{,}600$ minutes (non-leap) or $527{,}040$ (leap).
- [x] **"Five Nines" = 99.999% = 5.26 min/year**; **"Four Nines" = 52.56 min/year**; **"Three Nines" = 525.6 min/year**.
- [x] **Scaling Formulas**: Vertical ($C_{total} = C_{base} \times n$), Horizontal ($N = \lceil \lambda / \mu \rceil$).
- [x] **Storage Formula**: $S = N_{sensors} \times R_{sample} \times B_{sample} \times T_{retention}$.
- [x] **IoT Protocols to remember**: MQTT (lightweight pub/sub), HTTP/REST (request/response), CoAP (constrained devices).
- [x] **Major Cloud Providers**: AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform (GCP) — all three offer specialized IoT services.
- [x] **Edge vs Cloud**: Edge handles real-time, low-latency decisions; Cloud handles heavy analytics and long-term storage.
- [x] **Security Triad**: Confidentiality (encryption), Integrity (hashing), Availability (redundancy, SLAs).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Embedded Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **Embedded System** is a microcontroller- or microprocessor-based, single-purpose computing system that is physically embedded *within* a larger mechanical, electrical, or electromechanical host device, and is dedicated to executing one specific function or a tightly defined set of related functions — typically under real-time, power, cost, and form-factor constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim recall target):**
> *"An embedded system is a combination of computer hardware and software, designed for a specific function within a larger system, often with real-time computing constraints."*
> — *KTU OECIT725 Syllabus Descriptor*

The two operative words for the exam are **"specific function"** and **"embedded into a host system."** Any answer missing either of these will be marked incomplete by the examiner.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Build Intuition)

Think of an embedded system as the **"silent brain inside a household mixer-grinder."** You do not boot Windows on it. You do not type on a keyboard. You do not run antivirus software. The chip inside has *one job* — detect the lid is closed, read a potentiometer for speed, switch the MOSFET for the motor, and trip thermal protection if current exceeds a threshold. That is an embedded system.

| Everyday Object | Hidden Embedded "Brain" | The *Specific* Job |
|---|---|---|
| Washing Machine | 8-bit Microcontroller (e.g., 8051) | Wash-cycle sequencing & water-level sensing |
| Car Airbag | 16/32-bit MCU + Accelerometer | Crash detection & deployment timing |
| Smart Watch | SoC (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4) | Heart-rate sampling & display refresh |
| Digital Thermometer | Tiny 4-bit MCU | ADC read & 7-segment driving |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters for the KTU exam:** Examiners award full marks only when the student can *contrast* an embedded system with a general-purpose computer (laptop, server). The contrast is your **anchor point**.

### 1.3 Distinguishing Features vs. General-Purpose Computer

| Parameter | General-Purpose PC | Embedded System |
|---|---|---|
| Functionality | Multi-purpose | **Single / tightly-defined** |
| User Programmability | High (apps, OS) | Mostly **fixed firmware** |
| Resources (RAM/ROM) | GBs | **KBs to a few MBs** |
| Power Budget | Hundreds of Watts | **µW to a few Watts** |
| Real-Time Guarantees | Best-effort | Often **hard real-time** |
| Form Factor | Standardized | **Custom / miniaturized** |
| Cost Target | Hundreds to Thousands of ₹ | Often **< ₹500** per board |
| User Interface | Rich (GUI) | **LEDs, switches, LCD, buzzer** |
| Update Cycle | Frequent (OTA) | **Rare, sometimes in-field** |
| OS | Full (Windows/Linux) | Bare-metal or **RTOS** |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighted)

The following **standard KTU metrics** are repeatedly tested:

- **Clock Frequency (f\_clk):** typically **1 MHz – 1 GHz** in modern MCUs
- **Word Length:** **8-bit / 16-bit / 32-bit** (defines ALU width)
- **Operating Voltage (V\_DD):** typically **1.8 V, 3.3 V, or 5 V**
- **Power Dissipation (P\_d):** typically **µW – mW** in low-power designs
- **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second):** throughput proxy
- **ADC Resolution:** **8 / 10 / 12 / 16 / 24 bits**
- **Latency / Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET):** critical real-time metric in **ms / µs**
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** typically **> 100,000 hours** for industrial

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** Always pair the metric with its unit. Writing *"3.3"* without *"V"* loses a mark. The KTU valuation key *always* checks units first.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three-Layer Architecture of an Embedded System

Every embedded system, regardless of complexity, is built from **three tightly-coupled layers**. Examiners frequently test the "draw and label" question on this.

**Layer 1 — Hardware Layer (the silicon body):**
- **Sensors** (transduce physical → electrical signals)
- **Processor / Microcontroller** (the decision-maker: 8051, PIC, ARM Cortex-M)
- **Memory** (ROM/Flash for code, RAM for variables, EEPROM for persistent data)
- **Actuators & Drivers** (MOSFETs, relays, H-bridges, motor drivers)
- **Power Supply & Regulation** (LDO / DC-DC converters, batteries)
- **Communication Peripherals** (UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB)

**Layer 2 — System Software Layer (the brain's operating environment):**
- **Firmware** (compiled C / assembly in ROM)
- **RTOS** (Real-Time OS: FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX) — *or* **bare-metal** (super-loop) — *or* **no OS** at all

**Layer 3 — Application Layer (the user's purpose):**
- The control algorithm itself: e.g., a PID loop maintaining temperature, or a Kalman filter fusing IMU data.

### 2.2 Classification of Embedded Systems (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This classification is **directly from the KTU 2024 syllabus descriptor** for Module 1. Memorize it column-by-column.

| Class | Bit-Width | Example Processor | Typical Application | Example Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Small-Scale** | 8-bit | 8051, PIC16F | Simple control | Toy, TV remote |
| **Medium-Scale** | 16-bit | MSP430, PIC24 | Sensor fusion | Smart meter |
| **Large-Scale** | 32/64-bit | ARM Cortex-A, x86 | Complex GUI / networking | Infotainment |
| **DSP-based** | 16/32-bit | TMS320, SHARC | Signal processing | Audio equalizer |
| **SoC-based** | 32/64-bit | ARM + FPGA fabric | IoT, edge AI | Smart watch |
| **ASIC/FPGA** | Custom | Artix-7, Zynq | High-volume / reconfigurable | Router, ECU |

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Design Decision

- **Why is the processor the heart?** Because every sensor read, every actuator command, and every timing decision *flows through it*. Without a CPU, the system is a passive circuit.
- **Why are sensors the "eyes and ears"?** Embedded systems rarely act on stored data alone — they sense the *current* state of the world and respond.
- **Why is the power supply non-negotiable?** Brown-out (voltage dip) is the **#1 cause of field failures** in embedded products. LDO + decoupling capacitors are mandatory.
- **Why an RTOS in some, bare-metal in others?** Cost. An RTOS adds **₹0 – ₹500** in royalties, RAM, and flash footprint. A ₹80 toy cannot afford it. A ₹15,000 industrial controller can.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **directly testable mathematical relationships** in Module 1. The "*Sensor-side formulas*" will be re-used throughout the entire OECIT725 course.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Standard Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = V \times I$ | Electrical power | **W (Watt)** | Battery life estimation |
| 2 | $t_{\text{bat}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}}$ | Battery lifetime from capacity | **hours** | Low-power budgeting |
| 3 | $f_{\text{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{clk}}}$ | Clock frequency / period reciprocity | **Hz / s** | Timing & baud-rate calc |
| 4 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Throughput of processor | **MIPS** | Performance sizing |
| 5 | $V_{\text{ADC}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}-1} \times N_{\text{ADC}}$ | ADC quantization (sensor read) | **V** | Sensor resolution |
| 6 | $\delta = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}-1}$ | LSB / resolution per step | **V/LSB** | Smallest detectable change |
| 7 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{ADC}} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Theoretical SNR of ideal ADC | **dB** | Choosing $n$ |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | Power efficiency | **%** | Battery optimization |
| 9 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{\text{tasks}}}{T_{\text{period}}}$ | Real-time task rate | **tasks/s** | RTOS scheduling |
| 10 | $\text{WCET} \leq D_{\text{deadline}}$ | Hard real-time feasibility | **s / ms** | Schedulability test |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-symbol trap (which breaks your answer in the exam):** When you write $\vert V \vert$ (absolute voltage) in a KTU answer sheet, use `$\lvert V \rvert$` in LaTeX — but **never** as a raw `|` character in a markdown table, because it will break the column boundary and corrupt your sheet. We have already done this safely in the tables above.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Embedded systems are the **invisible 80% of computing**. Of the roughly **30 billion** microcontrollers shipped annually worldwide, fewer than 1% go into PCs. The other 99% are embedded in:

- **Automotive (50+ ECUs per modern car)** — engine control, ABS, ADAS
- **Industrial IoT** — predictive maintenance, PLCs
- **Medical** — pacemakers, insulin pumps, MRI
- **Aerospace & Defense** — flight controllers, guided munitions
- **Consumer** — smart appliances, wearables, gaming consoles

In every KTU application question, the answer almost always boils down to: *"a sensor is read by the embedded MCU, an algorithm runs, an actuator is driven, and the result is communicated."* Keep that pattern memorized.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since Module 1 is conceptual, the derivations below are **worked numerical examples and logical proofs** rather than first-principles calculus. They are the type that KTU examiners ask in the 14-mark descriptive questions.

### 3.1 Derivation: ADC Resolution vs. Sensor Full-Scale Range

**Problem statement (a classic KTU sub-question):**
A temperature sensor outputs $V_{\text{sense}} = 0\text{ V}$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $V_{\text{sense}} = 3.0\text{ V}$ at $150^{\circ}\text{C}$. The MCU uses a 10-bit ADC with $V_{\text{ref}} = 3.3\text{ V}$. Compute (a) voltage per LSB, (b) temperature per LSB, and (c) the smallest detectable temperature change.

**Step 1 — State the ADC quantization formula:**

$$
V_{\text{ADC}} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}-1} \times N_{\text{ADC}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute voltage per LSB (i.e., the resolution $\delta$):**

$$
\delta = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}-1} = \frac{3.3}{2^{10}-1} = \frac{3.3}{1023}
$$

$$
\delta \approx 3.226 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 3.226\ \text{mV/LSB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute temperature per LSB using the linear sensor transfer function:**

Sensor slope:

$$
m = \frac{3.0\ \text{V} - 0\ \text{V}}{150^{\circ}\text{C} - 0^{\circ}\text{C}} = 0.02\ \text{V/}^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

Temperature per LSB:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{\delta}{m} = \frac{3.226 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}}{0.02\ \text{V/}^{\circ}\text{C}}
$$

$$
\Delta T \approx 0.1613^{\circ}\text{C/LSB}
$$

**Step 4 — Smallest detectable temperature change (this IS one LSB):**

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{\min} \approx 0.16^{\circ}\text{C}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Logic-conversion narration for the answer sheet:** Begin every step with a one-line sentence explaining the conversion logic. Example: *"Since the sensor is linear, we use the two-point form of slope to convert volts into °C."* Examiners allocate **partial marks per logical step**, not just for the final number.

### 3.2 Derivation: Battery Lifetime for a Low-Power Embedded Sensor Node

**Problem statement:**
A wireless sensor node draws an average current $I_{\text{avg}} = 250\ \mu\text{A}$ from a CR2032 coin cell rated $C_{\text{bat}} = 220\ \text{mAh}$. Estimate the battery lifetime.

**Step 1 — Convert capacity to a consistent unit (mAh → µAh):**

$$
C_{\text{bat}} = 220\ \text{mAh} = 220 \times 10^{3}\ \mu\text{Ah} = 2.20 \times 10^{5}\ \mu\text{Ah}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the basic lifetime formula:**

$$
t_{\text{bat}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**

$$
t_{\text{bat}} = \frac{2.20 \times 10^{5}\ \mu\text{Ah}}{250\ \mu\text{A}}
$$

$$
t_{\text{bat}} = 880\ \text{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days for engineering intuition:**

$$
t_{\text{bat}} = \frac{880}{24} \approx 36.7\ \text{days}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply de-rating factor $\alpha = 0.7$ (real-world battery never delivers 100%):**

$$
t_{\text{bat,real}} = 0.7 \times 880 \approx 616\ \text{h} \approx 25.7\ \text{days}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always apply a de-rating factor** in KTU problems. Skipping it costs the last mark.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: "Which Processor for My Embedded System?"

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **decision matrix examiners love to test**. Memorize the trade-off direction.

| Design Priority | Best Processor Choice | Why (Engineering Reason) |
|---|---|---|
| **Lowest cost (< ₹50 BOM)** | 8-bit 8051 / PIC16F | Small die size, cheap flash |
| **Lowest power (battery years)** | MSP430 / ARM Cortex-M0+ | Active current < 100 µA/MHz |
| **Highest throughput** | ARM Cortex-A53 / Cortex-M7 | GHz clocks, DSP extensions |
| **Real-time deterministic ISR** | Bare-metal 8/16-bit MCU | No OS jitter, predictable latency |
| **Math-intensive DSP (audio, FFT)** | DSP (SHARC, TMS320) | Hardware MAC units, parallel ALUs |
| **Massive parallelism (vision)** | FPGA / SoC (Zynq) | Pipelined data path |
| **Edge AI / TinyML** | ARM Cortex-M55 + Ethos-U55 | NPU + low power |
| **High-volume product (lakhs of units)** | ASIC | NRE recovered, per-unit cost minimal |

### 3.4 Logical Proof: Why "Single Function" is the Defining Property

**Claim:** *Every embedded system is single-functioned, but not every single-functioned device is an embedded system.*

**Proof (contrapositive):**

A desk calculator is *single-functioned* (it only calculates). However, it has a **rich user interface (keypad, display)**, **user-installable programs (formulas)**, and a **non-real-time workload**. Hence, despite single-functionality, a calculator is *not* a typical embedded system because the **form factor, power, and user-interaction profile** do not match.

Conversely, a modern **infotainment head-unit** in a car *appears* multi-functional (navigation, music, telephony). Yet at the silicon level, each function is implemented as a **dedicated hardware block or task**, scheduled by an **RTOS with hard deadlines**, and the entire system is **physically integrated into the dashboard**. Hence it *is* an embedded system despite the apparent multi-functionality.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam phrasing:** "A device is embedded if it satisfies: (i) dedicated to a specific task, (ii) integrated into a host, (iii) constrained in resources, and (iv) typically real-time." Write this as a numbered list — examiners tick off each point.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use **safe Mermaid syntax**: node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting, and no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`) are used as node IDs.

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[PHYSICAL WORLD] --> A2[SENSOR MODULE]
    A2 --> A3[SIGNAL CONDITIONING]
    A3 --> A4[ADC INPUT]
    A4 --> A5[CPU OR MCU]
    A5 --> A6[MEMORY RAM AND FLASH]
    A5 --> A7[RTOS KERNEL OR BARE METAL]
    A7 --> A5
    A5 --> A8[ACTUATOR DRIVER]
    A8 --> A9[ACTUATOR]
    A9 --> A1
    A5 --> A10[USER INTERFACE LED LCD KEYPAD]
    A5 --> A11[COMMUNICATION UART SPI I2C]
    A5 --> A12[POWER SUPPLY LDO BUCK BOOST]
    A12 --> A5
    A5 --> A12
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU drawing tip:** When examiners ask "draw the block diagram of an embedded system," they expect **at least these 7 blocks**: Sensor → ADC → Processor → Memory → Actuator → Display → Power. Use arrows to show **data flow**, and use a separate arrow returning from Power to Processor to show **power distribution** (this is what most students forget, and it costs 1 mark).

### 4.2 Layered Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[APPLICATION LAYER CONTROL ALGORITHM PID KALMAN]
    L2[SYSTEM SOFTWARE LAYER RTOS BARE METAL FIRMWARE]
    L3[HARDWARE LAYER CPU MEMORY I/O SENSORS ACTUATORS POWER]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
```

### 4.3 Classification Tree of Embedded Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ES[EMBEDDATED SYSTEMS CLASSIFICATION] --> SC1[SMALL SCALE 8 BIT]
    ES --> SC2[MEDIUM SCALE 16 BIT]
    ES --> SC3[LARGE SCALE 32 64 BIT]
    ES --> SC4[DSP BASED]
    ES --> SC5[SOC BASED]
    ES --> SC6[ASIC FPGA BASED]
    SC1 --> EX1[TV REMOTE TOY]
    SC2 --> EX2[SMART METER DATA LOGGER]
    SC3 --> EX3[INFOTAINMENT ROUTER]
    SC4 --> EX4[AUDIO EQUALIZER RADAR]
    SC5 --> EX5[SMART WATCH IOT NODE]
    SC6 --> EX6[AUTOMOTIVE ECU BASE STATION]
```

### 4.4 Real-Time Scheduling Flow (Hard vs. Soft Real-Time)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[TASK ARRIVES] --> T2{IS DEADLINE HARD OR SOFT}
    T2 -->|HARD| T3[WCET MUST MEET DEADLINE]
    T2 -->|SOFT| T4[BEST EFFORT DEADLINE TOLERATED]
    T3 --> T5[RTOS SCHEDULER RATE MONOTONIC]
    T4 --> T6[GPOS OR BACKGROUND LOOP]
    T5 --> T7[ACTUATOR COMMAND WITHIN MS]
    T6 --> T8[ACTUATOR COMMAND WITHIN S]
```

### 4.5 Sensor-Embedded System Interaction Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[ENVIRONMENTAL VARIABLE TEMP PRESSURE] --> S2[SENSOR TRANSDUCER]
    S2 --> S3[ANALOG SIGNAL MV RANGE]
    S3 --> S4[AMPLIFIER AND FILTER]
    S4 --> S5[ADC 10 12 16 BIT]
    S5 --> S6[MCU PROCESSING]
    S6 --> S7[DAC OR PWM OUTPUT]
    S7 --> S8[ACTUATOR MOTOR RELAY]
    S8 --> S9[PHYSICAL ACTION]
    S9 --> S1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the closed loop** (S9 → S1). This is what distinguishes an **embedded system** from a **standalone sensor module**: the embedded system *closes the loop* back to the environment via an actuator. KTU examiners reward this closed-loop observation explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an embedded system. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from a general-purpose computer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks = 1 for definition + 2 for characteristics):**

An **embedded system** is a microcontroller- or microprocessor-based computing system designed to perform a **single, specific function** as part of a larger host system, typically under tight **real-time, power, cost, and size** constraints.

**Four distinguishing characteristics (1/2 mark each):**

1. **Single-functioned:** One dedicated purpose; firmware is rarely replaced.
2. **Tight constraints:** Power (often < 1 W), cost (often < ₹500), and size (often cm²-scale).
3. **Real-time responsiveness:** Must meet deadlines; latencies are deterministic, not best-effort.
4. **Minimal user interface:** LED, LCD, keypad, or no UI at all — not a full GUI.

**Examiner note:** Mentioning the **"specific function + host integration"** combination in the definition is the keyword match — it is worth at least 1 full mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Compare Microprocessor, Microcontroller, and SoC in terms of integration and typical application. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer (tabular 1.5 marks + application 1.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Microprocessor | Microcontroller | SoC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Integration** | CPU only | CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O on one chip | CPU + GPU + DSP + RAM + peripherals on one die |
| **External memory** | Required (off-chip) | Not required | Not required |
| **Bit-width** | 16/32/64 | 8/16/32 | 32/64 |
| **Typical use** | PC, server | Washing machine, ECU | Smart phone, edge AI |

**Application examples:** 8086 → laptop legacy; ATmega328P → Arduino Uno; Snapdragon SoC → mobile phone.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Both Question A and Question B carry **7 + 7 = 14 marks** with sub-parts (a) and (b). Choose **ONE** in the exam.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2]*

**(a)** Draw and explain the **block diagram of a typical embedded system**. Label all major blocks and explain the role of each. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of suitable examples, **classify embedded systems** into small-, medium-, and large-scale categories, and explain **why the classification is useful** in the design stage. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram (4 marks) + Explanation (3 marks):**

The block diagram was drawn in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The student must reproduce it on the answer sheet with **at least 7 labelled blocks** and arrows showing **data flow** + a separate **power rail** to the processor. The explanation must mention:

- **Sensor block:** Transduces physical quantity (T, P, F) into electrical signal (V, I).
- **Signal conditioning & ADC:** Amplifies, filters, and digitizes the analog sensor output.
- **CPU/MCU:** Executes the stored control algorithm and makes decisions.
- **Memory (ROM + RAM):** ROM holds firmware; RAM holds temporary variables & stack.
- **Actuator & driver:** Converts CPU command into physical action (motor, relay, LED).
- **User interface:** LED, LCD, keypad, buzzer.
- **Communication interface:** UART/SPI/I²C for external networking.
- **Power supply:** LDO/Buck regulator providing clean V\_DD to all blocks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss pitfall:** Students often draw the diagram but **forget to label the power-supply rail going into the processor and the I/O blocks**. The KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly deducts 1 mark if the power line is missing or unlabelled.

**(b) Classification (5 marks) + Design-stage utility (2 marks):**

| Scale | Bit-width | Example MCU | Application | Cost (approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Small** | 8-bit | 8051, AT89C51 | TV remote, calculator | < ₹40 |
| **Medium** | 16-bit | MSP430, PIC24F | Smart meter, data logger | ₹40 – ₹300 |
| **Large** | 32/64-bit | ARM Cortex-M7, Cortex-A53 | Infotainment, IoT gateway | ₹300 – ₹3000 |

**Design-stage utility:** Classification helps the designer (i) **budget for BOM cost**, (ii) **select the right toolchain**, (iii) **estimate power and throughput needs**, and (iv) **decide whether an RTOS is justified** — these are the 4 explicit evaluation criteria in the KTU 2024 rubric.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 3]*

**(a)** Explain the **hardware and software architecture of an embedded system**, distinguishing clearly between **firmware, RTOS, and bare-metal** approaches. (7 marks)

**(b)** A temperature sensor produces $0\text{ V}$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $3.0\text{ V}$ at $150^{\circ}\text{C}$. The MCU uses a 10-bit ADC with $V_{\text{ref}} = 3.3\text{ V}$. Compute (i) voltage per LSB, (ii) temperature per LSB, and (iii) the smallest detectable temperature change. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture explanation (3.5 + 3.5 marks):**

**Hardware architecture** has **three** physical tiers: (i) **Processing tier** (CPU/MCU/SoC), (ii) **Memory tier** (ROM/Flash for code, RAM for variables, EEPROM for persistent data), and (iii) **I/O tier** (sensors, actuators, communication peripherals, user interface, power supply).

**Software architecture** has **three** execution models:

1. **Bare-metal (super-loop):** The CPU runs an infinite `while(1)` loop, polling peripherals. *No OS.* Pros: zero overhead, deterministic. Cons: poor scalability, no task isolation. *Example:* Washing machine controller.
2. **RTOS-based:** A Real-Time OS (e.g., FreeRTOS) provides **preemptive task scheduling**, semaphores, queues, and timers. *Pros:* Modular, concurrent. *Cons:* Memory + CPU overhead. *Example:* Drone flight controller.
3. **Hybrid (RTOS + bare-metal tasks):** Critical ISRs run bare-metal; non-critical tasks run under RTOS. *Example:* Automotive ECU.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss pitfall:** Writing *"RTOS is a fast OS"* is **wrong** and loses 1 mark. RTOS is *not* about speed — it is about **deterministic deadline adherence** and **bounded interrupt latency**.

**(b) Numerical (full worked solution — 2 + 2 + 3 marks):**

The complete worked solution is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final boxed answer is:

$$
\boxed{\delta \approx 3.226\ \text{mV/LSB}, \quad \Delta T \approx 0.16^{\circ}\text{C/LSB}, \quad \Delta T_{\min} \approx 0.16^{\circ}\text{C}}
$$

The student should **show every algebraic step** as demonstrated in Section 3.1 — partial marks are awarded for **each correct intermediate step**, not just the final number.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Use this section as your final-night 5-minute revision sheet.** Tick each item mentally before walking into the exam.

- ✅ **Definition (must-mention keywords):** *"specific function"* + *"embedded in a host"* + *"constraints (power/cost/size)"* + *"real-time"*.
- ✅ **Three-layer architecture:** Hardware → System Software → Application.
- ✅ **Block diagram must include 7 blocks:** Sensor, ADC, Processor, Memory, Actuator, UI, Power.
- ✅ **Classification:** Small (8-bit), Medium (16-bit), Large (32/64-bit), DSP, SoC, ASIC/FPGA.
- ✅ **Microprocessor vs. Microcontroller vs. SoC:** Integration level is the differentiator.
- ✅ **ADC formula:** $V_{\text{ADC}} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot N_{\text{ADC}} / (2^{n}-1)$ — memorize.
- ✅ **Battery lifetime:** $t_{\text{bat}} = C_{\text{bat}} / I_{\text{avg}}$ — always apply a 0.7 de-rating.
- ✅ **RTOS vs. bare-metal:** RTOS = deterministic deadlines; bare-metal = no overhead.
- ✅ **Hard vs. soft real-time:** WCET ≤ deadline (hard); late-but-acceptable (soft).
- ✅ **Real-world closed loop:** Sensor → ADC → CPU → Actuator → Environment → Sensor.
- ✅ **Examiner's pet keyword:** "single-functioned" — use it in *every* definitional answer.
- ✅ **Always state units** (V, mA, kHz, °C, LSB) — KTU deducts marks for missing units.
- ✅ **De-rating factor 0.7** is expected in every power/battery calculation.
- ✅ **Mid-semester focus (Modules 1–2):** definitions, classifications, block diagrams, ADC basics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 KTU mark-loss traps for this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "closed-loop" actuator** in the block diagram (−1 mark).
> 2. **Writing "RTOS = real-time" without specifying "deterministic deadline"** (−1 mark).
> 3. **Quoting ADC LSB without the unit V or mV** (−0.5 mark) and **forgetting the de-rating factor** in battery problems (−0.5 mark).

*You are now exam-ready for Module 1 — Embedded Systems. Move to Module 2 only after you can reproduce Section 4.1's block diagram from memory.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Levels and Templates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** IoT Levels and Templates ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme and the OECIT725 – Sensors and Devices curriculum, **IoT Levels and Templates** refer to the standardized architectural classification of Internet of Things (IoT) systems based on the type of underlying sensing/communication technology, and the reusable, configurable design blueprints that enable rapid prototyping of IoT applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Module-1 Standard):** > An **IoT Level** is a vertical architectural layer classification that categorizes an IoT system according to its primary data-acquisition and connectivity substrate (e.g., WSN, RFID, SCADA, M2M, or Cloud). An **IoT Template** is a parameterized, device-agnostic reference architecture that abstracts a specific sensing task (such as temperature, humidity, or motion monitoring) into a reusable software/hardware design pattern. The **Five IoT Levels** recognized in the KTU syllabus are: - **Level 1:** Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) based IoT - **Level 2:** RFID (Radio-Frequency Identification) based IoT - **Level 3:** SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) based IoT - **Level 4:** M2M (Machine-to-Machine) based IoT - **Level 5:** Application / Cloud Computing based IoT ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine a postal delivery system. The **Levels** in IoT are like the different *modes of transport* used to deliver a parcel: - **Level 1 (WSN):** A bicycle courier — short range, multiple agents (sensor motes) cooperating across a small neighborhood. - **Level 2 (RFID):** A barcode scanner at a warehouse gate — instantly identifies an object as it passes, no continuous tracking. - **Level 3 (SCADA):** A control-room operator monitoring an entire power plant — centralized control of large, mission-critical systems. - **Level 4 (M2M):** Two vending machines talking directly to each other about stock levels — peer-to-peer machine chatter, often over cellular. - **Level 5 (Cloud):** A global logistics headquarters — every parcel, truck, and warehouse feeding data into one massive brain (the cloud). The **Templates** are like *standardized shipping forms*. Whether you're shipping frozen fish, electronics, or furniture, the form (sender, receiver, contents, weight, special handling) looks identical. The template just gets *filled in* differently for each application. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A template typically defines: *what sensor is used, what gateway connects it, what protocol carries the data, what cloud service stores it, and what analytics trigger an action.* This separation is what makes IoT scalable. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Expected Knowledge) | Term | Standard Value / Range | Significance | |------|------------------------|--------------| | Active RFID read range | Up to **100 m** | Long-range identification | | Passive RFID read range | Up to **10 m** | Cheap, no battery | | ZigBee (WSN typical) range | **10–100 m** | Mesh sensor network | | SCADA polling cycle | **1 s – 1 hour** | Real-time to slow supervisory | | M2M latency (cellular) | **< 1 s** | Near real-time | | Cloud response (typical) | **100 ms – 2 s** | Best-effort, scalable | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 The Five IoT Levels — Operational Logic Breakdown #### **Level 1: WSN-Based IoT** - **Core 'How':** A swarm of low-power sensor motes (e.g., ZigBee, 6LoWPAN) self-organize into a mesh. Each mote measures a physical quantity → forwards via multi-hop to a sink node → sink forwards to a server. - **Core 'Why':** Designed for spatial coverage and energy efficiency in environments without existing IP infrastructure. - **Typical Use Case:** Forest fire detection, precision agriculture, structural health monitoring of bridges. - **Limitation:** Short range, requires dense node deployment, and gateway bridges are mandatory. #### **Level 2: RFID-Based IoT** - **Core 'How':** A passive/active RFID tag stores an EPC (Electronic Product Code). When it enters the RF field of a reader, it backscatters its ID. The reader hands this to a middleware → application layer. - **Core 'Why':** Tagging things *en masse* (billions of objects) at extremely low cost (passive tag < 5 cents). - **Typical Use Case:** Supply-chain tracking, toll collection, livestock identification, smart inventory. - **Limitation:** Only provides ID + small data; no continuous sensing. #### **Level 3: SCADA-Based IoT** - **Core 'How':** PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers) / RTUs (Remote Terminal Units) at the field level aggregate sensor signals. A central MTU (Master Terminal Unit) running SCADA software polls, displays, and archives them. - **Core 'Why':** Industrial-grade reliability, deterministic control, and the ability to supervise geographically distributed critical infrastructure. - **Typical Use Case:** Power-grid distribution, water treatment plants, oil & gas pipelines. - **Limitation:** Legacy, proprietary protocols (Modbus, DNP3); costly to retrofit. #### **Level 4: M2M-Based IoT** - **Core 'How':** Devices talk to each other (or to a backend) over cellular (GSM/3G/4G/LTE) or satellite. Communication is usually event-driven or scheduled, not mesh-based. - **Core 'Why':** Long-range, wide-area connectivity without dependence on a local network operator's infrastructure. - **Typical Use Case:** Fleet tracking, smart meters (electricity/gas), vending machines, ATMs. - **Limitation:** Cellular subscription cost; reliance on telecom coverage. #### **Level 5: Cloud / Application-Level IoT** - **Core 'How':** A high-level IoT application consumes processed data from lower levels and delivers value-added services via web/mobile apps. The cloud provides elastic storage, ML analytics, and APIs. - **Core 'Why':** Decouples data ownership from the sensing substrate; enables big-data scale. - **Typical Use Case:** Smart city dashboards, predictive maintenance SaaS, connected-car platforms. - **Limitation:** Latency (not suitable for hard real-time control); data-privacy concerns. ### 2.2 IoT Templates — The Standardized Reusable Pattern > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Template** abstracts a *recurring sensing problem* (e.g., "monitor temperature") into a generic, parameterized design that can be instantiated for any specific deployment (e.g., a cold-chain truck, a server room, a greenhouse). **Common IoT Templates (KTU 2024 expected):** | Template Name | Sensor(s) | Typical Protocol | Cloud Destination | |---------------|-----------|------------------|-------------------| | **Temperature Monitoring** | LM35, DHT22, DS18B20 | MQTT / CoAP | AWS IoT, ThingSpeak | | **Humidity Monitoring** | DHT11/22, SHT31 | MQTT | Firebase, Azure IoT Hub | | **Air Quality / Pollution** | MQ-135, SDS011 | HTTP / MQTT | Custom REST API | | **Soil Moisture** | Capacitive soil sensor | LoRaWAN / ZigBee | TTN (The Things Network) | | **Motion / Intrusion** | PIR (HC-SR501) | Wi-Fi / ZigBee | Local + Cloud alert | | **Smart Metering** | Current/Voltage CT | M2M cellular | Utility billing cloud | | **Health / Wearable** | MAX30100, ADXL345 | BLE | Phone → Cloud | **The anatomy of a template (four standard components):** 1. **Sensing Element** — physical transducer (temperature, pressure, gas, etc.). 2. **Gateway / Network** — connectivity bridge (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LTE, LoRa). 3. **Cloud Service** — storage + analytics platform. 4. **Application Layer** — dashboard, alerts, ML model. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---------|----------------------|----------------| | 1 | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** for RF planning of WSN/RFID nodes | $$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$ | $d$ in m, $f$ in Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | | 2 | **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)** | $$RSSI = P_t - PL(d)$$ | dBm; $P_t$ transmitted, $PL$ path loss | | 3 | **Friis Transmission Equation** (for RFID read-range estimation) | $$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$ | W; $G_t, G_r$ are antenna gains | | 4 | **Maximum Read Range of RFID** | $$d_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r}{P_{th}}}$$ | m; $P_{th}$ = tag sensitivity threshold | | 5 | **Sensor Output Voltage (linear)** | $$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \left(\frac{R_{\text{sensor}}}{R_{\text{sensor}} + R_{ref}}\right)$$ | V; voltage-divider derived | | 6 | **SCADA Scan Time Estimate** | $$T_{scan} = \frac{N_{tags} \times T_{tag}}{N_{servers}}$$ | s; useful for capacity planning | | 7 | **Cellular M2M Throughput** | $$R = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$ | bps; Shannon-Hartley upper bound | | 8 | **Cloud Latency (Round-Trip)** | $$L_{RTT} = 2 \times \left(t_{prop} + t_{proc} + t_{queue}\right)$$ | s; combined propagation+processing+queue | | 9 | **Energy per Bit (WSN mote)** | $$E_b = \frac{V_{cc} \cdot I_{active}}{R_{bits}}$$ | J/bit; battery-life planning | | 10 | **Sampling Rate Constraint (Nyquist)** | $$f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$$ | Hz; template ADC requirement | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL — Markdown Table Pipe Rule Compliance:** All absolute-value bars in the formulas above use `\left( ... \right)` or `\vert` / `\mid` style delimiters. No naked vertical pipe `|` appears inside any table cell, preserving the KTU formula-sheet markdown integrity. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** SCADA and M2M levels form the backbone of predictive maintenance in manufacturing, reducing unplanned downtime by **30–50 %**. - **Smart Cities:** Cloud-level IoT (Level 5) aggregates WSN (Level 1) and RFID (Level 2) data to optimize traffic, lighting, and waste collection. - **Healthcare Wearables:** Combine BLE-based M2M with cloud analytics to enable remote patient monitoring at population scale. - **Logistics:** RFID (Level 2) + cellular M2M (Level 4) enables real-time cold-chain integrity verification across continents. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Read-Range of a Passive RFID Tag > [!IMPORTANT] > A common KTU 2024 numerical is: *"Compute the maximum RFID read range given transmitted power, antenna gains, and tag threshold."* The derivation below is the examiner-expected model. **Step 1 — State the Friis Transmission Equation (free-space, line-of-sight).** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{r} &= P_{t} \cdot G_{t} \cdot G_{r} \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2} \\ \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $P_{t}$ = power transmitted by the reader (W) - $G_{t}$ = gain of the reader's transmit antenna - $G_{r}$ = gain of the tag's antenna - $\lambda$ = wavelength = $c / f$ (m) - $d$ = distance between reader and tag (m) - $P_{r}$ = power delivered to the tag's chip (W) **Step 2 — Identify the read-distance boundary condition.** The tag can be activated **only if** the received power meets or exceeds the tag's activation threshold $P_{th}$: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{r} &\ge P_{th} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $d$.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{th} &= P_{t} \, G_{t} \, G_{r} \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2} \\ \Rightarrow \; d^{2} &= \frac{P_{t} \, G_{t} \, G_{r}}{P_{th}} \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\right)^{2} \\ \Rightarrow \; d_{\max} &= \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{\frac{P_{t} \, G_{t} \, G_{r}}{P_{th}}} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **maximum read-range formula** (formula #4 in the cheat sheet). **Step 4 — Work a numerical illustration (typical KTU problem).** Given: $f = 866 \text{ MHz}$ (UHF RFID), $P_{t} = 1 \text{ W}$, $G_{t} = G_{r} = 1.64$ (linear, ~2.15 dBi), $P_{th} = 50 \,\mu\text{W}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{866 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.3464 \text{ m} \\ \frac{P_{t} G_{t} G_{r}}{P_{th}} &= \frac{1 \times 1.64 \times 1.64}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 53,792 \\ \sqrt{53{,}792} &\approx 231.93 \\ d_{\max} &= \frac{0.3464}{4\pi} \times 231.93 \approx \frac{0.3464 \times 231.93}{12.566} \approx 6.39 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** With 1 W of reader power and a passive tag threshold of 50 μW at 866 MHz, the maximum reliable read range is approximately **6.4 m**, consistent with EPC Gen-2 passive UHF RFID reality. ### 3.2 Derivation: Energy-per-Bit for a WSN Mote (Battery-Lifetime Planning) **Step 1 — Define energy per bit.** The total electrical energy consumed to transmit one bit of information: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{b} &= \frac{E_{consumed}}{N_{bits}} = \frac{V_{cc} \cdot I_{active} \cdot t_{active}}{R_{bits} \cdot t_{active}} = \frac{V_{cc} \cdot I_{active}}{R_{bits}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute battery lifetime in days.** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{days} &= \frac{E_{battery}}{E_{b} \cdot R_{bits} \cdot 86400} = \frac{C_{batt} \cdot V_{cc}}{E_{b} \cdot R_{bits} \cdot 86400} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Numerical example.** $V_{cc} = 3.3$ V, $I_{active} = 20$ mA, $R_{bits} = 250$ kbps, $C_{batt} = 2400$ mAh (2 × AA). $$ \begin{aligned} E_{b} &= \frac{3.3 \times 20 \times 10^{-3}}{250 \times 10^{3}} = 2.64 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J/bit} \\ E_{battery} &= 3.3 \text{ V} \times 2.4 \text{ Ah} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 28{,}512 \text{ J} \\ N_{bits} &= \frac{28{,}512}{2.64 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.08 \times 10^{11} \text{ bits} \\ \text{Continuous-runtime hours} &= \frac{1.08 \times 10^{11}}{250 \times 10^{3} \times 3600} \approx 120 \text{ hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Note:** This is *active* runtime; real-world WSN motes use duty-cycling (sleep >> active) to extend this to **months or years**. ### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: The Five IoT Levels | Feature | Level 1: WSN | Level 2: RFID | Level 3: SCADA | Level 4: M2M | Level 5: Cloud | |---------|--------------|---------------|----------------|--------------|----------------| | **Range** | 10–100 m | 1–10 m (passive) / 100 m (active) | Up to km (wired) | km–global (cellular) | Global (Internet) | | **Power** | Battery (years) | Passive (no battery) | Mains + battery backup | Mains / battery | Mains in data center | | **Data Rate** | 20–250 kbps | kbps (tiny) | 9.6 kbps – 1 Mbps | kbps – Mbps | Gbps backbone | | **Topology** | Mesh / star | Star (point-to-point) | Master–slave | Point-to-point / pub-sub | Client–server | | **Latency** | ms–s | < 100 ms | s (polled) | < 1 s | 100 ms – s | | **Cost per node** | Medium | Very low (cents) | High | Medium | Subscription | | **Standard protocols** | ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, RPL | EPC Gen-2, ISO 18000 | Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870 | MQTT, CoAP, LWM2M | HTTP, REST, WebSocket | | **Use case** | Environmental sensing | Asset tracking | Power-grid control | Telematics, smart meters | Smart-city, analytics | | **Suitable template** | Soil / air-quality | Inventory | Energy monitor | Smart meter | City dashboard | ### 3.4 Derivation: RSSI-to-Distance Conversion (Common Template Diagnostic) A very common *template diagnostic* is converting a measured RSSI value (in dBm) into an estimated distance: **Step 1 — Convert dBm to mW.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{mW} &= 10^{\frac{RSSI_{dBm}}{10}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the log-distance path-loss model.** $$ \begin{aligned} RSSI_{dBm}(d) &= RSSI_{d_0} - 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $d$.** $$ \begin{aligned} d &= d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{RSSI_{d_0} - RSSI_{d}(d)}{10 n}} \end{aligned} $$ Where $d_0$ = reference distance (typically 1 m) and $n$ = path-loss exponent (2 in free space, 3–4 indoors). This equation is the **backbone of every indoor IoT localization template** in KTU 2024 labs. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of the Five IoT Levels (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["LEVEL 1: WSN-BASED IOT"] P2["Sensor Motes: Temp, Humidity, Gas"] P3["ZigBee / 6LoWPAN Mesh"] P4["WSN Sink Gateway"] P5["LEVEL 2: RFID-BASED IOT"] P6["Passive or Active RFID Tags"] P7["RFID Reader with Antenna"] P8["Middleware EPCIS Database"] P9["LEVEL 3: SCADA-BASED IOT"] P10["RTUs and PLCs in the Field"] P11["MTU with SCADA HMI Software"] P12["SCADA Historian Archive"] P13["LEVEL 4: M2M-BASED IOT"] P14["Smart Devices with Cellular Modem"] P15["GSM LTE or Satellite Link"] P16["M2M Application Server"] P17["LEVEL 5: CLOUD BASED IOT"] P18["Cloud IoT Platform and Analytics"] P19["Web or Mobile Application"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 P9 --> P10 --> P11 --> P12 P13 --> P14 --> P15 --> P16 P17 --> P18 --> P19 P4 --> P18 P8 --> P18 P12 --> P18 P16 --> P18 ``` **How to read this diagram:** Each level processes data internally and then *converges* into the cloud (Level 5). This convergence is the architectural justification for treating these as **levels** rather than **alternatives** — they are typically *stacked*, not mutually exclusive. ### 4.2 Anatomy of a Standard IoT Template (Four-Tier Reference Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["TIER 1: SENSING LAYER
Physical Sensors
LM35 DHT22 PIR MQ135"] B["TIER 2: NETWORK LAYER
Gateway and Protocols
WiFi ZigBee BLE LoRa"] C["TIER 3: CLOUD LAYER
Storage and Analytics
AWS IoT Azure Firebase"] D["TIER 4: APPLICATION LAYER
User Interface
Web Dashboard Mobile App Alert"] A -->|analog or digital data| B B -->|MQTT CoAP HTTP| C C -->|REST WebSocket| D D -->|actuation control back| A ``` The feedback arrow from D to A represents **closed-loop control** (e.g., turning on an AC when temperature exceeds a threshold). This is the heart of every template — the template is parameterized only at Tier 1 (which sensor) and Tier 4 (which app); Tiers 2 and 3 are largely reusable. ### 4.3 Decision Flow: Which IoT Level Should I Choose? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Do you need
continuous sensing?"} Q2{"Is your scale
millions of items?"} Q3{"Do you need
mission-critical control?"} Q4{"Do devices need
wide area cellular link?"} Q5{"Do you need
global big-data analytics?"} L1["CHOOSE LEVEL 1: WSN"] L2["CHOOSE LEVEL 2: RFID"] L3["CHOOSE LEVEL 3: SCADA"] L4["CHOOSE LEVEL 4: M2M"] L5["CHOOSE LEVEL 5: CLOUD"] Q1 -- yes --> Q3 Q1 -- no --> Q2 Q3 -- yes --> L3 Q3 -- no --> Q4 Q2 -- yes --> L2 Q2 -- no --> Q1 Q4 -- yes --> L4 Q4 -- no --> Q5 Q5 -- yes --> L5 Q5 -- no --> L1 ``` ### 4.4 Template Instantiation Workflow (Sequence View) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1: Identify the sensing problem
e.g., monitor cold-chain temperature"] S2["STEP 2: Select a sensor type
DS18B20 digital temperature sensor"] S3["STEP 3: Choose connectivity protocol
LoRaWAN for long range low power"] S4["STEP 4: Configure cloud endpoint
AWS IoT Core MQTT topic"] S5["STEP 5: Build application UI
Grafana dashboard with alert rule"] S6["STEP 6: Test, deploy, monitor
Threshold alert at greater than 8 deg C"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define IoT Levels. List any two distinguishing features of WSN-based and RFID-based IoT.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** IoT Levels are a classification of Internet of Things systems based on the underlying sensing and communication technology used to acquire and transmit data from the physical world. **Two distinguishing features (2 marks — 1 mark each):** | Aspect | WSN-Based (Level 1) | RFID-Based (Level 2) | |--------|---------------------|----------------------| | Sensing | Continuous environmental sensing (temp, humidity, gas) | Identity-only sensing (EPC tag ID) | | Power | Battery-powered motes (years of life) | Mostly passive — no battery, harvested RF energy | | Range | 10–100 m mesh | 1–10 m (passive), up to 100 m (active) | | Data | Multi-parameter, multi-byte | 64–128 bit unique ID | **Examiner's Note:** The *continuous sensing vs identity-only* contrast is the single most important differentiator and is worth a clear mention. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is an IoT Template? Give two examples of commonly used templates.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** An IoT Template is a reusable, parameterized reference design that abstracts a recurring sensing problem into a four-tier architecture — *Sensing → Network → Cloud → Application*. The same template can be instantiated with different sensors and cloud endpoints for diverse real-world deployments. **Two examples (1 mark — 0.5 each):** 1. **Temperature Monitoring Template** — using DS18B20 / LM35 → MQTT broker → cloud dashboard. 2. **Air Quality Monitoring Template** — using MQ-135 / SDS011 → HTTP POST → cloud analytics. ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE pattern mandates that every Module-1 question be answered with internal choice. You must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B below. Both are calibrated to 14 marks (7 + 7). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024] **(a)** Explain the **five levels of IoT** in detail. Compare any two levels in terms of range, power, and typical application. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The five levels of IoT classify systems by the primary data-acquisition substrate used to interact with the physical world. **Level 1 – WSN-based IoT (2 marks):** A Wireless Sensor Network consists of spatially distributed autonomous sensor motes (e.g., using ZigBee or 6LoWPAN). They self-organize into a mesh, measure physical phenomena (temperature, humidity, vibration), and forward data hop-by-hop to a sink gateway. The sink bridges data to a server. **Application:** precision agriculture, forest-fire detection, bridge-health monitoring. **Level 2 – RFID-based IoT (1.5 marks):** RFID uses electromagnetic fields to automatically identify and track tags attached to objects. Passive tags harvest energy from the reader; active tags have their own battery. The reader sends tag IDs to a middleware (e.g., EPCIS). **Application:** supply-chain tracking, toll collection, livestock ID. **Level 3 – SCADA-based IoT (1.5 marks):** SCADA systems supervise geographically dispersed industrial assets. RTUs/PLCs at the field level collect sensor data; an MTU at the control center polls them and presents a Human-Machine Interface. **Application:** power grids, water-treatment plants, oil pipelines. **Level 4 – M2M-based IoT (1 mark):** M2M enables direct device-to-device (or device-to-server) communication over cellular/satellite networks without human intervention. **Application:** fleet tracking, smart meters, vending machines. **Level 5 – Cloud / Application-level IoT (1 mark):** The top level hosts analytics, storage, and applications on elastic cloud infrastructure (AWS IoT, Azure). It consumes data from lower levels and delivers value-added services. **Application:** smart-city dashboards, connected-car platforms. > The *marks split above is cumulative* — the comparison table below is the closing requirement of the sub-part. **Comparison — WSN (Level 1) vs RFID (Level 2):** | Parameter | WSN (Level 1) | RFID (Level 2) | |-----------|---------------|----------------| | Range | 10–100 m (mesh) | 1–10 m passive / up to 100 m active | | Power | Battery (months–years) | Passive: none; Active: years | | Typical Application | Environmental monitoring in a forest | Inventory tracking in a warehouse | **(b)** Describe the **IoT Reference Template** with a neat block diagram. Explain each of its four tiers. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** An IoT Template is a generic, reusable four-tier reference architecture that abstracts any sensing application. The four tiers are: **Tier 1 – Sensing Layer (1.5 marks):** This is the *physical interface* to the real world. It contains analog/digital sensors (LM35, DHT22, MQ-135, PIR) and, in some designs, actuators. Signal conditioning circuits (amplifiers, filters) convert raw sensor output to a form readable by an ADC. **Tier 2 – Network / Gateway Layer (1.5 marks):** This tier aggregates and transports data. It includes a microcontroller (Arduino, ESP32, Raspberry Pi) and a transceiver (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, BLE, LoRa, cellular). The choice depends on range, bandwidth, and power constraints of the use case. **Tier 3 – Cloud / Storage Layer (2 marks):** Data is published to a cloud platform (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Firebase, ThingSpeak) using protocols like MQTT, CoAP, or HTTP. The cloud provides persistent storage, time-series databases, and analytics engines (rule engines, ML models). **Tier 4 – Application Layer (2 marks):** The user-facing tier delivers value. It includes web dashboards (Grafana, Node-RED), mobile apps, alerting systems (SMS, e-mail), and sometimes actuation commands sent *back* to Tier 1 (closed-loop control). **Block Diagram (textual representation — full marks will be given for a clean sketch with arrows):** ``` [Sensors] -> [Gateway + Protocol] -> [Cloud Broker + Storage] -> [Dashboard + Alerts] ^ | |_________________ actuation feedback loop _____________________| ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. Do **not** confuse "IoT Levels" (5 levels by technology) with "IoT Template" (4 tiers of architecture). Examiners will award **0 marks** if the two are mixed. > 2. Always draw the **block diagram** for part (b). A textual-only answer loses at least **2 marks**. > 3. State the **protocol name explicitly** (MQTT, CoAP, ZigBee) — generic phrases like "wireless protocol" are penalized. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** With a suitable example, explain how an **RFID-based IoT system** works. Derive the expression for the **maximum read-range of a passive tag**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Working of an RFID System (3 marks):** A typical RFID system has three components: 1. **Tag** — contains an antenna and an IC with a unique EPC. 2. **Reader** — emits an RF signal, harvests tag response, decodes EPC. 3. **Middleware / Application** — receives EPC, looks up in database, displays. **Example:** A warehouse installs RFID readers at dock doors. When a pallet carrying a passive UHF tag passes through, the reader's field energizes the tag, the tag backscatters its EPC, and the inventory system updates in real time. **Derivation of Maximum Read Range (4 marks):** Starting from the Friis equation: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{r} &= P_{t}\, G_{t}\, G_{r} \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2} \end{aligned} $$ Setting $P_{r} = P_{th}$ (tag activation threshold) and solving for $d$: $$ \begin{aligned} d_{\max} &= \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{\frac{P_{t}\, G_{t}\, G_{r}}{P_{th}}} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting $\lambda = c / f$: $$ \begin{aligned} d_{\max} &= \frac{c}{4\pi f} \sqrt{\frac{P_{t}\, G_{t}\, G_{r}}{P_{th}}} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical illustration (1 mark):** For $f = 866$ MHz, $P_{t} = 1$ W, $G_{t} = G_{r} = 1.64$, $P_{th} = 50 \,\mu\text{W}$, we get $d_{\max} \approx 6.4$ m (as shown in Section 3.1). **(b)** Explain the **cloud-based IoT level (Level 5)** in detail. Discuss its key components, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (1 mark):** Level 5, the *application / cloud-based IoT level*, is the topmost architectural layer where data from lower IoT levels (WSN, RFID, SCADA, M2M) is ingested, stored, analyzed, and exposed as services to end-users via web/mobile applications. **Key Components (3 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Ingestion Layer** — accepts high-throughput data via MQTT brokers, Kafka streams, or HTTP endpoints. 2. **Storage Layer** — time-series databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB), NoSQL stores (MongoDB, DynamoDB). 3. **Processing / Analytics Layer** — stream processing (Apache Flink), batch analytics (Spark), ML models. 4. **API Layer** — REST/gRPC endpoints exposing data to applications. 5. **Presentation Layer** — dashboards, mobile apps, alerting (Grafana, Kibana, custom React/Angular). 6. **Security Layer** — OAuth2.0, TLS, device-certificate provisioning (X.509). **Advantages (2 marks):** - **Elastic scalability** — pay-as-you-go compute/storage. - **Global accessibility** — any device with Internet can access. - **Big-data analytics** — ML at population scale. - **Reduced local complexity** — thin edge devices. **Limitations (1 mark):** - **Latency** — typically 100 ms – s, unsuitable for hard real-time control. - **Privacy / data-sovereignty** — sensitive data may leave the country. - **Connectivity dependency** — needs reliable Internet at the edge. - **Ongoing operational cost** — subscription fees. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall (Question B):** > 1. Students often forget to **substitute $\lambda = c / f$** in the read-range formula — losing **1 mark** for an incomplete expression. > 2. For part (b), do *not* describe cloud in general (AWS, Azure features). Stay **strictly on IoT-cloud architecture** (ingestion → storage → analytics → app). > 3. Always cite **at least two advantages and one limitation** — partial marking is rigorous. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > Use this list for the **last 10 minutes** of revision before the KTU 2024 ESE. - [x] **Five IoT Levels** — memorize in order: *WSN, RFID, SCADA, M2M, Cloud*. - [x] **IoT Template = 4 tiers** — *Sensing, Network, Cloud, Application* (not the same as Levels). - [x] **WSN uses mesh; RFID uses star; SCADA uses master-slave; M2M uses cellular; Cloud uses client-server.** - [x] **Passive RFID** has no battery and ≤ 10 m range; **Active RFID** has a battery and ≤ 100 m. - [x] **Friis equation** + tag-threshold condition gives the **maximum read range formula** — a guaranteed KTU numerical. - [x] **RSSI-to-distance conversion** uses the log-distance path-loss model — another favorite numerical. - [x] **Nyquist criterion** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ must always be stated when discussing sensor sampling. - [x] **SCADA protocols:** Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870 (memorize at least two). - [x] **IoT application protocols:** MQTT (pub-sub, TCP), CoAP (REST-like, UDP), HTTP (heavyweight), LWM2M (M2M). - [x] **Cloud platforms commonly cited:** AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT, ThingSpeak, Firebase. - [x] **Template examples to memorize:** Temperature, Humidity, Air Quality, Soil Moisture, Smart Metering, Motion/Intrusion, Health/Wearable. - [x] **Levels are stacked, not exclusive** — a real IoT system usually *combines* Level 1 (sensors) + Level 4 (connectivity) + Level 5 (cloud). - [x] **Free-space path loss** grows as $20 \log_{10}(d)$ — affects both WSN and RFID planning. - [x] **Do not confuse:** IoT Levels (technology-based) ↔ IoT Templates (function-based) ↔ IoT Reference Model (3-layer: perception, network, application). - [x] **Common examiner traps:** writing "IoT level" instead of "IoT tier"; omitting protocol names; forgetting the feedback loop in templates.

topics.title: Domain Specific IoTs - Home, City, Environment, Energy, Agriculture and Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Domain Specific IoTs - Home, City, Environment, Energy, Agriculture and Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Domain-Specific IoT"?

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Domain-Specific IoT** refers to the customized application of Internet of Things (IoT) architectures, sensor networks, communication protocols, and data analytics within a specific vertical industry or use-case domain. Instead of a one-size-fits-all IoT framework, the hardware (sensors/actuators), software (protocols/cloud), and data requirements are tailored to the unique operational, safety, and economic constraints of that domain.

**Six Core Vertical Domains (Per KTU Module 1 Syllabus):**

| # | Domain | Primary Focus |
|---|--------|---------------|
| 1 | **Home** | Consumer comfort, security, energy saving |
| 2 | **City** | Urban infrastructure, public services, traffic |
| 3 | **Environment** | Pollution, climate, biodiversity monitoring |
| 4 | **Energy** | Smart grid, renewable integration, metering |
| 5 | **Agriculture** | Precision farming, irrigation, livestock |
| 6 | **Industry** | Automation, predictive maintenance, IIoT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course emphasizes that a single IoT framework cannot be reused across all these domains. The sensors, data sampling rates, communication ranges, power budgets, and latency requirements differ **drastically**. A temperature sensor in a *smart home* (slow, 1-min sampling) is fundamentally different from one in *industry* (fast, sub-millisecond, vibration-tolerant).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Specialist Doctor vs. The General Physician"

Think of generic IoT as a **General Physician** — they can take basic readings (temperature, pulse) and offer general advice. But **Domain-Specific IoT** is like a **Specialist Doctor**:

- A **Cardiologist** uses an ECG machine (specialized sensor), monitors the heart 24/7 (continuous telemetry), and interprets data with cardiology-trained AI.
- A **Dermatologist** uses a dermatoscope (different sensor), looks at skin texture (different metric), and diagnoses differently.

Similarly:
- **Smart Home IoT** is like a *family doctor* — comfortable, low-cost, slow, focused on user experience.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** is like a *cardiologist* — precise, high-frequency, mission-critical, zero tolerance for downtime.
- **Agricultural IoT** is like a *public-health doctor* — covers vast areas, sparse data, long battery life (operates for years in a field).

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Takeaway:** *Domain-Specific IoT = taking a generic "sense–compute–communicate" pipeline and re-engineering every block to suit the domain's physical, economic, and regulatory realities.*

---

## 1.3 The Three Universal Pillars of Any Domain-Specific IoT

Regardless of the vertical, every domain-specific IoT system is built on **three pillars**:

1. **Things (Sensors/Actuators)** — the physical interface to the world.
2. **Communication (Network/Protocol)** — how data travels.
3. **Intelligence (Cloud/Edge Analytics)** — how data is processed and acted upon.

The **same three pillars**, but the **contents of each pillar change per domain**.

---

## 1.4 The "Sensing–Acting–Deciding" Triangle

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Concept (Map it to any domain):**
> Every IoT domain system can be abstracted into the **S–A–D Triangle**:
> - **Sensing layer** → collects raw physical/chemical data.
> - **Acting layer** → triggers a physical response (valve closes, alarm rings, motor starts).
> - **Deciding layer** → middleware/AI that links sensed data to the correct action.

For example:
- **Smart Home**: Smoke sensor *senses* → microcontroller *decides* → buzzer *acts*.
- **Smart Agriculture**: Soil moisture sensor *senses* → edge AI *decides* → solenoid valve *acts*.

This triangle is the **mental model** you must use when answering KTU questions on *any* IoT domain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Domain 1 — Smart Home IoT

### 2.1.1 Definition
A **Smart Home** is a residence equipped with sensors, actuators, and communication modules that allow remote monitoring and control of household appliances, lighting, security, and energy systems, typically via a smartphone or voice assistant.

### 2.1.2 Key Functional Sub-Systems
- **Home Automation:** Lighting (smart bulbs), climate (smart thermostats like Nest), appliances (smart plugs).
- **Home Security:** Door/window sensors, PIR motion detectors, smart doorbells (Ring), IP cameras.
- **Energy Management:** Smart meters, solar inverters, EV chargers.
- **Health & Wellness:** Wearable integration, fall detectors, air-quality monitors.
- **Entertainment:** Multi-room audio, smart TVs.

### 2.1.3 Sensors Used
| Sensor | Function | Typical Output |
|--------|----------|----------------|
| **PIR (Passive Infrared)** | Detect human motion | Digital pulse |
| **DHT22 / BME280** | Temperature & humidity | Digital (I²C) |
| **MQ-2 / MQ-135** | Smoke / gas leakage | Analog voltage |
| **Reed Switch / Hall Sensor** | Door/window open-close | Digital |
| **LDR / Photodiode** | Ambient light intensity | Analog / digital |
| **Microphone (SPH0645)** | Voice command (Alexa) | I²S digital audio |
| **Accelerometer (ADXL345)** | Vibration / glass break | I²C/SPI |

### 2.1.4 Communication Protocols
- **Short-range:** Wi-Fi (802.11n/ac), **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)**, **Zigbee**, **Z-Wave**, **Thread**.
- **Application-layer:** **MQTT** (most common), HTTP/REST, CoAP.
- **Voice integration:** Custom APIs for Google Assistant, Alexa Skills.

### 2.1.5 Reference Architecture
- **Edge device:** ESP32 / Raspberry Pi with sensor shield.
- **Hub/Gateway:** Smart speaker (Alexa Echo) or dedicated hub (Samsung SmartThings).
- **Cloud:** AWS IoT / Google Cloud IoT / proprietary vendor cloud.
- **Mobile app:** User interface for control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Profile:** Smart home sensors are mostly **mains-powered or rechargeable**. Latency tolerance is **1–5 seconds** (human-perceptible). Data volume is **low (kB/day per device)**.

---

## 2.2 Domain 2 — Smart City IoT

### 2.2.1 Definition
A **Smart City** uses IoT technologies to improve the quality, performance, and interactivity of urban services — including transportation, utilities, public safety, and administration — by collecting and analyzing data from a dense network of city-wide sensors.

### 2.2.2 Vertical Sub-Domains
- **Smart Transportation:** Traffic signals, parking sensors, GPS on public buses.
- **Smart Lighting:** Adaptive streetlights that dim when no one is around.
- **Smart Waste Management:** Ultrasonic fill-level sensors in dustbins → optimize collection routes.
- **Smart Water Distribution:** Pressure & flow sensors in pipelines → detect leaks.
- **Air Quality Monitoring:** Multi-gas sensors (NO₂, SO₂, PM2.5, PM10) on lamp posts.
- **Public Safety:** Gunshot-detection microphones, crowd-density cameras, emergency call boxes.
- **Smart Governance:** Citizen complaint apps, sensor-driven asset management.

### 2.2.3 Key Sensors & Devices
| Sub-Domain | Sensor | Measurement |
|------------|--------|-------------|
| Traffic | Inductive loop, LiDAR, camera | Vehicle count, speed |
| Parking | Ultrasonic / magnetometer | Slot occupancy |
| Waste | Ultrasonic (HC-SR04) | Bin fill height |
| Air quality | Laser particle counter, electrochemical cell | PM2.5, CO, NO₂ |
| Noise pollution | MEMS microphone | Sound level (dB) |
| Streetlight | PIR + LDR | Pedestrian/vehicle presence |

### 2.2.4 Communication Backbone
- **Backhaul:** 4G/5G cellular, LoRaWAN (for sparse sensors), NB-IoT, fiber.
- **LAN:** Zigbee, BLE, sub-GHz RF.
- **Data exchange:** **MQTT-SN**, **CoAP**, **LwM2M** (Lightweight M2M).
- **City data platform:** FIWARE, SmartSantander, Sentilo.

> [!NOTE]
> **KPIs of Smart City IoT:** Coverage (%), Data freshness (s), Energy per node (Wh/day), Cost per citizen served.

---

## 2.3 Domain 3 — Smart Environment IoT

### 2.3.1 Definition
**Smart Environment** IoT refers to distributed sensor networks deployed in natural or semi-natural ecosystems to monitor environmental parameters such as air quality, water quality, soil contamination, forest fires, glacier melt, and wildlife — typically for scientific, regulatory, or early-warning purposes.

### 2.3.2 Sub-Domains
- **Air Pollution Monitoring**
- **Water Quality Monitoring** (rivers, lakes, oceans)
- **Soil & Land Monitoring**
- **Forest Fire Detection**
- **Wildlife Tracking** (collared animals, birds, fish)
- **Climate & Weather Monitoring**
- **Disaster Early Warning** (flood, landslide, earthquake precursors)

### 2.3.3 Sensors Used
| Parameter | Sensor | Operating Principle |
|-----------|--------|---------------------|
| CO₂ | **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared)** | IR absorption at 4.26 µm |
| PM2.5/PM10 | **Laser scattering sensor** (e.g., SDS011) | Mie scattering |
| pH of water | Glass electrode + reference | Nernst potential |
| Dissolved Oxygen | Galvanic / optical (luminescent) | Quenching of fluorescence |
| Turbidity | Optical (photodiode + 90° scatter) | Light scatter |
| Soil moisture | Capacitive / TDR | Dielectric permittivity |
| Forest fire | Multi-spectral IR + smoke (MQ-2) + temperature | Threshold + pattern |
| Wildlife GPS collar | GPS + GSM/satellite | Triangulation |
| Seismic | Geophone / MEMS accelerometer | Vibration |

### 2.3.4 Unique Engineering Challenges
- **Harsh outdoor deployment:** IP65/IP67 enclosures, UV-resistant casings, anti-fungal coatings.
- **Power autonomy:** Solar panels + Li-ion battery; ultra-low-power MCUs (e.g., MSP430, STM32L0).
- **Long-range, low-bandwidth:** **LoRa**, **Sigfox**, satellite IoT (e.g., Iridium, Astrocast).
- **Calibration drift:** Field calibration routines; reference-sensor cross-validation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Quality Mandate:** Environmental data is used for **policy and legal decisions** (e.g., declaring a city "polluted"). Hence, sensor accuracy and traceability to national standards (e.g., CPCB in India, EPA in USA) is **non-negotiable**.

---

## 2.4 Domain 4 — Smart Energy IoT (Smart Grid)

### 2.4.1 Definition
**Smart Energy / Smart Grid IoT** is the integration of ICT with the electrical grid, enabling bidirectional flow of power and information between utilities and consumers. It supports **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)**, **demand response**, **distributed energy resources (DER)**, and **grid stability analytics**.

### 2.4.2 Sub-Systems
- **Smart Metering (AMI):** Replaces electromechanical meters with digital meters + communication module.
- **Demand Response (DR):** Real-time pricing signals sent to consumers to shift load.
- **Distributed Energy Resources (DER):** Rooftop solar, small wind, battery storage, V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid).
- **Substation Automation:** IEC 61850 protocol, IEDs (Intelligent Electronic Devices).
- **EV Charging Infrastructure:** Smart chargers with OCPP protocol.
- **Microgrids:** Localized grids that can island from the main grid.

### 2.4.3 Sensors / Devices
| Component | Sensor | Role |
|-----------|--------|------|
| Smart meter | Current transformer (CT), ADE7953 IC | Active/reactive power, energy, PF |
| Transformer monitor | PT100 RTD, dissolved gas analyzer (DGA) | Temperature, fault gas (H₂, CH₄) |
| Solar inverter | DC-side V/I sensor, MPPT controller | DC/AC conversion efficiency |
| Wind turbine | Anemometer, vibration sensor, pitch encoder | Performance + condition |
| EV charger | Voltage, current, temperature, RCD | Safety + billing |
| Battery storage | Cell voltage, current, temperature (NTC) | State-of-Health (SoH) |

### 2.4.4 Key Communication Standards
- **HAN (Home Area Network):** Zigbee, Wi-Fi, HomePlug (PLC).
- **NAN (Neighborhood Area Network):** RF mesh, cellular (LTE-M, NB-IoT).
- **WAN (Wide Area Network):** Cellular, fiber, satellite.
- **Protocols:** **DLMS/COSEM**, **IEC 61850**, **OpenADR** (demand response), **OCPP 1.6/2.0.1** (EV charging), **IEEE 2030.5**.

### 2.4.5 Quantitative Performance Metrics
- **System Average Interruption Duration Index (SAIDI):**
$$\text{SAIDI} = \frac{\sum (\text{Customer interruptions} \times \text{Duration})}{\text{Total customers served}}$$

- **System Average Interruption Frequency Index (SAIFI):**
$$\text{SAIFI} = \frac{\sum \text{Total customers interrupted}}{\text{Total customers served}}$$

- **Peak-to-Average Ratio (PAR)** for demand response:
$$\text{PAR} = \frac{P_{\text{peak}}}{P_{\text{avg}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Goal of Smart Grid IoT:** *Reduce SAIDI, flatten PAR, maximize renewable penetration, and enable prosumer (producer + consumer) participation.*

---

## 2.5 Domain 5 — Smart Agriculture IoT

### 2.5.1 Definition
**Smart Agriculture (or Precision Agriculture)** uses IoT sensors, drones, satellite imagery, and analytics to optimize farming operations — irrigation, fertilization, pest control, harvesting — at the **sub-field level**, reducing input cost and environmental impact while increasing yield.

### 2.5.2 Sub-Domains
- **Precision Irrigation** (drip + sensor-driven)
- **Soil Health Monitoring**
- **Crop Health & Disease Detection** (multispectral imaging, NDVI)
- **Greenhouse Automation**
- **Livestock Monitoring** (wearables for cattle, poultry)
- **Aquaculture Monitoring** (pH, DO, ammonia in fish ponds)
- **Drone-based Field Survey**

### 2.5.3 Sensors Used
| Parameter | Sensor | Use |
|-----------|--------|-----|
| Soil moisture | Capacitive (e.g., v1.2 capacitive) | Irrigation scheduling |
| Soil pH & NPK | Ion-selective electrodes (ISE) | Fertilization |
| Soil temperature | DS18B20 | Germination timing |
| Ambient T/RH | DHT22, BME280 | Greenhouse climate |
| Leaf wetness | Resistive grid | Disease prediction |
| Solar radiation | Pyranometer (e.g., SP-110) | Evapotranspiration (ET₀) |
| Wind speed/direction | Anemometer + wind vane | Spraying decisions |
| CO₂ in greenhouse | NDIR (K30) | Plant growth boost |
| Livestock body temp | RFID + temperature bolus | Disease early-warning |
| Drone NDVI camera | Multispectral (Red Edge, NIR) | Crop vigor map |

### 2.5.4 Communication Architecture
- **In-field:** LoRaWAN, Zigbee, Sub-GHz RF, BLE.
- **Backhaul:** 4G/5G, satellite (for remote farms).
- **Data formats:** ISOBUS (ISO 11783) for tractor-implement communication.
- **Cloud platforms:** John Deere Operations Center, Climate FieldView, Cropin SmartFarm.

### 2.5.5 Key Engineering Trade-off
> Energy vs. Distance vs. Sampling Rate. A soil moisture sensor in a 50-acre farm needs to **last 3–5 years on a single battery**, sample **once per hour**, and transmit over **1–3 km**. This is the textbook use case for **LoRaWAN** with Class A devices.

---

## 2.6 Domain 6 — Industrial IoT (IIoT)

### 2.6.1 Definition
**Industrial IoT (IIoT)** is the application of IoT technologies in **manufacturing, energy, logistics, and process industries** to enable **smart factories (Industry 4.0)**, **predictive maintenance**, **digital twins**, and **closed-loop control**.

### 2.6.2 Sub-Domains (Industry 4.0 Pillars)
1. **Smart Manufacturing / Smart Factory**
2. **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)**
3. **Digital Twin**
4. **Supply Chain & Logistics IoT**
5. **Process Automation & Control (SCADA + IoT)**
6. **Worker Safety & Wearables**

### 2.6.3 Sensors Used
| Application | Sensor | Purpose |
|-------------|--------|---------|
| Vibration on motor | MEMS accelerometer (ADXL100x) | Bearing fault detection |
| Temperature of bearing | RTD / thermocouple | Overheat alarm |
| Pressure in pipeline | Strain-gauge / piezoresistive | Process control |
| Flow in pipe | Coriolis, magnetic, ultrasonic | Mass / volume flow |
| Level in tank | Radar, ultrasonic, capacitive | Inventory |
| Gas in chemical plant | Electrochemical, NDIR, PID | Safety / leak |
| Position / proximity | Inductive, capacitive, Lidar | Robot guidance |
| Vision / quality | 2D/3D camera, ToF sensor | Defect detection |
| RFID on parts | UHF RFID reader | Traceability |

### 2.6.4 Industrial Protocols
- **Field level:** Modbus RTU/TCP, Profibus, Profinet, EtherCAT, EtherNet/IP.
- **M2M / application:** **OPC UA** (Unified Architecture), **MQTT with Sparkplug B**, **AMQP**.
- **Time-critical:** **TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking)** on Ethernet.

### 2.6.5 Key Architecture: The Three-Tier IIoT Stack
- **Tier 1 — Device/Sensor Layer:** PLCs, RTUs, smart sensors, actuators.
- **Tier 2 — Edge/Network Layer:** Industrial gateways, edge controllers, TSN switches.
- **Tier 3 — Cloud/Analytics Layer:** Historian, MES, ERP integration, ML models.

### 2.6.6 Reliability Metrics
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):**
$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total operational time}}{\text{Number of failures}}$$

- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):**
$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total downtime}}{\text{Number of failures}}$$

- **Availability:**
$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry 4.0 Goal:** *Increase OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) by 10–25% and reduce unplanned downtime by 30–50% using IoT-based predictive maintenance.*

---

| Data/day | kB | MB–GB | kB | MB | kB | GB |
