# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions model the KTU 2024 ESE Part A format (typically 3–5 mark questions, requiring concise definition-style or one-paragraph answers).

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`
**Q: List the four primary categories of factors driving the deployment of sensor technology in modern engineering systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four primary categories are:
1. **Technological Drivers** — advances in MEMS/NEMS, novel materials, wireless protocols, and edge-AI.
2. **Economic Drivers** — falling sensor unit costs, economies of scale, and favorable ROI on automation.
3. **Application/Pull Drivers** — rising demand from IoT, Industry 4.0, automotive ADAS, healthcare wearables, and smart-city applications.
4. **Regulatory/Policy Drivers** — government mandates on emissions (BS-VI, Euro 7), worker safety, food cold-chain logging, and data-privacy laws (GDPR, DPDP Act).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`
**Q: Define the *cost-decay constant* $\lambda$ in the context of sensor unit-pricing trends, and state its typical range for MEMS sensors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The cost-decay constant $\lambda$ (in **year⁻¹**) is the proportionality factor in the exponential cost-decay equation $C_s(t) = C_{s,0} e^{-\lambda (t - t_0)}$, representing the fractional rate at which the unit price of a sensor category declines over time. For MEMS sensors, historical data indicates a typical $\lambda$ range of **0.15 to 0.25 per year**, corresponding to 14%–22% annual price reductions. A higher $\lambda$ implies faster commoditization and stronger economic incentive for large-scale deployment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. The student is offered an internal choice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **technological drivers** that are accelerating the deployment of modern sensor technology. Substantiate your answer with at least four concrete examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the exponential cost-decay equation $C_s(t) = C_{s,0} e^{-\lambda (t - t_0)}$ from first principles. If a MEMS accelerometer costs **USD 2.50** in 2022 and $\lambda = 0.20\ \text{year}^{-1}$, estimate its projected unit cost in **2028**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

The technological drivers accelerating sensor deployment are:

1. **MEMS and NEMS Miniaturization (2 marks):** Photolithographic batch-fabrication enables thousands of sensor dice per wafer. A modern Bosch BMI270 IMU, for instance, integrates a 3-axis accelerometer and 3-axis gyroscope on a 2.0 mm × 2.5 mm die, with power consumption under 1 mW.

2. **Advanced Materials — 2D Materials (1.5 marks):** Graphene and MoS₂ offer extraordinary surface-area-to-volume ratios, enabling gas sensors with sub-ppb detection limits. Their mechanical flexibility also supports wearable form factors.

3. **Wireless Communication Protocols (1.5 marks):** BLE 5.x, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, and 5G NR-Light have removed the wired tether, allowing untethered, large-scale sensor meshes (e.g., 10,000-node LoRa deployments in smart agriculture).

4. **Edge AI and TinyML (2 marks):** On-sensor inference using microcontrollers (e.g., MAX78000, STM32N6) enables privacy-preserving, low-latency decision-making, eliminating cloud round-trips and making sensors *autonomous*.

*Real-world convergence example:* A wearable continuous-glucose monitor (CGM) like the Dexcom G7 integrates all four drivers — MEMS electrochemical sensor, flexible substrate, BLE telemetry, and on-device predictive alerts.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (4 marks):** [Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes; the student must show all 8 steps explicitly, from empirical observation through exponentiation.]

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

$$
t - t_0 \;=\; 2028 - 2022 \;=\; 6\ \text{years}
$$

$$
C_s(2028) \;=\; 2.50 \cdot e^{-(0.20)(6)} \;=\; 2.50 \cdot e^{-1.20}
$$

Computing the exponential term:

$$
e^{-1.20} \;\approx\; 0.3012
$$

$$
C_s(2028) \;\approx\; 2.50 \times 0.3012 \;\approx\; 0.75\ \text{USD}
$$

**Conclusion:** The projected unit cost in 2028 is approximately **USD 0.75**, representing a **70% cost reduction** over six years, well within the typical MEMS commoditization trajectory.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Explain the role of **regulatory and policy drivers** in the deployment of sensor technology, with specific reference to emissions monitoring and worker safety standards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **operational battery lifetime equation** for a duty-cycled wireless sensor node and use it to compute the lifetime (in days) of a node with: $C_{batt} = 2.4\ \text{Ah}$, $I_{active} = 15\ \text{mA}$, $I_{sleep} = 5\ \mu\text{A}$, $t_{active} = 0.5\ \text{s}$, $t_{sleep} = 9.5\ \text{s}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

Regulatory and policy drivers are *non-market* forces that *mandate* sensor deployment. The student should cover:

1. **Emissions Monitoring (3 marks):** BS-VI norms in India (effective April 2020) and Euro 7 (planned) require every internal combustion vehicle to be equipped with multiple gas sensors (NOx, lambda oxygen, particulate). The European On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II) directive mandates continuous emission-relevant sensor monitoring. These regulations have created a *guaranteed market* of hundreds of millions of automotive sensors per year.

2. **Worker Safety (2 marks):** The Indian Factories Act 1948 (amended) and OSHA-equivalent regulations mandate the deployment of toxic-gas detectors (H₂S, CO, CH₄, LEL) in confined industrial spaces such as petrochemical plants, underground mines, and sewage systems. Non-compliance invites punitive action, making sensor deployment *legally compulsory*.

3. **Food Safety and Cold Chain (1 mark):** FSSAI and HACCP norms mandate temperature/humidity logging for perishable goods in transit, driving deployment of low-cost BLE temperature loggers.

4. **Data Privacy (1 mark):** GDPR (EU) and India's DPDP Act 2023 require that personal-data-collecting sensors (e.g., surveillance cameras, biometric wearables) incorporate *privacy-by-design* principles such as on-device anonymization and federated learning.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (4 marks):** [Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the full 7-step derivation. The student must explicitly state the formula derived in Step 7.]

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**
Given:
- $C_{batt} = 2.4\ \text{Ah} = 2.4 \times 3600 = 8640\ \text{C}$ (coulombs, used implicitly in the energy calculation).
- $I_{active} = 15 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A} = 0.015\ \text{A}$.
- $I_{sleep} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A} = 0.000005\ \text{A}$.
- $t_{active} = 0.5\ \text{s}$; $t_{sleep} = 9.5\ \text{s}$.

**Cycle period:**

$$
T_{cycle} = 0.5 + 9.5 = 10\ \text{s}
$$

**Charge consumed per cycle (Coulombs):**

$$
Q_{cycle} = I_{active} \cdot t_{active} + I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}
$$

$$
Q_{cycle} = (0.015)(0.5) + (0.000005)(9.5) = 0.0075 + 0.0000475 \approx 0.0075475\ \text{C}
$$

**Total lifetime in seconds:**

$$
T_{life} = \frac{8640}{0.0075475} \approx 1{,}144{,}928\ \text{s}
$$

**Convert to days:**

$$
T_{life,\ days} = \frac{1{,}144{,}928}{86{,}400} \approx 13.25\ \text{days}
$$

**Conclusion:** The wireless sensor node will operate for approximately **13.25 days** on a single 2.4 Ah battery under the given duty cycle. This relatively short lifetime — despite the aggressive 5% duty cycle — illustrates *why ultra-low-power design* is a technological driver for sensor deployment in inaccessible locations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic**
> 1. **Forgetting the units of $\lambda$:** Students often write $\lambda = 0.18$ without specifying **year⁻¹**. Always include units in your final answer. *(−0.5 marks)*
> 2. **Confusing voltage-domain SNR (20 log₁₀) with power-domain SNR (10 log₁₀):** This single error costs up to 2 marks if the question is SNR-based. *(−2 marks)*
> 3. **Omitting the base year $t_0$ in the cost-decay equation:** Many students write $C_s(t) = C_{s,0} e^{-\lambda t}$ without anchoring $t_0$. The KTU evaluator will deduct 1 mark for this oversight.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps in Part (b) of 14-mark questions:** The KTU 2024 scheme mandates *stepwise* derivation. Even if your final answer is correct, omitting intermediate steps can cost 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Treating drivers as independent:** When asked to "discuss deployment drivers," examiners expect students to highlight the *convergence* of all four pillars. A pillar-by-pillar answer without mentioning convergence is incomplete. *(−1 to 2 marks)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam)**

- ☐ The **four pillars** of deployment drivers: Technological, Economic, Application/Pull, Regulatory/Policy.
- ☐ The **cost-decay equation**: $C_s(t) = C_{s,0} e^{-\lambda (t - t_0)}$; units of $\lambda$ are **year⁻¹**.
- ☐ The **breakeven equation**: $T_{breakeven} = C_{install} / (S_{annual} - O_{annual})$; output is in **years**.
- ☐ The **IoT growth equation**: $N_{IoT}(t) = N_0 e^{g (t - t_0)}$; CAGR $g$ is in **year⁻¹**.
- ☐ The **battery lifetime equation**: $T_{life} = (C_{batt} \times 3600 \times T_{cycle}) / (I_{active} t_{active} + I_{sleep} t_{sleep})$; output is in **seconds**.
- ☐ The **SNR equations** (voltage-domain uses 20 log₁₀; power-domain uses 10 log₁₀).
- ☐ **SNR in dB** is dimensionless but conventionally carries the unit "dB".
- ☐ Typical $\lambda$ for MEMS sensors is in the range **0.15 to 0.25 year⁻¹**.
- ☐ Typical IoT CAGR $g$ is in the range **0.12 to 0.18 year⁻¹** (12%–18% per annum).
- ☐ **Convergence principle:** Deployment occurs ONLY when all four pillars align — memorize this for the 7-mark discussion questions.
- ☐ **Real-world anchors** to cite in descriptive answers: Bosch BMI270, Dexcom G7, LoRaWAN smart-agriculture deployments, BS-VI/Euro 7 mandates, GDPR/DPDP Act.
- ☐ Always include **units** in your final numerical answers; KTU evaluators explicitly reward unit discipline.
- ☐ For derivation questions, **show all algebraic steps**; the KTU 2024 scheme values stepwise reasoning (typically 60–70% of marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Light Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Light Sensor** is an optoelectronic transducer that detects the presence, intensity, wavelength, and spatial distribution of incident electromagnetic radiation within the visible, ultraviolet, or infrared spectrum, and converts this optical energy into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or a change in resistance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A light sensor is a photosensitive device that exploits the **photoelectric effect**, **photoconductive effect**, or **photovoltaic effect** to translate photon flux ($\Phi$) into an electrical quantity, following the energy conservation principle $E = h\nu$ where $h$ is the Planck's constant ($6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s) and $\nu$ is the frequency of incident light.

### Classification of Light Sensors (Based on Operating Principle)

| Principle | Sensor Type | Output Signal |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| **External Photoelectric Effect** | Photoemissive Tube (Phototube) | Current |
| **Internal Photoelectric Effect** | Photodiode, Phototransistor | Current |
| **Photoconductive Effect** | Photoresistor (LDR) | Resistance change |
| **Photovoltaic Effect** | Solar Cell, Photodiode (zero bias) | Voltage |
| **Photoelectromagnetic Effect** | PEM Detector | Voltage |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Rain Bucket"**
> Imagine a bucket placed outdoors to collect raindrops. The **amount of water collected** represents the **photocurrent**, while the **intensity of rainfall** is analogous to the **light intensity (lux)**. Just as a wider bucket catches more rain, a larger photosensitive area collects more photons, generating a stronger electrical signal. When the rain stops (darkness), the bucket remains empty (no current). The LDR works like a bucket whose **drain hole size changes with light** — more light = wider drain = lower resistance, draining current faster. Photodiodes, on the other hand, actively count photons and translate each absorbed photon into a measurable electron.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Speed of light (c):** $3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - **Planck's constant (h):** $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - **Elementary charge (e):** $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
> - **Standard Illuminance Unit:** **lux (lx)** — 1 lx = 1 lumen/m²
> - **Luminous Flux Unit:** **lumen (lm)**
> - **Spectral Range Covered:** UV (~200 nm) → Visible (400–700 nm) → IR (~1100 nm for Si)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory of Major Light Sensor Types

### 2.1.1 Photoresistor (LDR — Light Dependent Resistor)
- Constructed from a high-resistance **cadmium sulfide (CdS)** semiconductor.
- When photons strike the CdS film, electrons in the valence band gain energy and jump to the conduction band, increasing carrier concentration and **decreasing resistance**.
- Resistance typically drops from ~**1 MΩ (dark)** to ~**few hundred ohms (bright light)**.

**Why it works:** Photons with energy $E \geq E_g$ (bandgap of CdS ≈ 2.42 eV) generate electron-hole pairs, dramatically increasing conductivity.

### 2.1.2 Photodiode
- Operated typically in **reverse bias** (photoconductive mode) for linear response.
- Photons absorbed in the depletion region create electron-hole pairs separated by the strong electric field, producing a **photocurrent** $I_p$ proportional to incident optical power $P$.
- Fast response time (nanoseconds) — ideal for high-speed applications.

### 2.1.3 Phototransistor
- Essentially a photodiode integrated with a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) amplifier on the same chip.
- Base current is provided by the photogenerated current, which is then amplified by the transistor's current gain $\beta$.
- Provides **100–1000× more sensitivity** than a bare photodiode, but at the cost of slower response.

### 2.1.4 Photovoltaic Cell (Solar Cell)
- Operates in **photovoltaic mode** (zero bias) — the junction itself generates an EMF.
- The open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$ and short-circuit current $I_{SC}$ characterize its behavior.
- Follows the **Shockley diode equation** modified for light generation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Joules (J) or eV | Energy of a single incident photon |
| 2 | Photocurrent | $I_p = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$ | Amperes (A) | $R_\lambda$ = responsivity (A/W) |
| 3 | Responsivity | $R_\lambda = \dfrac{\eta \cdot e \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$ | A/W | $\eta$ = quantum efficiency |
| 4 | Quantum Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{electrons collected/sec}}{\text{incident photons/sec}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Conversion efficiency |
| 5 | LDR Resistance | $R \propto L^{-\gamma}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $L$ = illuminance, $\gamma$ ≈ 0.7–0.9 |
| 6 | Photodiode Current (Total) | $I = I_{dark} - I_p$ | A | Reverse-biased photodiode |
| 7 | Solar Cell Power | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watts (W) | Max at Maximum Power Point (MPP) |
| 8 | Fill Factor (FF) | $FF = \dfrac{V_{MP} \cdot I_{MP}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Solar cell quality metric |
| 9 | Phototransistor Current | $I_C = (\beta + 1) \cdot I_p$ | A | Amplified photocurrent |
| 10 | Wavelength–Frequency | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{\nu}$ | meters (m) | Used for spectral response |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Memorize $R_\lambda = \dfrac{\eta e \lambda}{hc}$. The relationship $R_\lambda \propto \lambda$ shows that **responsivity increases with wavelength** (until the cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Photoresistors:** Streetlight auto-control, camera exposure meters, security alarms.
- **Photodiodes:** Fiber-optic communication receivers, smoke detectors, bar-code scanners, medical pulse oximeters, automotive LiDAR.
- **Phototransistors:** Object detection, optocouplers, tape readers, IR remote decoders.
- **Photovoltaic Cells:** Solar power generation, satellite power systems, calculators, IoT sensor nodes.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Photocurrent from Quantum Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define incident photon flux.**
The number of photons incident per second on an active area of cross-section $A$ is given by:

$$N_{ph} = \frac{P_{opt}}{E_{photon}} = \frac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

*where $P_{opt}$ is the incident optical power in watts, $\lambda$ is the wavelength in meters, $h$ is Planck's constant, and $c$ is the speed of light.*

**Step 2 — Apply quantum efficiency.**
Only a fraction $\eta$ of these incident photons generate collectable electron–hole pairs:

$$N_{e} = \eta \cdot N_{ph} = \frac{\eta \cdot P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

**Step 3 — Convert electrons per second to current.**
Current is charge per unit time. Each electron carries charge $e$:

$$I_p = N_e \cdot e = \frac{\eta \cdot e \cdot \lambda \cdot P_{opt}}{h \cdot c}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the proportionality constant (responsivity).**
Comparing with the sensor model $I_p = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$:

$$R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \cdot e \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

$$\boxed{\; R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \, e \, \lambda}{h \, c} \;}$$

*This is the governing equation KTU examiners frequently test. Note that responsivity grows linearly with wavelength, which is why silicon detectors peak near 850–900 nm.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LDR Resistance vs. Illuminance Characteristic

**Step 1 — Empirical observation.**
The resistance of a CdS photoresistor follows a power-law decay with illuminance:

$$R = A \cdot L^{-\gamma}$$

*where $A$ is a device constant (ohms), $L$ is illuminance in lux, and $\gamma$ is the slope exponent (typically 0.7–0.9 for CdS).*

**Step 2 — Linearize via logarithm.**
Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$\ln(R) = \ln(A) - \gamma \cdot \ln(L)$$

**Step 3 — Slope interpretation.**
The slope of the $\ln(R)$ vs. $\ln(L)$ plot equals $-\gamma$, which characterizes the device's sensitivity.

**Step 4 — Voltage divider output.**
In a typical circuit with series resistor $R_S$ and supply $V_S$:

$$V_{out} = V_S \cdot \frac{R}{R + R_S}$$

*As light increases, $R$ drops, so $V_{out}$ decreases — this inverted response must be accounted for in circuit design.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Solar Cell Fill Factor and Efficiency

**Step 1 — Maximum power point.**
Define voltage and current at the maximum power point (MPP) as $V_{MP}$ and $I_{MP}$.

**Step 2 — Define open-circuit and short-circuit parameters.**
- Open-circuit voltage: $V_{OC}$ (voltage when $I = 0$)
- Short-circuit current: $I_{SC}$ (current when $V = 0$)

**Step 3 — Define Fill Factor.**

$$FF = \frac{V_{MP} \cdot I_{MP}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$$

**Step 4 — Define conversion efficiency.**

$$\eta_{cell} = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$$

*where $P_{in}$ is the total incident solar power on the cell's active area. Typical commercial Si cells: $V_{OC} \approx 0.6$ V, $FF \approx 0.75$, $\eta \approx 15\text{–}22\%$.*

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A silicon photodiode ($\eta = 0.85$, $\lambda = 900$ nm) receives $P_{opt} = 5$ µW of optical power. Calculate the photocurrent and verify the responsivity.

**Given:**
- $\eta = 0.85$
- $\lambda = 900 \times 10^{-9}$ m
- $P_{opt} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ W
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

**Step 1 — Compute responsivity.**

$$R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \cdot e \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R_\lambda &= \frac{0.85 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 900 \times 10^{-9}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}} \\
&= \frac{1.2255 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} \\
&\approx 0.616 \; \text{A/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute photocurrent.**

$$I_p = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt} = 0.616 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{\; I_p \approx 3.08 \; \mu\text{A} \;}$$

*This is a typical expected output — KTU often asks for verification of $I_p$ in the microampere range for such inputs.*

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Light Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Light Sensor] --> B[Photoemissive]
    A --> C[Photoconductive]
    A --> D[Photovoltaic]
    A --> E[Photojunction]
    B --> B1[Phototube PMT]
    C --> C1[LDR CdS]
    C --> C2[Photoconductive Cell]
    D --> D1[Solar Cell]
    D --> D2[Photodiode Zero Bias]
    E --> E1[Photodiode Reverse Bias]
    E --> E2[Phototransistor]
    E --> E3[CCD CMOS Image Sensor]
```

## 4.2 Photodiode Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Incident Photons] --> ABS[Absorption Region Depletion Zone]
    ABS --> GEN[Electron Hole Pair Generation]
    GEN --> SEP[Electric Field Separates Carriers]
    SEP --> AMP[Amplifier Stage]
    AMP --> OUT[Output Current I_p]
    BIAS[Reverse Bias Voltage] --> ABS
    DARK[Dark Current Component] --> OUT
    OUT --> SIG[Signal Conditioning Circuit]
```

## 4.3 Light Sensor Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Application Requirement] --> Q1{Need High Speed?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> P1[Use Photodiode]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Need High Sensitivity?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> P2[Use Phototransistor]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need Power Generation?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P3[Use Photovoltaic Cell]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Need Simple Low Cost?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> P4[Use LDR Photoresistor]
    Q4 -- No --> P5[Use CCD or CMOS Array]
```

## 4.4 Spectral Response Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UV[UV Region] --> VIS[Visible 400 700 nm]
    VIS --> IR[Infrared Region]
    IR --> MIC[Microwave Region]
    LDRx[LDR Peak ~550 nm] -.-> VIS
    SIX[Silicon Detector Peak ~900 nm] -.-> IR
    GEX[Germanium Detector Peak ~1500 nm] -.-> IR
    INX[InGaAs Detector Peak ~1700 nm] -.-> IR
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a light sensor. List any four types of light sensors with one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A light sensor is a device that converts incident optical radiation into a measurable electrical signal using the photoelectric, photoconductive, or photovoltaic effect.

| Type | Application |
|------|-------------|
| Photoresistor (LDR) | Automatic streetlight control |
| Photodiode | Optical fiber communication receiver |
| Phototransistor | IR remote control receiver |
| Photovoltaic Cell | Solar power generation |

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks table = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term *quantum efficiency* of a photodetector. State its typical range for a silicon photodiode.**

**Model Answer:**
Quantum efficiency ($\eta$) is defined as the ratio of the number of charge carriers (electrons) collected by the detector to the number of incident photons, per unit time:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Number of electrons collected/sec}}{\text{Number of incident photons/sec}}$$

For a high-quality silicon photodiode, $\eta$ typically lies in the range **0.6 to 0.9 (60% to 90%)** at its peak wavelength (~900 nm).

*(1.5 marks definition + 1.5 marks typical value = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **photodiode** with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for its **responsivity** in terms of quantum efficiency. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the operation of a **phototransistor**. Compare its performance with a photodiode. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(a):**

**Working Principle of Photodiode:**

A photodiode is a PN-junction semiconductor device operated under reverse bias. The depletion region formed at the junction contains a strong built-in electric field. When photons of energy $E \geq E_g$ strike the depletion region, electron-hole pairs are generated. The electric field immediately sweeps the electrons toward the N-side and the holes toward the P-side, producing a photocurrent $I_p$ in the reverse direction. The total current through the device is:

$$I = I_{dark} - I_p$$

where $I_{dark}$ is the small reverse leakage current present even without illumination.

**Derivation of Responsivity:**

Let the incident optical power be $P_{opt}$ at wavelength $\lambda$. The photon flux (number of photons per second) is:

$$N_{ph} = \frac{P_{opt}}{E_{photon}} = \frac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

If quantum efficiency is $\eta$, the number of electrons generated per second is:

$$N_{e} = \eta \cdot N_{ph} = \frac{\eta \cdot P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

Each electron carries charge $e$, so the photocurrent is:

$$I_p = N_e \cdot e = \frac{\eta \cdot e \cdot \lambda \cdot P_{opt}}{h \cdot c}$$

Comparing with $I_p = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$:

$$\boxed{\; R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \cdot e \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c} \; \text{A/W} \;}$$

*(3 marks working + 4 marks derivation = 7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(b):**

**Phototransistor Operation:**

A phototransistor integrates a photodiode at its base–collector junction with a BJT amplifier. Photogenerated carriers at the base–collector junction act as the base current $I_B = I_p$. This base current is then amplified by the transistor's current gain $\beta$ to produce a much larger collector current:

$$I_C = (\beta + 1) \cdot I_p$$

The device is typically operated with only two external leads (collector and emitter), with the base acting as a light-sensitive input.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Photodiode | Phototransistor |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Sensitivity | Low (µA range) | High (mA range) |
| Response Time | Fast (ns) | Slower (µs) |
| Gain | Unity | $\beta$ (100–1000) |
| Output Current | Small | Amplified |
| Application | High-speed comms, instrumentation | Object detection, optocouplers |

*(3 marks operation + 2 marks block diagram + 2 marks comparison = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **photoconductive effect** with reference to an LDR. Discuss its V–I characteristics and a typical application circuit. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **photovoltaic (solar) cell**. Define **fill factor** and **conversion efficiency**, and state the typical values for a commercial silicon cell. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(a):**

**Photoconductive Effect and LDR:**

The photoconductive effect is the phenomenon in which the electrical conductivity of a semiconductor material increases when exposed to light of suitable wavelength. In an LDR (Light Dependent Resistor), a thin film of cadmium sulfide (CdS) is deposited on a ceramic substrate with interdigitated electrodes.

When photons of energy $E \geq E_g$ (≈ 2.42 eV for CdS) strike the CdS film, valence electrons are excited into the conduction band, generating additional charge carriers. This reduces the resistance from ~1 MΩ (darkness) to a few hundred ohms (bright light).

**V–I Characteristics:**
The V–I curve of an LDR is **ohmic** (linear) at a fixed illumination level — current is directly proportional to applied voltage, following Ohm's law. However, the slope of the V–I line changes with light intensity: more light → steeper slope → lower resistance.

**Resistance vs Illuminance:**

$$R = A \cdot L^{-\gamma}$$

where $\gamma$ ≈ 0.7–0.9.

**Application Circuit — Automatic Streetlight Control:**

The LDR forms a voltage divider with a fixed resistor $R_S$. The voltage at the junction is fed to a comparator that switches ON a relay (and thus the streetlight) when ambient light falls below a preset threshold.

*(2 marks effect + 2 marks V–I + 3 marks circuit = 7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(b):**

**Photovoltaic Cell Working:**

A photovoltaic cell is a large-area PN-junction diode. When sunlight strikes the cell, photons generate electron-hole pairs in and around the depletion region. The built-in electric field separates these carriers, with electrons flowing to the N-side and holes to the P-side. This produces a voltage across the terminals — the cell acts as a **current source** in parallel with a forward-biased diode.

**V–I Equation of a Solar Cell:**

$$I = I_{SC} - I_0 \left( e^{qV/kT} - 1 \right)$$

where $I_{SC}$ is the light-generated current, $I_0$ is the diode saturation current, $q$ is electron charge, $k$ is Boltzmann's constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

**Fill Factor (FF):**

$$FF = \frac{V_{MP} \cdot I_{MP}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$$

**Conversion Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{cell} = \frac{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$$

**Typical Values for Commercial Silicon Cell:**
- Open-circuit voltage: $V_{OC} \approx 0.6$ V
- Short-circuit current density: ~35 mA/cm²
- Fill Factor: $FF \approx 0.7 - 0.8$
- Efficiency: $\eta \approx 15\% - 22\%$

*(2 marks diagram + 2 marks V–I equation + 3 marks FF and η = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $R_\lambda$ (responsivity) with $R$ (resistance)** — these are entirely different quantities.
> 2. **Units trap:** Quantum efficiency is dimensionless. Responsivity is A/W. Do not interchange units.
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign** in photodiode current $I = I_{dark} - I_p$ costs 1 mark.
> 4. **In LDR circuits**, students often draw the LDR as the lower resistor incorrectly — its position depends on whether the design is light-activate or dark-activate.
> 5. **For solar cells, do not state $V_{OC} > 1$ V** for silicon — typical is 0.55–0.65 V per cell. Modules have higher voltages due to series connection.
> 6. **Always mention $E \geq E_g$** when discussing the photoelectric threshold — KTU examiners specifically test this boundary condition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Light sensors** convert optical radiation into electrical signals via the **photoelectric**, **photoconductive**, or **photovoltaic** effect.
- ✅ The four major types are: **LDR, Photodiode, Phototransistor, Photovoltaic Cell**.
- ✅ **LDR resistance drops** with light intensity following $R = A \cdot L^{-\gamma}$.
- ✅ **Photodiode** is reverse-biased for linear response; the photocurrent $I_p = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$.
- ✅ **Responsivity** $R_\lambda = \dfrac{\eta e \lambda}{hc}$ — increases with wavelength up to cutoff $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$.
- ✅ **Quantum efficiency** $\eta$ is dimensionless; ranges 0.6–0.9 for good Si photodiodes.
- ✅ **Phototransistor** = photodiode + BJT amplifier; current $I_C = (\beta + 1) I_p$.
- ✅ **Solar cell parameters:** $V_{OC}$, $I_{SC}$, $FF$, and $\eta_{cell}$ — typical Si cell: $V_{OC} \approx 0.6$ V, $FF \approx 0.75$, $\eta \approx 15\text{–}22\%$.
- ✅ **Photon energy threshold:** $E = h\nu \geq E_g$ is mandatory for photogeneration.
- ✅ **Speed ranking (fastest → slowest):** Photodiode → Phototransistor → Photovoltaic cell → LDR.
- ✅ **Spectral range of silicon:** ~400 nm to ~1100 nm, peak around 850–900 nm.
- ✅ **Standard units:** lux (illuminance), lumen (luminous flux), W/m² (irradiance), nm (wavelength).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Sensor with Thermistor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temperature Sensor with Thermistor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Temperature Sensor with Thermistor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **THERMISTOR (Thermal Resistor)** — A **thermistor** is a passive, two-terminal solid-state semiconductor device whose electrical **resistance is highly sensitive to temperature**. The word is a portmanteau of *thermal* and *resistor*. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a thermistor used as a temperature sensor exploits the predictable, non-linear inverse (or sometimes direct) variation of resistance with absolute temperature to transduce thermal energy into a measurable electrical quantity.

In the 2024 syllabus, thermistors are classified as **resistive temperature transducers** under the broader category of **contact-type temperature sensors** (alongside RTDs and thermocouples). Unlike RTDs that use pure metals (Platinum, Nickel, Copper) where resistance change is small and linear, thermistors use **semiconductor ceramic materials** (oxides of Manganese, Nickel, Cobalt, Iron) exhibiting large but highly non-linear resistance changes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **crowded staircase at a railway station** during different times of the day:
- **During peak hours (High Temperature):** Everyone is restless, pushing, moving fast — they pass through quickly with little "resistance." The staircase acts like a **low-resistance** path.
- **During off-hours (Low Temperature):** People are calm, slow, and they tend to "stick" or cluster in groups. The staircase appears "resistant" to flow.

> This is exactly how a **thermistor behaves**! At high temperatures, the charge carriers (electrons/holes) inside the semiconductor gain energy, move more freely, and **resistance drops**. At low temperatures, carriers are sluggish, and **resistance rises**. The thermistor is essentially a "thermally-controlled valve" for electric current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The two main classes of thermistors asked in the examination are:
> 1. **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient)** — Resistance *decreases* as temperature *increases* (most common).
> 2. **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient)** — Resistance *increases* as temperature *increases* (used in resettable fuses and overcurrent protection).
>
> Standard metric to remember: **β (Beta constant)** typically lies between **3000 K to 5000 K** for NTC thermistors.

### 1.3 Classification of Thermistors

| Parameter | NTC Thermistor | PTC Thermistor |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature Coefficient | **Negative** | **Positive** |
| Material | Sintered metal oxides (Mn, Ni, Co) | Barium titanate (BaTiO₃) doped |
| Typical Range | –55 °C to +150 °C (extended to 300 °C) | 60 °C to 180 °C (switching-type) |
| Sensitivity | ~ –3 % to –6 % per °C | Highly non-linear (sharp switch) |
| Primary Use | Precise temperature measurement | Self-regulating heaters, overcurrent protection |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — The Physical "Why"

The physics behind a thermistor rests on **semiconductor band theory**:

1. **At Absolute Zero (theoretical)**: Charge carriers in the semiconductor valence band are "frozen." Very few can jump to the conduction band, so the material is almost an **insulator** with extremely high resistance.
2. **As temperature rises**: Thermal energy excites electrons across the band gap. Carrier concentration increases **exponentially** (not linearly!).
3. **Result**: Conductivity (σ) increases exponentially, meaning **resistance decreases exponentially** as temperature rises.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This exponential behavior is the **single most important concept** for KTU exams — the relationship is fundamentally **non-linear**, which is why linearization circuits are often required in practice.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) and internal assessments.

**Table: Master Formula Reference for Thermistors**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Steinhart–Hart Equation** (Most accurate) | $\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C [\ln(R)]^3$ | $T$ in Kelvin, $R$ in Ω, $A, B, C$ are device constants |
| 2 | **Beta (β) Parameter Equation** (Most used) | $R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta \!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | $R_0$ = resistance at reference temp $T_0$ (usually 298.15 K / 25 °C) |
| 3 | **Simplified NTC form** | $R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta / T}$ | When $T_0 \to \infty$ limit |
| 4 | **Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR)** | $\alpha = \frac{1}{R} \frac{dR}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$ | α in /K (per Kelvin). Negative for NTC |
| 5 | **Sensitivity** | $S = \frac{dR}{dT} = -\frac{\beta \cdot R(T)}{T^2}$ | Magnitude of resistance change per °C |
| 6 | **Voltage Divider Output** | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{th}}{R_{th} + R_s}$ | $R_{th}$ = thermistor, $R_s$ = series resistor |
| 7 | **Wheatstone Bridge Balance** | $R_{th} = R_1 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}$ | At balance, $V_{out} = 0$ |
| 8 | **Self-Heating Power** | $P = I^2 \cdot R = \frac{V^2}{R}$ | Causes measurement error; $P_{max}$ specified by manufacturer |
| 9 | **Thermal Dissipation Constant** | $\delta = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta T}$ | Power (mW) needed to raise thermistor 1 °C above ambient |
| 10 | **Time Constant (τ)** | $\tau = \frac{C_{th}}{\delta}$ | $C_{th}$ = thermal capacitance; τ in seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL: Do NOT use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside LaTeX expressions in KTU answer sheets — KTU's online valuer scripts (and any markdown renderer) can mis-parse it. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value if needed.**

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive**: Engine coolant temperature, intake air temperature, oil temperature.
- **Medical**: Infant incubators, digital thermometers, dialysis machines.
- **Consumer Electronics**: Battery packs (NTC for charge protection), 3D printer hot-ends, coffee machines.
- **Industrial**: HVAC duct sensors, process control loops, fire alarm trigger circuits.
- **IoT & Wearables**: Low-power skin/body temperature patches.
- **Inrush Current Limiters (NTC)**: Televisions, SMPS — cold resistance limits startup surge.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Beta Parameter Equation (KTU Hot Topic)

**Starting Point:** For an NTC thermistor, conductivity is governed by thermal activation of charge carriers. The carrier concentration $n$ follows the Arrhenius-type law:

$$n(T) \propto e^{-E_g / 2k_B T}$$

Since conductivity $\sigma = n \cdot q \cdot (\mu_n + \mu_p)$ and assuming mobility is weakly temperature dependent compared to the exponential term, we can write:

$$\sigma(T) \propto e^{-E_g / 2k_B T}$$

Taking the reciprocal to get resistivity (and thus resistance, assuming geometry is constant):

$$R(T) \propto e^{+E_g / 2k_B T}$$

**Step 1:** Define a constant $\beta = \dfrac{E_g}{2k_B}$ (in Kelvin). Then:

$$R(T) = C \cdot e^{\beta / T}$$

where $C$ is a constant of proportionality that depends on the geometry and material properties.

**Step 2:** Evaluate at a reference temperature $T_0$ (typically 25 °C = 298.15 K):

$$R_0 = C \cdot e^{\beta / T_0}$$

**Step 3:** Divide the two equations to eliminate $C$:

$$\frac{R(T)}{R_0} = \frac{e^{\beta / T}}{e^{\beta / T_0}} = e^{\beta (1/T - 1/T_0)}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange into the standard textbook form:

$$\boxed{\,R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]\,}$$

This is the **Beta Parameter Equation** — the most commonly tested form in KTU exams.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR)

Starting with the simplified form $R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta/T}$ (assuming $T_0 \to \infty$ for simplicity):

**Step 1:** Take the natural log of both sides:

$$\ln R = \ln R_0 + \frac{\beta}{T}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to temperature $T$:

$$\frac{1}{R} \frac{dR}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

**Step 3:** By definition, the **Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR)** is:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{R} \frac{dR}{dT}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,\alpha = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}\,}$$

> The negative sign confirms that for an NTC thermistor, resistance decreases as temperature increases. Numerically, if $\beta = 4000\,\text{K}$ and $T = 298\,\text{K}$:

$$\alpha = -\frac{4000}{(298)^2} \approx -0.0450 \,\text{K}^{-1} = -4.50\%/\text{K}$$

Compare this to a Platinum RTD (PT100), which has $\alpha \approx +0.00385\,/\text{K}$ — roughly **12× smaller** in magnitude, illustrating why thermistors are far more sensitive.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

**Problem:** An NTC thermistor has a resistance of $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ at 25 °C and a Beta constant of 3950 K. Calculate its resistance at 100 °C.

**Given:**
- $R_0 = 10\,000\;\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25\,°\text{C} = 298.15\,\text{K}$
- $\beta = 3950\,\text{K}$
- $T = 100\,°\text{C} = 373.15\,\text{K}$

**Step 1:** Compute the temperature-difference term:

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}$$

$$\frac{1}{373.15} = 2.6800 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{298.15} = 3.3540 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\text{Difference} = 2.6800 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} = -6.740 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by $\beta$:

$$\beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) = 3950 \times (-6.740 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.6623$$

**Step 3:** Apply the exponent:

$$\exp(-2.6623) = 0.0700$$

**Step 4:** Final resistance:

$$R(100\,°\text{C}) = 10\,000 \times 0.0700 = 700\;\Omega$$

**Verification:** $\dfrac{700}{10\,000} = 0.07$. The thermistor's resistance dropped from 10 kΩ to just 700 Ω as temperature rose by 75 °C — a ~93 % drop, vividly demonstrating the high sensitivity of thermistors.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thermistor Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermal Energy Input - Temperature Change] --> B[Semiconductor Ceramic Element - Mn-Ni Oxide]
    B --> C[Change in Carrier Concentration - Exponential]
    C --> D[Change in Electrical Resistance - R drops for NTC]
    D --> E[Signal Conditioning Circuit - Voltage Divider / Bridge]
    E --> F[Linearization Stage - Look-up Table / Op-Amp]
    F --> G[ADC Conversion]
    G --> H[Microcontroller / Display Output]
    D --> I[Self-Heating Check - P equals I squared R]
    I --> J{Within Specs}
    J -->|Yes| K[Accurate Reading]
    J -->|No| L[Measurement Error - Apply Correction]
```

### 4.2 Voltage Divider Interface Circuit (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC[Vcc - Reference Voltage] --> RS[Rs - Fixed Series Resistor]
    RS --> NODE1[Junction Node - Vout]
    RTH[Thermistor - NTC] --> NODE1
    NODE1 --> GND[GND - Ground]
    NODE1 --> ADC[ADC Input - Microcontroller]
    ADC --> MCU[Compute Temperature - Using Beta Eq.]
    MCU --> DISP[Display / Transmit Result]
```

### 4.3 Wheatstone Bridge Configuration (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VIN[Excitation Voltage - Vex] --> NA[Node A]
    VIN --> NB[Node B]
    NA --> R1[R1 - Fixed]
    R1 --> NC[Node C - Vout positive]
    NA --> RTH[Rth - Thermistor]
    RTH --> ND[Node D - Vout negative]
    NB --> R2[R2 - Fixed]
    R2 --> ND
    NB --> R3[R3 - Variable - Balance]
    R3 --> NC
    NC --> AMP[Differential Amplifier]
    ND --> AMP
    AMP --> OUT[Calibrated Output Voltage]
```

### 4.4 Resistance vs. Temperature Characteristic Curve (ASCII Schematic)

```
   R (Ω)
   │
10k├─●
   │  ╲
   │   ╲
   │    ╲
 5k├─────●─
   │      ╲
   │       ╲
   │        ╲
 1k├─────────●──
   │          ╲___
   │              ●───────
100├────────────────────●────────
   └────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬────► T (°C)
        0   25   50   75  100  125
            ↑
        Reference T0 = 25°C
       (NTC: exponential decay)
```

> The above schematic shows the **classic NTC exponential decay** of resistance with temperature — note how steep the curve is at low temperatures, which is why NTC thermistors are extremely sensitive in the cold range.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** Differentiate between NTC and PTC thermistors. Give two applications of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | NTC | PTC |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient | Negative | Positive |
| Material | Mn, Ni, Co oxides | BaTiO₃ doped with lanthanum/ytterbium |
| Behavior | Resistance ↓ as T ↑ | Resistance ↑ sharply above Curie point |
| Apps | Digital thermometers, inrush current limiters, battery monitoring | Self-resetting fuses, motor overload protection, degaussing circuits |

*(3 marks awarded for table + correct applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** Define the term "Beta constant" of a thermistor. What is its typical range?

**Model Answer:**
The **Beta constant (β)** is a material-specific parameter (in Kelvin) that characterizes the slope of the resistance–temperature curve of an NTC thermistor. It appears in the equation:

$$R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

Higher β means greater sensitivity. **Typical range: 3000 K to 5000 K** for most commercial NTC thermistors.

---

### 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of an NTC thermistor. Derive the expression for its temperature coefficient of resistance.

**(b) [7 Marks)** An NTC thermistor has $\beta = 4000\,\text{K}$ and $R_{25} = 8\,\text{k}\Omega$. Determine (i) its resistance at 80 °C, and (ii) its TCR at 50 °C.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Construction:**
- **Body**: A small bead, disc, rod, or chip of sintered semiconductor oxide (typically a mixture of Mn₃O₄, NiO, CoO, Fe₂O₃).
- **Leads**: Two metal lead wires (Dumet or platinum) embedded into the body for electrical contact.
- **Encapsulation**: Glass coating, epoxy resin, or stainless-steel probe for environmental protection.
- **Surface-mount (SMD) variants** also exist for PCB use.

**Working Principle:**
1. The semiconductor ceramic has many charge carriers trapped in the valence band at low T.
2. As temperature rises, thermal energy excites electrons across the band gap.
3. Carrier concentration increases **exponentially** with T.
4. Conductivity rises; resistance **falls** — this is the NTC effect.

**Derivation of TCR (covered in Section 3.2):**
Starting from $R(T) = R_0 e^{\beta/T}$, taking $\ln$ and differentiating:

$$\frac{1}{R}\frac{dR}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \alpha = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $\beta = 4000\,\text{K}$, $R_0 = 8000\;\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298.15\,\text{K}$, $T = 80\,°\text{C} = 353.15\,\text{K}$, and for (ii) $T = 50\,°\text{C} = 323.15\,\text{K}$.

**(i) Resistance at 80 °C:**

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{353.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 2.8317 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= -5.223 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) = 4000 \times (-5.223 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.0892$$

$$R(80\,°\text{C}) = 8000 \times e^{-2.0892} = 8000 \times 0.1237 = 989.6\;\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R(80\,°\text{C}) \approx 990\;\Omega}$$

**(ii) TCR at 50 °C:**

$$\alpha = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} = -\frac{4000}{(323.15)^2} = -\frac{4000}{104\,426} = -0.0383\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx -3.83\%/\text{K}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the Wheatstone bridge interface circuit used with a thermistor for precision temperature measurement. Explain the role of self-heating error and how it can be minimized.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 10 kΩ NTC thermistor is used in a voltage divider with $R_s = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$. The thermistor is rated at $\delta = 2\,\text{mW/°C}$ (thermal dissipation constant) and $\beta = 3435\,\text{K}$. Calculate the self-heating error when ambient temperature is 25 °C.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Wheatstone Bridge Interface:**

The thermistor is placed in one arm of a four-arm bridge. The other three arms contain precision fixed resistors ($R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$). The bridge is excited by a stable DC voltage $V_{ex}$. Output is taken differentially.

At balance: $V_{out} = 0$ when $\dfrac{R_{th}}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$. A small change in $R_{th}$ due to temperature produces a differential voltage proportional to temperature change.

**Self-Heating Error:**
Power dissipated in the thermistor ($P = I^2 R_{th} = V^2 / R_{th}$) raises its own temperature above ambient. This creates a measurement offset.

**Minimization strategies:**
- Use pulsed excitation (turn $V_{ex}$ ON briefly, then OFF).
- Keep excitation voltage low.
- Use the largest possible reference resistor to limit current.
- Thermally couple the thermistor to a heat sink (probe-type designs).

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $R_{th} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ at 25 °C, $R_s = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$, $\delta = 2\,\text{mW/°C}$.

**Step 1: Voltage divider output current:**

The current through the divider is:

$$I = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_s + R_{th}} = \frac{5}{10\,000 + 10\,000} = 0.25\,\text{mA}$$

**Step 2: Power dissipated in thermistor:**

$$P_{th} = I^2 \cdot R_{th} = (0.25 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 10\,000 = 6.25 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{W} = 0.625\,\text{mW}$$

**Step 3: Self-heating temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{P_{th}}{\delta} = \frac{0.625\,\text{mW}}{2\,\text{mW/°C}} = 0.3125\,°\text{C}$$

**Step 4: Convert to resistance error using TCR:**

At 25 °C = 298.15 K, with $\beta = 3435\,\text{K}$:

$$\alpha = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} = -\frac{3435}{(298.15)^2} = -0.03865\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta R = \alpha \cdot R \cdot \Delta T = -0.03865 \times 10\,000 \times 0.3125 \approx -120.8\;\Omega$$

$$\boxed{\text{Self-heating error} \approx +0.31\,°\text{C} \;\;(\text{resistance drops by } 121\;\Omega)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Temperature unit confusion** — The Beta equation uses **Kelvin**, not Celsius. Forgetting to convert is the #1 mark-loser. Always add 273.15.
> 2. **Sign errors in TCR** — α is **negative** for NTC. Examiners award 1 mark just for the correct sign.
> 3. **Forgetting self-heating analysis** — When asked "design considerations," students routinely skip self-heating. This costs 2 marks easily.
> 4. **Mixing up Steinhart-Hart with Beta equation** — The three-constant Steinhart-Hart is more accurate but is usually only asked in 14-mark questions. The Beta (two-point) form is the default for 3 to 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Wrong units for sensitivity** — Sensitivity S = dR/dT has units of **Ω/K**, not Ω alone.
> 6. **Drawing thermistor as a variable resistor without a temperature symbol** — Always mark the temperature dependence with a small "T" or arrow on the symbol.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> - **Definition**: Thermistor = Thermal Resistor; semiconductor oxide device; resistance varies strongly with T.
> - **Two types**: NTC (R ↓ as T ↑) and PTC (R ↑ as T ↑).
> - **Beta Equation (Master Formula)**: $R(T) = R_0 \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$ — always in **Kelvin**.
> - **TCR**: $\alpha = -\beta / T^2$ (negative for NTC; magnitude ~ 3–6 %/K).
> - **Steinhart-Hart** (3-constant): more accurate, used in high-precision digital systems.
> - **Self-heating** is a parasitic effect; minimize using low excitation, pulsed current, or heat-sinked probes.
> - **Thermal dissipation constant δ** (mW/°C) and **time constant τ** (seconds) are key spec parameters.
> - **Voltage divider** is the most common interface; **Wheatstone bridge** for precision.
> - **Sensitivity** of thermistor is ~ 10× greater than RTD but with severe non-linearity.
> - **Materials**: NTC → Mn, Ni, Co, Fe oxides; PTC → doped BaTiO₃.
> - **Range**: typically –55 °C to +150 °C (NTC), 60–180 °C (PTC switching).
> - **Applications to recall**: inrush current limiting, battery monitoring, medical thermometry, 3D-printer hot-ends.
> - **Comparison must-know**: Thermistor vs RTD vs Thermocouple — sensitivity, linearity, range, cost, self-heating.
> - **Linearization methods**: parallel/series resistor, look-up table, Steinhart-Hart polynomial, op-amp piecewise circuits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Voltage Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ VOLTAGE SENSOR — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Sensor:** A voltage sensor is a transducer-based electronic device that detects, measures, conditions, and converts an input voltage (DC or AC) into a proportional, standardized electrical output signal — typically a low-level analog voltage (e.g., 0–5 V, 0–10 V), a current loop signal (4–20 mA), or a digital value — suitable for further processing by a microcontroller, PLC, data acquisition system (DAQ), or protective relay.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme context, a voltage sensor is classified under **Signal-Conditioning & Power-Transduction Sensors**. It does **not** merely measure voltage like a passive voltmeter; it actively transforms a potentially hazardous, high-energy, or floating potential into a **galvanically isolated, attenuated, and scaled** representation of that voltage. The output of a voltage sensor must satisfy three cardinal engineering properties:

1. **Proportionality** — Output $V_{out} = K \cdot V_{in} + C$, where $K$ is the scaling constant and $C$ is a calibration offset.
2. **Galvanic Isolation** — The output side is electrically separated from the input side, typically rated at **2.5 kV to 5 kV RMS isolation voltage** for industrial sensors.
3. **Bandwidth & Linearity** — The sensor must respond within a specified frequency range (e.g., DC to **100 kHz**) with a linearity error typically under $\pm 0.1\%$ of Full Scale (FS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from a Voltmeter:** A voltmeter is a *display instrument* whose input impedance is the only loading concern. A voltage sensor is a *transduction device* designed to interface with downstream electronics, so it must provide isolation, scaling, and a low-impedance, noise-immune output.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the **deck of a large ship** in a violent storm, and you need to read the **height of a massive ocean wave** to your colleague standing safely **inside the ship's bridge** (an instrumented room).

- The **ocean wave** is the **high-voltage signal** — powerful, dangerous, and too large to be directly displayed.
- **You** are the **voltage sensor** — you measure the wave's height, scale it down (you do not send the actual wave!), and report a proportional value (e.g., "the wave is 3 m tall" rather than letting the wave itself crash into the bridge).
- The **bridge** is the **microcontroller/DAQ** — it receives a safe, scaled, proportional reading of the wave's height.
- The **closed door between you and the bridge** is **galvanic isolation** — even if the storm (voltage spike) overwhelms you, the bridge remains safe.

This is *exactly* what a voltage sensor does: it **safely observes** an electrical quantity, **scales** it, and **delivers** a safe replica to the controller.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Specifications (In Bold)

The following parameters are universally used to characterize voltage sensors in industrial datasheets and KTU exam problems:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Input Voltage** | $V_{in,nom}$ | **24 V, 110 V, 230 V, 400 V, 600 V AC/DC** |
| **Nominal Output Voltage** | $V_{out,nom}$ | **0–5 V, 0–10 V, $\pm$10 V** |
| **Output Current Loop** | $I_{out}$ | **4–20 mA** |
| **Accuracy Class** | — | **0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0** (Class 0.5 = $\pm 0.5\%$ FS) |
| **Bandwidth** | $f_{BW}$ | **DC to 10 kHz** (typical) |
| **Galvanic Isolation** | $V_{iso}$ | **2.5 kV / 4 kV / 5 kV RMS** (50 Hz, 1 min) |
| **Response Time** | $t_{r}$ | **< 1 ms** (fast), **< 100 ms** (average) |
| **Input Impedance** | $Z_{in}$ | **> 1 M$\Omega$** (high) |
| **Operating Temperature** | $T_{op}$ | **$-25^\circ$C to $+70^\circ$C** (industrial) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question says *"a voltage sensor has an accuracy class of 0.5 and a range of 0–500 V"*, the maximum permissible error is $\pm 0.5\%$ of 500 V = **$\pm 2.5$ V**. Always show this full calculation step in your answer.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Voltage Sensors

Voltage sensors are broadly classified based on their **transduction principle**:

1. **Resistive Voltage Divider (RVD) Sensors** — Passive, low-cost, used for DC and low-frequency AC.
2. **Capacitive Voltage Divider Sensors** — Used in **Medium-Voltage (MV) and High-Voltage (HV)** transmission line monitoring.
3. **Hall-Effect Voltage Sensors** — Use the magnetic field produced by a current proportional to the voltage; provide inherent isolation.
4. **Current-Transformer (CT) / Voltage-Transformer (VT / PT) Based Sensors** — Use magnetic coupling for AC voltage measurement; provide intrinsic galvanic isolation.
5. **Opto-isolated / Fiber-Optic Voltage Sensors** — Use the Pockels effect (electro-optic modulation) for ultra-high-voltage and EMI-immune applications.
6. **Active Electronic Sensors (Operational Amplifier Based)** — Use active op-amp circuits (differential amplifiers) for precision low-voltage measurement.
7. **Divider-Plus-Isolation-Amplifier Sensors** — Industry-standard modern sensors combining RVD + isolation amplifier (e.g., based on AD202, ISO124).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes **Resistive Voltage Dividers**, **Capacitive Dividers**, **Hall-Effect Voltage Sensors**, and the **mathematical analysis of attenuation, loading error, and isolation principles**. These are the high-yield topics for ESE and internal assessments.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Generic Block Architecture

Every voltage sensor, regardless of its underlying transduction physics, can be decomposed into **four universal functional stages**:

1. **Input Stage (Attenuation / Sensing Element)** — Receives the raw, often hazardous, input voltage and produces a scaled, lower-magnitude replica. The most common realization is a **resistive voltage divider** (RVD) or **capacitive divider**.
2. **Isolation Stage** — Galvanically separates the high-voltage input side from the low-voltage output side. Realizations include **opto-couplers**, **isolation amplifiers** (e.g., AD202/AD210), **Hall-effect isolation**, or **magnetic CT/VT coupling**.
3. **Signal Conditioning Stage** — Performs amplification, filtering, level shifting, and linearization. Active op-amp circuits (differential amplifiers, instrumentation amplifiers like INA128) are dominant.
4. **Output Stage** — Delivers a standardized, low-impedance signal. Outputs include **0–10 V**, **4–20 mA**, **0–5 V**, **RS-485 / Modbus digital**, or **PWM**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why isolation is non-negotiable in industrial voltage sensors:**
> - Protects the **microcontroller / PLC** from over-voltage transients, surges, and ground loops.
> - Rejects **common-mode voltages** that can be thousands of volts in motor drives and power inverters.
> - Required for **personnel safety** and compliance with **IEC 61010-1** and **IEC 61850** standards.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of the Resistive Voltage Divider (RVD) Sensor

The **Resistive Voltage Divider** is the heart of most low- and medium-voltage DC sensors. It is governed by the classic voltage divider rule, but in a sensor context, we must carefully account for **loading effects** of the downstream stage.

### 2.2.1 Ideal (Unloaded) Divider

Consider a simple two-resistor series circuit. Let the input voltage be $V_{in}$ applied across $R_1$ and $R_2$ in series, with the output taken across $R_2$.

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Define the **Attenuation Factor** $K$ (also called the **Divider Ratio** or **Scaling Factor**):

$$K = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

For a sensor designed to measure up to 500 V and output 0–5 V, we require $K = 5/500 = 0.01$. A common realization: $R_1 = 990 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.

### 2.2.2 Loaded Divider — The Loading Error

In a real sensor, the output is connected to a finite **load impedance** $R_L$ (e.g., the input impedance of an op-amp, ADC, or measurement instrument). The output voltage becomes:

$$V_{out,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2 \parallel R_L}{R_1 + (R_2 \parallel R_L)}$$

where $R_2 \parallel R_L = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L}$.

The **Loading Error** (also called **Attenuation Error** $\varepsilon_{L}$) is defined as:

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{V_{out,ideal} - V_{out,loaded}}{V_{out,ideal}} \times 100\%$$

This is a **systematic negative error** — the loaded output is always *less* than the ideal output. To minimize it, we use the design rule: $R_L \geq 100 \cdot R_2$.

### 2.2.3 Power Dissipation in RVD Sensors

The total input power is $P_{in} = \frac{V_{in}^2}{R_1 + R_2}$. For high-voltage sensors, this is non-trivial. Example: for $V_{in} = 500$ V and $R_1 + R_2 = 1$ M$\Omega$, $P_{in} = 0.25$ W — tolerable. But for $V_{in} = 5$ kV with the same divider, $P_{in} = 25$ W — impractical. Hence high-voltage RVDs are impractical and replaced by capacitive dividers or VT-based sensors.

---

## 2.3 Capacitive Voltage Divider (CVD) Sensor — HV Transmission Line Monitoring

For voltages above **~1 kV**, resistive dividers become impractical due to power dissipation and self-heating. **Capacitive dividers** use the AC reactance of capacitors to perform the same function. The capacitive divider ratio is:

$$K_C = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{X_{C2}}{X_{C1} + X_{C2}} = \frac{1/\omega C_2}{1/\omega C_1 + 1/\omega C_2} = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$$

Note that for capacitive dividers, the high-voltage arm is $C_1$ (small capacitance, e.g., pF range) and the low-voltage arm is $C_2$ (large capacitance, e.g., nF range). This is the *opposite* of the resistive divider convention. The reason: reactance $X_C = 1/(\omega C)$ is inversely proportional to $C$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** In a **resistive divider**, the bigger resistor is on the high-voltage side. In a **capacitive divider**, the **smaller** capacitor is on the high-voltage side.

---

## 2.4 Hall-Effect Voltage Sensor — Closed-Loop Principle

A Hall-effect voltage sensor works indirectly: it passes a current proportional to the input voltage through a primary coil, and the resulting magnetic flux is measured by a Hall element. The Hall output drives a **compensation coil** (secondary) that nulls the flux (closed-loop operation). The secondary current $I_s$ is then a precise linear image of the primary voltage $V_p$:

$$I_s = \frac{N_p}{N_s} \cdot \frac{V_p}{R_p}$$

where $N_p$ and $N_s$ are the primary and secondary turns, and $R_p$ is a precision primary resistor through which $V_p$ is converted to a primary current $V_p / R_p$. The output voltage is then $V_{out} = I_s \cdot R_{meas}$.

**Closed-loop Hall sensors** offer $\pm 0.5\%$ accuracy, very fast response (< 1 $\mu$s), and inherent galvanic isolation — they are the workhorse of modern industrial voltage sensing (e.g., LEM LV 25-P, Allegro ACS series).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** you need for KTU ESE questions on Voltage Sensors.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Resistive voltage divider output | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $K = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Divider attenuation / scaling factor | Dimensionless, $0 < K < 1$ |
| 3 | $V_{out,L} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{(R_2 \parallel R_L)}{R_1 + (R_2 \parallel R_L)}$ | Loaded divider output | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_L = \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{out,L}}{V_{out,ideal}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Loading error percentage | % |
| 5 | $P_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in}^2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Power dissipated in divider | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $K_C = \dfrac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$ | Capacitive divider ratio (AC) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Capacitive reactance | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 8 | $V_{out} = V_p \cdot \dfrac{R_{meas}}{R_p} \cdot \dfrac{N_p}{N_s}$ | Closed-loop Hall voltage sensor output | Volts (V) |
| 9 | $V_{err} = \pm \left(\dfrac{\text{Accuracy Class}}{100}\right) \times \text{Full Scale}$ | Maximum permissible error | Volts (V) |
| 10 | $f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq}}$ | Bandwidth of divider (3-dB cutoff) | Hertz (Hz) |
| 11 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_{diff}}{A_{CM}}\right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | Decibels (dB) |
| 12 | $V_{out} = K \cdot V_{in} + C$ | Generic linear transfer function | Volts (V); $C$ is offset |

> **Note on Units:** All voltages in **Volts (V)**, resistances in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**, capacitances in **Farads (F)**, frequencies in **Hertz (Hz)**, and errors in **% of Full Scale (FS)** unless otherwise stated.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Renewable Energy Systems:** DC voltage sensors in solar PV strings (0–1500 V DC) for MPPT control and string monitoring.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Battery Management Systems (BMS):** Voltage sensors on every cell (e.g., 0–5 V per Li-ion cell) for State-of-Charge (SoC) estimation.
- **Industrial Motor Drives:** DC-link voltage sensing (0–800 V DC) in VFDs for over-voltage protection.
- **Smart Grid & Substation Automation:** Capacitive voltage dividers and VT-based sensors (PTs) for 11 kV, 33 kV, 110 kV bus voltage monitoring.
- **Medical Equipment:** Isolated voltage sensing in defibrillators and patient monitoring (IEC 60601 isolation requirements).
- **Railway Traction:** 25 kV AC overhead line voltage sensing for pantograph control and protection.
- **Aerospace:** 270 V DC and 115 V AC bus voltage monitoring in more-electric aircraft (MEA).

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Loading Error of a Resistive Voltage Divider

### Problem Setup
A resistive voltage divider is built with $R_1 = 990 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ to measure an input voltage of $V_{in} = 500$ V. The output is connected to a measurement instrument with input resistance $R_L = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$. Find:
- (a) The ideal (unloaded) output voltage.
- (b) The actual (loaded) output voltage.
- (c) The percentage loading error.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the equivalent resistance of $R_2$ in parallel with $R_L$.**

The two resistances are connected in parallel because $R_L$ is the load attached across the output terminals. The equivalent resistance is:

$$R_{2L} = R_2 \parallel R_L = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$R_{2L} = \frac{(10 \times 10^3) \cdot (50 \times 10^3)}{(10 \times 10^3) + (50 \times 10^3)}$$

$$R_{2L} = \frac{500 \times 10^6}{60 \times 10^3} = 8.333 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2: Compute the ideal (unloaded) output voltage using the basic divider formula.**

In the unloaded case, the load is removed, so the output is simply across $R_2$:

$$V_{out,ideal} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_{out,ideal} = 500 \cdot \frac{10 \times 10^3}{990 \times 10^3 + 10 \times 10^3} = 500 \cdot \frac{10}{1000} = 5.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Compute the loaded output voltage.**

With the load in place, replace $R_2$ with $R_{2L}$ in the divider formula:

$$V_{out,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{2L}}{R_1 + R_{2L}}$$

$$V_{out,loaded} = 500 \cdot \frac{8.333 \times 10^3}{990 \times 10^3 + 8.333 \times 10^3} = 500 \cdot \frac{8.333}{998.333}$$

$$V_{out,loaded} = 500 \cdot 0.008347 = 4.174 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Compute the loading error as a percentage of the ideal output.**

$$\varepsilon_L = \left(1 - \frac{V_{out,loaded}}{V_{out,ideal}}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$\varepsilon_L = \left(1 - \frac{4.174}{5.000}\right) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.8347) \times 100\% = 16.53\%$$

### Verification & Engineering Interpretation

> [!WARNING]
> A **16.53% error is completely unacceptable** in any practical instrument! This is why a buffer amplifier (voltage follower using an op-amp with input impedance $> 10^{12} \Omega$) is always placed at the output of a voltage sensor's RVD. With a buffer, $R_L \to \infty$, so $R_{2L} \to R_2$, and the error vanishes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Design of a Capacitive Divider for 11 kV AC Bus Monitoring

### Problem Setup
A capacitive voltage divider is used to monitor an 11 kV, 50 Hz bus and produce a 0–110 V output for a relay. Find the required capacitance ratio. Available: $C_1$ is a high-voltage compressed-gas capacitor. Compute $C_2$ if $C_1 = 100$ pF.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: State the required attenuation factor.**

The sensor must scale 11,000 V down to 110 V. Therefore:

$$K_C = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{110}{11000} = 0.01$$

**Step 2: Apply the capacitive divider formula.**

$$K_C = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$$

Rearranging to solve for $C_2$:

$$0.01 = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2} \implies 0.01(C_1 + C_2) = C_1 \implies 0.01 C_2 = 0.99 C_1$$

$$C_2 = 99 \cdot C_1 = 99 \cdot 100 \text{ pF} = 9900 \text{ pF} = 9.9 \text{ nF}$$

**Step 3: Verify the reactances at 50 Hz to confirm the design.**

$$X_{C1} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$X_{C1} = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-8}} = 31.83 \text{ M}\Omega$$

$$X_{C2} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 9.9 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$X_{C2} = \frac{1}{3.111 \times 10^{-6}} = 321.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Verify the divider output using reactance ratio.**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{X_{C2}}{X_{C1} + X_{C2}} = 11000 \cdot \frac{321.5 \times 10^3}{31.83 \times 10^6 + 321.5 \times 10^3}$$

$$V_{out} = 11000 \cdot \frac{0.3215}{32.1515} = 11000 \cdot 0.0100 = 110 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

### Engineering Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> The output of a capacitive voltage divider **must be loaded by a very high impedance** (e.g., a buffer amplifier or electrometer) to prevent loading by the measuring instrument. Also, capacitive dividers are **not suitable for DC voltage measurement** because $X_C \to \infty$ at $f = 0$ Hz, which makes the divider pass no DC signal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Loop Hall-Effect Voltage Sensor Output

### Problem Setup
A LEM-type closed-loop Hall voltage sensor is used to measure a DC bus voltage of 600 V. The sensor has the following specifications:
- Primary resistor: $R_p = 30 \text{ k}\Omega$ (internal, converts $V_p$ to primary current)
- Primary turns: $N_p = 1$
- Secondary turns: $N_s = 1000$
- Measuring resistor (burden): $R_{meas} = 100 \Omega$

Find: The output voltage $V_{out}$ across the burden resistor.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the primary current.**

The primary current is the input voltage divided by the primary resistance:

$$I_p = \frac{V_p}{R_p} = \frac{600}{30 \times 10^3} = 0.020 \text{ A} = 20 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2: Apply the closed-loop ampere-turn balance.**

In a closed-loop Hall sensor, the ampere-turns of the primary and secondary are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction (nulling flux):

$$N_p \cdot I_p = N_s \cdot I_s$$

Solving for the secondary current:

$$I_s = \frac{N_p}{N_s} \cdot I_p = \frac{1}{1000} \cdot 0.020 = 20 \text{ }\mu\text{A}$$

**Step 3: Compute the output voltage across the burden resistor.**

$$V_{out} = I_s \cdot R_{meas} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 100 = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2.0 \text{ mV}$$

### Engineering Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> The output is only **2 mV** — too small for direct ADC interfacing. In practice, an external instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128, gain = 100) boosts it to **200 mV**, which is suitable for a 0–5 V ADC with a 1.2 V reference. This shows that **closed-loop Hall sensors always need an external conditioning stage** in real systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Voltage Sensor Types

| Property | RVD Sensor | Capacitive Divider | Hall-Effect Sensor | VT (PT) Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Best for Voltage Range** | 0–1000 V | 1 kV–500 kV | 0–6500 V | 50 V–500 kV |
| **DC Capability** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (AC only) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (AC only) |
| **Galvanic Isolation** | ❌ External needed | ❌ External needed | ✅ Inherent | ✅ Inherent |
| **Accuracy** | $\pm 0.1\%$ (with buffer) | $\pm 0.5\%$ | $\pm 0.5\%$ | $\pm 0.2$ to $\pm 1.2\%$ |
| **Bandwidth** | DC–100 kHz | 10 Hz–1 MHz | DC–100 kHz | 50/60 Hz only |
| **Power Consumption** | Low (passive) | Low (passive) | Medium (active) | Low (passive) |
| **Cost** | ₹ Low | ₹ Low–Medium | ₹₹ Medium | ₹₹₹ High |
| **Typical Use** | DC bus, BMS | HV substation | Drives, EVs | Grid metering |

# 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Block Architecture of a Voltage Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HVInput["HV Input Voltage<br>V_in up to 500 V"] --> DIVIDER["Stage 1 Attenuator<br>Resistive or Capacitive Divider<br>K = R2 / R1+R2"]
    DIVIDER --> BUFFER["Stage 2 Buffer Amplifier<br>Voltage Follower Op-Amp<br>Z_in greater than 1 TeraOhm"]
    BUFFER --> ISO["Stage 3 Galvanic Isolation<br>Isolation Amplifier or Optocoupler<br>V_iso = 2.5 kV RMS"]
    ISO --> COND["Stage 4 Signal Conditioning<br>Gain Filtering Level Shift<br>CMRR greater than 80 dB"]
    COND --> ADC["Stage 5 ADC Interface<br>0 to 5 V or 4 to 20 mA<br>or Modbus Digital Output"]
    ADC --> MCU["Microcontroller or PLC<br>or Data Acquisition System"]
    PWR["Isolated DC-DC Converter<br>Power Supply for HV Side"] -.-> BUFFER
    PWR -.-> ISO
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows represent the **signal path** (left to right). Dotted arrows represent the **auxiliary power path** (the isolated DC-DC converter provides power to the high-voltage-side active components). The five numbered stages are the universal architecture of *any* modern voltage sensor.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Schematic — Resistive Divider with Buffer, Isolation, and Instrumentation Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HV["HIGH VOLTAGE SIDE"]
        VIN["V_in"] --- R1["R1 = 990 kOhm<br>High Voltage Attenuator"]
        R1 --- R2["R2 = 10 kOhm"]
        R2 --- GND1["Ground HV"]
        BUF["Buffer Op-Amp<br>Voltage Follower<br>TL072 or OPA2188"]
        R2 --- INP["Input to Buffer"]
        BUF --- ISOIN["Output of Buffer"]
    end
    subgraph ISOLATION["GALVANIC ISOLATION BARRIER 2.5 kV"]
        ISOIN --- OPTO["Optocoupler or<br>Digital Isolator<br>ADuM1411 or HCPL-7840"]
        OPTO --- ISOMID["Isolated Signal"]
    end
    subgraph LV["LOW VOLTAGE SIDE"]
        ISOMID --- INA["Instrumentation Amplifier<br>INA128 Gain G = 5"]
        INA --- FILTER["2nd Order Low-Pass Filter<br>fc = 1 kHz"]
        FILTER --- OUTPUT["V_out = 0 to 5 V<br>to Microcontroller ADC"]
        OUTPUT --- GND2["Ground LV"]
    end
    HV ~~~ ISOLATION
    ISOLATION ~~~ LV
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight from the Diagram:** Notice that the *ground reference* changes across the isolation barrier (GND1 vs GND2). This is the essence of galvanic isolation — the two grounds are physically separate and can be at vastly different potentials (e.g., a 400 V common-mode difference). The optocoupler carries information via *light* (or via a *modulated carrier* in digital isolators), not via direct electrical connection.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Closed-Loop Hall-Effect Voltage Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VP["V_p 600 V DC Bus"] --> RP["R_p 30 kOhm<br>Primary Resistor"]
    RP --> NP["Primary Coil<br>N_p = 1 turn"]
    NP --> HALL["Hall Element<br>in Air Gap"]
    HALL --> AMPLIFIER["Amplifier and<br>Compensation Driver"]
    AMPLIFIER --> NS["Secondary Coil<br>N_s = 1000 turns"]
    NS --> RMEAS["Burden Resistor<br>R_meas = 100 Ohm"]
    RMEAS --> VOUT["V_out 2 mV to Instrumentation Amp"]
    NS -. Nulling Flux .-> HALL
    AMPLIFIER -. Feedback .-> HALL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dotted lines in this diagram represent the **negative feedback loop** that defines the "closed-loop" nature of the sensor. The secondary current is continuously adjusted so that the **net flux in the magnetic core is zero**. This null-balance principle is what gives the closed-loop Hall sensor its excellent linearity and accuracy.

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
A voltage sensor is a transducer that detects an input voltage and converts it into a proportional, isolated, and standardized electrical output signal (such as 0–5 V, 4–20 mA, or a digital value) suitable for processing by a microcontroller, PLC, or data acquisition system.

**Key Specifications (1.5 Marks — any four):**

5. **Bandwidth / Response time** (e.g., DC to 10 kHz, or < 1 ms)
6. **Operating temperature range** (e.g., $-25^\circ$C to $+70^\circ$C)
