## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a generic voltage sensor. List the four main stages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A resistive voltage divider sensor is designed with $R_1 = 99 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ to monitor a 100 V DC source. The output is connected to a digital voltmeter with an input resistance of 10 k$\Omega$. Calculate:
- (i) The ideal output voltage
- (ii) The actual loaded output voltage
- (iii) The percentage loading error. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**The four universal stages of a voltage sensor are:**

**Stage 1 — Attenuation / Sensing Element (2 Marks):**
This is typically a **resistive voltage divider** (RVD) or **capacitive voltage divider** that scales the input voltage $V_{in}$ to a smaller, manageable level. The output of this stage is $V_{out,1} = K \cdot V_{in}$, where $K$ is the divider ratio.

**Stage 2 — Buffer Amplifier (1.5 Marks):**
A high-input-impedance op-amp voltage follower (e.g., OPA2188) is connected at the divider output to prevent loading of the divider. Its input impedance ($> 1$ T$\Omega$) ensures the divider operates in its ideal regime.

**Stage 3 — Galvanic Isolation (2 Marks):**
The buffered signal is passed through an **isolation amplifier** (e.g., AD202, ISO124) or **digital isolator** (e.g., ADuM1411) which separates the high-voltage side from the low-voltage side. The isolation voltage is typically **2.5 kV to 5 kV RMS**, protecting the downstream electronics and personnel from transients and ground loops.

**Stage 4 — Signal Conditioning and Output (1.5 Marks):**
An **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., INA128) provides gain, a low-pass filter removes noise, and a level shifter maps the signal to a standard output range (**0–5 V, 0–10 V, 4–20 mA, or Modbus digital**). The output is then fed to a microcontroller, PLC, or DAQ.

> **Block Diagram:** Refer to the architecture shown in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $R_1 = 99 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 100$ V, $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 1: Compute the equivalent resistance of $R_2$ in parallel with $R_L$ (1 Mark):**

$$R_{2L} = R_2 \parallel R_L = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L} = \frac{1 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot 10 \text{ k}\Omega}{1 \text{ k}\Omega + 10 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

$$R_{2L} = \frac{10}{11} \text{ k}\Omega \approx 0.9091 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2: Compute the ideal (unloaded) output voltage (2 Marks):**

$$V_{out,ideal} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 100 \cdot \frac{1 \text{ k}\Omega}{99 \text{ k}\Omega + 1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 100 \cdot \frac{1}{100} = 1.000 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Compute the actual (loaded) output voltage (2 Marks):**

$$V_{out,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{2L}}{R_1 + R_{2L}} = 100 \cdot \frac{0.9091}{99 + 0.9091} = 100 \cdot \frac{0.9091}{99.9091}$$

$$V_{out,loaded} = 100 \cdot 0.009099 = 0.9099 \text{ V} \approx 0.910 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Compute the percentage loading error (2 Marks):**

$$\varepsilon_L = \left(1 - \frac{V_{out,loaded}}{V_{out,ideal}}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{0.910}{1.000}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$\varepsilon_L = 0.090 \times 100\% = 9.0\%$$

**Conclusion (½ Mark):** A **9% loading error is unacceptably high**. A buffer amplifier with input impedance $> 10$ M$\Omega$ must be used at the divider output to reduce this error to under 0.1%.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **closed-loop Hall-effect voltage sensor** with a neat block diagram. Derive the relation between the input voltage and the output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A capacitive voltage divider is used to monitor a **33 kV, 50 Hz** substation bus. The high-voltage arm has $C_1 = 50$ pF. Find:
- (i) The value of $C_2$ required to obtain an output of 110 V RMS.
- (ii) The reactances of both capacitors.
- (iii) Comment on why capacitive dividers are unsuitable for DC measurements. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
A closed-loop Hall-effect voltage sensor measures voltage indirectly by first converting the input voltage $V_p$ into a proportional primary current $I_p = V_p / R_p$ using a precision primary resistor $R_p$. This current flows through a primary winding of $N_p$ turns wound around a magnetic core. The resulting magnetic flux is sensed by a **Hall element** placed in the air gap of the core. The Hall output is amplified and fed back to a **secondary compensation winding** of $N_s$ turns. The secondary current $I_s$ is automatically adjusted so that the **net flux in the core is zero** (null-balance principle).

**Derivation (3 Marks):**
Under null-flux condition, the ampere-turns of primary and secondary are equal in magnitude:

$$N_p \cdot I_p = N_s \cdot I_s$$

Since $I_p = V_p / R_p$:

$$I_s = \frac{N_p}{N_s} \cdot \frac{V_p}{R_p}$$

The output voltage is taken across a burden resistor $R_{meas}$:

$$V_{out} = I_s \cdot R_{meas} = \frac{N_p}{N_s} \cdot \frac{V_p}{R_p} \cdot R_{meas}$$

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes.

**Key Advantages:** Galvanic isolation is inherent; very fast response (< 1 $\mu$s); high accuracy ($\pm 0.5\%$); wide bandwidth.

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V_{in} = 33 \text{ kV} = 33{,}000$ V, $V_{out} = 110$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $C_1 = 50$ pF.

**Step 1: Compute the required attenuation factor (1 Mark):**

$$K_C = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{110}{33{,}000} = \frac{1}{300} \approx 0.003333$$

**Step 2: Compute $C_2$ from the capacitive divider formula (2 Marks):**

$$K_C = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2} \implies C_2 = C_1 \cdot \left(\frac{1 - K_C}{K_C}\right)$$

$$C_2 = 50 \text{ pF} \cdot \left(\frac{1 - 0.003333}{0.003333}\right) = 50 \cdot 299 = 14{,}950 \text{ pF} \approx 14.95 \text{ nF}$$

**Step 3: Compute the reactances at 50 Hz (2 Marks):**

$$X_{C1} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{1.5708 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 63.66 \text{ M}\Omega$$

$$X_{C2} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 14.95 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{4.696 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 212.9 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Verify by reactance ratio (1 Mark):**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{X_{C2}}{X_{C1} + X_{C2}} = 33000 \cdot \frac{0.2129}{63.66 + 0.2129} = 33000 \cdot 0.003333 = 110 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Why capacitive dividers fail for DC (1 Mark):**
The reactance of a capacitor is $X_C = 1 / (2\pi f C)$. At **DC** ($f = 0$ Hz), $X_C \to \infty$ for *any* finite $C$. This means no current can flow through the divider, no voltage can develop across $C_2$, and the output collapses to **zero**. Hence capacitive dividers are fundamentally **AC-only** devices.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Voltage Sensor Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the buffer amplifier assumption** — When a problem says "voltage sensor output is 5 V for 500 V input", students often assume $K = 5/500 = 0.01$ directly. But examiners *love* to include a *finite load resistance* and ask for the loading error. Always state the assumption explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing the divider ratio for RVD vs CVD** — In RVD, **larger** resistance is on the HV side. In CVD, **smaller** capacitance is on the HV side. Mixing this up is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> 3. **Units mismatch** — $R_1$ and $R_2$ must be in the **same units** (either both k$\Omega$ or both $\Omega$) before substitution. Examiners deduct ½ mark for unit inconsistency.
> 4. **Forgetting the burden resistor $R_{meas}$** in Hall sensor derivations — The output voltage is across $R_{meas}$, not just the secondary current. Show this clearly.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** — After computing $C_2$ or $R_2$, always substitute back and verify. Examiners award bonus marks for this.
> 6. **Confusing accuracy class with absolute error** — Accuracy class 0.5 means $\pm 0.5\%$ of **Full Scale**, not $\pm 0.5\%$ of the **measured value**. This is a classic trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Voltage Sensor**

- ✅ **Definition:** A voltage sensor is a *transducer* that converts a high-voltage input into a **proportional, isolated, standardized low-level output** (0–5 V, 4–20 mA, or digital).
- ✅ **Four universal stages:** Attenuator → Buffer → Galvanic Isolation → Signal Conditioning → Output.
- ✅ **Resistive Voltage Divider (RVD):** $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$. Used for **DC and low-frequency AC**, up to ~1000 V.
- ✅ **Capacitive Voltage Divider (CVD):** $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$. Used for **AC only** (DC blocked), from 1 kV to 500 kV.
- ✅ **Hall-Effect Voltage Sensor:** Indirect measurement via primary current; closed-loop operation gives **inherent isolation, fast response, high accuracy**.
- ✅ **Loading Error Formula:** $\varepsilon_L = \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{out,loaded}}{V_{out,ideal}}\right) \times 100\%$. Mitigated by **buffer amplifier** (input impedance $> 10$ M$\Omega$).
- ✅ **Design Rule of Thumb:** $R_L \geq 100 \cdot R_2$ to keep loading error below **1%**.
- ✅ **Power dissipation in RVD:** $P_{in} = V_{in}^2 / (R_1 + R_2)$. For HV applications, this becomes impractical — switch to **CVD or Hall-effect**.
- ✅ **Accuracy Class = % of Full Scale Error:** Class 0.5 on 0–500 V range = $\pm 2.5$ V maximum error.
- ✅ **Galvanic Isolation Voltage:** 2.5 kV RMS (standard), 4 kV / 5 kV (high isolation grade).
- ✅ **Common Sensor Outputs:** 0–5 V, 0–10 V, $\pm$10 V, 4–20 mA, RS-485 / Modbus.
- ✅ **CMRR Requirement:** $\geq 80$ dB for industrial sensors to reject common-mode noise.
- ✅ **RVD vs CVD Memory Trick:** **R**esistor — **R**atio is direct; **C**apacitor — **C**apacitance ratio is **inverse**.
- ✅ **Hall Sensor Output:** $V_{out} = \dfrac{N_p}{N_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_p}{R_p} \cdot R_{meas}$ — must always include $R_{meas}$ in the formula.
- ✅ **Why buffers are essential:** A 990 k$\Omega$/10 k$\Omega$ divider loaded by 50 k$\Omega$ shows a **16.5% error** — buffer reduces it to < 0.1%.
- ✅ **KTU Real-world applications:** EV BMS, solar PV monitoring, substation automation, VFD DC-link sensing, railway traction, medical equipment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ADC and DAC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ADC and DAC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3: ADC AND DAC — SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Signal Conversion

In the realm of modern sensors and instrumentation, signals exist in two fundamental domains: the **analog domain** (continuous in time and amplitude) and the **digital domain** (discrete in time and amplitude). Since most physical phenomena captured by sensors — temperature, pressure, light, strain — are inherently analog in nature, while modern processing, storage, and communication systems operate digitally, the bridge between these two worlds becomes critical. This bridge is built using two essential building blocks: the **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** and the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is an electronic device that converts a digital input code (binary word) into a proportional analog output voltage or current. Conversely, an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** transforms a continuous analog input signal into a discrete digital output code representing the input magnitude.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Thermometer Translator" 🧠

Imagine you are a tourist in Japan who only speaks English, but the street signs are entirely in Japanese (which is a symbolic, character-based system — much like digital code). To navigate, you need a **translator**.

- The **Japanese signs** = **Digital Signals** (discrete, coded representations)
- The **English street names spoken aloud** = **Analog Signals** (continuous, varying tones)
- The **translator converting English to Japanese** = **ADC** (real-world → digital system language)
- The **translator converting Japanese to English** = **DAC** (digital system language → real-world actuation)

In a real sensor-based system:
- A **thermistor** produces a continuously varying voltage (analog) → ADC → Microcontroller processes it → DAC → Drives a motor or display that needs an analog control signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Sensors and Devices:**
> Almost every sensor readout chain in industrial IoT, biomedical instrumentation, automotive ECUs, and consumer electronics contains at least one ADC and often a DAC for control or actuation. The performance of the entire system is dictated by the resolution, speed, and accuracy of these converters.

## 1.3 Core Quantities in Data Conversion

| Parameter | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Resolution** | Smallest change in input that can be resolved | Bits (n) |
| **Full-Scale Range (FSR)** | Maximum range of analog input/output | Volts (V) |
| **Step Size (q)** | Smallest distinguishable analog change | Volts (V) |
| **Conversion Time** | Time required to complete one conversion | Seconds (s) |
| **Sampling Rate (fs)** | Number of samples per second | Samples/Second (Hz) |
| **Quantization Error** | Maximum deviation due to discretization | LSB |

## 1.4 The Big Picture — Where ADC/DAC Sit in a Sensor System

A typical signal chain flows as follows:

**Physical Phenomenon → Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Digital Processor (MCU/DSP) → DAC → Actuator / Display**

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the focus on **ADC/DAC** within "Sensors and Devices" emphasizes their role as the **interface between the analog sensor world and the digital processing world**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)

A DAC accepts an n-bit binary word $D = b_{n-1} b_{n-2} \ldots b_1 b_0$ and produces an analog output that is proportional to this digital input.

### 2.1.1 Ideal Transfer Equation

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$$

Where:
- $V_{ref}$ = Reference voltage
- $b_i$ = i-th bit of the digital input (0 or 1)
- $n$ = Number of bits

### 2.1.2 Step Size (Resolution in Volts)

$$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$

Where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale output voltage.

### 2.1.3 Types of DACs (KTU Syllabus Focus)

#### A) Weighted Resistor DAC
- Uses binary-weighted resistors ($R, 2R, 4R, \ldots, 2^{n-1}R$)
- Each bit drives an op-amp summing junction through its corresponding resistor
- **Disadvantage**: Wide range of resistor values makes integration difficult for high bit counts ($n > 8$)

#### B) R-2R Ladder DAC (Most Common)
- Uses only two resistor values: **R** and **2R**
- Easy to fabricate in integrated circuits
- Works on the principle of current division at each ladder node
- **Advantage**: Suitable for high-resolution (12, 14, 16-bit) converters

### 2.1.4 Operational Logic of R-2R Ladder — Step by Step

1. The digital word is applied via switches that connect each ladder node either to **ground (logic 0)** or to the **op-amp virtual ground (logic 1)**.
2. At each node, the current **halves** because of the R-2R impedance network.
3. The MSB (Most Significant Bit) contributes the largest current; the LSB (Least Significant Bit) contributes the smallest.
4. The op-amp converts the summed current into a proportional output voltage.

## 2.2 The Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)

An ADC performs three fundamental operations:
1. **Sampling** — capturing the input at discrete time intervals
2. **Quantization** — mapping the continuous amplitude to discrete levels
3. **Encoding** — assigning a binary code to each level

### 2.2.1 The Sampling Theorem (Nyquist Criterion)

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

Where $f_s$ is the sampling frequency and $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component of the input signal. If violated, **aliasing** occurs.

### 2.2.2 Quantization

$$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

(For an ideal ADC starting from 0 V, the number of quantization levels is $2^n$, hence this slightly different formula from the DAC case.)

### 2.2.3 Types of ADCs (KTU Syllabus Focus)

#### A) Flash (Parallel) ADC
- Uses $2^n - 1$ comparators
- Fastest type — single clock conversion
- Limited to low bit counts (typically ≤ 8 bits) due to exponential hardware growth
- Conversion time: $\sim$ tens of nanoseconds

#### B) Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC
- Uses a single comparator, a DAC, and a successive approximation register
- Binary search algorithm — finds the output in **n clock cycles**
- Excellent trade-off between speed and resolution (8 to 18 bits typical)

#### C) Dual Slope ADC
- Integrates the input for a fixed time, then de-integrates using a reference
- Excellent noise rejection
- Slow but very accurate — used in digital voltmeters

#### D) Counter-Type / Tracking ADC
- Uses a counter driven by a DAC and a comparator
- Simple but slow — conversion time varies with input

## 2.3 🎯 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DAC Output | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \sum b_i 2^i$ | Summation of weighted bits |
| 2 | DAC Step Size | $q = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$ | Smallest analog increment |
| 3 | ADC Quantization Step | $q = V_{FS} / 2^n$ | Smallest input change detected |
| 4 | Resolution (Percentage) | $\%R = \dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100$ | % of full scale |
| 5 | Resolution (dB) | $R_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76$ | SNR-limited dynamic range |
| 6 | Quantization Error | $E_q = \pm q/2 = \pm V_{FS}/(2^{n+1})$ | Maximum deviation |
| 7 | Nyquist Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling frequency |
| 8 | SNR (ideal ADC) | $SNR = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB | Signal-to-noise ratio |
| 9 | Full-Scale Binary | All 1's = $2^n - 1$ | Maximum code |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU DISTINCTION**: The DAC uses $2^n - 1$ in the denominator because the output starts at **0 V** and the **maximum** output corresponds to code $(2^n - 1)$. The ADC uses $2^n$ in the denominator because the **number of quantization levels** is $2^n$ for an n-bit converter (e.g., 3 bits → 8 levels → codes 000 to 111). This distinction is a **favorite KTU examiner question**! 🎯

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Sensors**: ADCs read oxygen sensors, throttle position, and manifold pressure in real time.
- **Medical Instrumentation**: ECG and EEG signals are digitized using 16/24-bit ADCs for accurate diagnostics.
- **Audio Systems**: DACs reconstruct audio from MP3/FLAC files in smartphones and Hi-Fi systems.
- **Industrial Process Control**: 4–20 mA current loops paired with ADCs for sensor interfacing.
- **Data Acquisition Systems (DAQ)**: Used in labs for capturing sensor data with high precision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Size of an n-bit DAC

### Problem Statement
Derive the step size (resolution in volts) of an n-bit DAC whose full-scale output is $V_{FS}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** An n-bit DAC has binary input codes ranging from:

$$0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots, (2^n - 1)$$

**Step 2:** The analog output is proportional to the digital code. The output for code $k$ is:

$$V_{out}(k) = k \cdot q$$

**Step 3:** The full-scale output $V_{FS}$ corresponds to the maximum code $(2^n - 1)$:

$$V_{FS} = (2^n - 1) \cdot q$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $q$:

$$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$

**Conclusion:** This is the smallest analog increment the DAC can produce.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Resolution of an n-bit ADC in % of Full Scale

### Problem Statement
A 12-bit ADC has a full-scale input of 10 V. Find (a) the step size, (b) the resolution as a percentage, (c) the maximum quantization error.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $n = 12$ bits
- $V_{FS} = 10$ V

**(a) Step Size:**

$$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.4414 \text{ mV}$$

**(b) Resolution in percentage:**

$$\%R = \frac{1}{2^n} \times 100 = \frac{1}{4096} \times 100 = 0.0244\%$$

**(c) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$E_{q,max} = \pm \frac{q}{2} = \pm \frac{2.4414 \text{ mV}}{2} = \pm 1.2207 \text{ mV}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: R-2R Ladder DAC Output Voltage

### Problem Statement
A 4-bit R-2R ladder DAC uses $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and a feedback resistor $R_f = R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. The reference voltage is $V_{ref} = 5 \text{ V}$. Find the output voltage for the digital input $D = 1011_2$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the binary word:

$$D = b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1 \, 0 \, 1 \, 1$$

**Step 2:** Convert to decimal:

$$N = (1 \times 2^3) + (0 \times 2^2) + (1 \times 2^1) + (1 \times 2^0) = 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11$$

**Step 3:** Apply the R-2R ladder output formula:

$$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{N}{2^n}$$

The negative sign comes from the inverting op-amp configuration. We take the magnitude:

$$\vert V_{out} \vert = 5 \cdot \frac{11}{2^4} = 5 \cdot \frac{11}{16} = \frac{55}{16} = 3.4375 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** Verify with the bit-by-bit method:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref}\left( \frac{b_3}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_1}{8} + \frac{b_0}{16} \right)$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{0}{4} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{16} \right)$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \left( 0.5 + 0 + 0.125 + 0.0625 \right)$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \times 0.6875 = 3.4375 \text{ V} \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Flash ADC Number of Comparators

### Problem Statement
For an n-bit Flash ADC, derive the formula for the number of comparators required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** A flash ADC generates $2^n - 1$ reference voltages by using a resistor ladder.

**Step 2:** Each reference voltage is fed into one comparator along with the input voltage.

**Step 3:** The number of comparators equals the number of reference voltages:

$$N_{comp} = 2^n - 1$$

**Step 4:** Example for n = 3 bits:

$$N_{comp} = 2^3 - 1 = 7 \text{ comparators}$$

**Step 5:** For n = 8 bits:

$$N_{comp} = 2^8 - 1 = 255 \text{ comparators}$$

**Conclusion:** This exponential growth is why flash ADCs are limited to low bit counts (typically ≤ 8).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Successive Approximation ADC — Number of Clock Cycles

### Problem Statement
How many clock cycles does an 8-bit SAR ADC require to complete one conversion?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** The SAR ADC uses a **binary search algorithm**.

**Step 2:** At each clock cycle, the SAR tests one bit — starting from the MSB and proceeding to the LSB.

**Step 3:** Total clock cycles = number of bits:

$$N_{cycles} = n = 8$$

**Conclusion:** An 8-bit SAR ADC requires exactly **8 clock cycles** for one full conversion (plus a small acquisition time, but this is the core timing).

> [!NOTE]
> This is why SAR ADCs are popular in **microcontroller-integrated converters** (e.g., Arduino's 10-bit SAR ADC takes 10–13 ADC clock cycles per sample).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Performance Comparison of ADC Types

| Feature | Flash | SAR | Dual Slope | Counter-Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | Fastest | Medium | Slowest | Slow |
| **Resolution** | ≤ 8 bits | 8–18 bits | 12–20 bits | 8–12 bits |
| **Hardware** | $2^n - 1$ comparators | 1 comparator + DAC | Integrator + comparator | Counter + DAC + comparator |
| **Conversion Time** | 1 clock cycle | $n$ clock cycles | $\propto 2^n$ | $\propto$ input magnitude |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Noise Rejection** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Poor |
| **KTU Use Case** | Oscilloscopes, video | MCU sensors, DAQ | Digital multimeters | Simple controls |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: A Complete Sensor Signal Chain with ADC/DAC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Phenomenon] --> B[Sensor]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    C --> D[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    D --> E[ADC]
    E --> F[Digital Processor MCU DSP]
    F --> G[DAC]
    G --> H[Actuator or Display]
    H -.Feedback.-> A
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The **physical phenomenon** (e.g., temperature, pressure) is sensed.
- The **sensor** converts it into a low-level analog electrical signal.
- The **signal conditioning amplifier** boosts the signal to a usable range.
- The **anti-aliasing filter** removes high-frequency components above $f_s/2$.
- The **ADC** digitizes the signal.
- The **digital processor** (MCU/DSP) analyzes and computes a response.
- The **DAC** reconstructs an analog control signal.
- The **actuator** responds to the physical world, completing the loop.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BitSources["Digital Input Bits"]
        B3[b3 MSB]
        B2[b2]
        B1[b1]
        B0[b0 LSB]
    end
    subgraph Switches["CMOS Switches"]
        S3[SW3]
        S2[SW2]
        S1[SW1]
        S0[SW0]
    end
    subgraph Ladder["R-2R Resistor Ladder Network"]
        R1[R]
        R2[2R]
        R3[R]
        R4[2R]
        R5[R]
        R6[2R]
        R7[R]
        R8[2R]
        R9[R]
    end
    OpAmp[Op-Amp Summing Inverting Configuration]
    Vref[Vref Reference Voltage]
    Vout[Vout Analog Output]
    GND[Ground]
    B3 --> S3
    B2 --> S2
    B1 --> S1
    B0 --> S0
    Vref --> Ladder
    Ladder --> OpAmp
    S3 --> Ladder
    S2 --> Ladder
    S1 --> Ladder
    S0 --> Ladder
    OpAmp --> Vout
    GND --> Ladder
```

**Operating Principle:**
- Each switch (SW0–SW3) routes its bit's contribution to either **virtual ground (op-amp input)** or **actual ground**.
- The ladder network performs binary-weighted current division.
- The op-amp sums these currents through $R_f$ to produce $V_{out}$.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vin[Analog Input Vin] --> Comp[Comparator Positive Input]
    Vdac[DAC Output] --> Comp
    Comp --> SARReg[Successive Approximation Register SAR]
    SARReg --> OutputReg[Output Register N-bit Digital Code]
    SARReg --> Vdac
    Clock[Clock] --> SARReg
    Start[Start of Conversion SOC] --> SARReg
    EOC[End of Conversion EOC Flag] --> SARReg
    OutputReg --> Dout[Digital Output Word]
```

**Operating Sequence:**
1. The **Start** signal initiates the conversion.
2. The **SAR** sets the MSB = 1, all other bits = 0; the DAC produces the corresponding analog voltage.
3. The **comparator** checks if $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$: if yes, MSB remains 1; if no, MSB is cleared to 0.
4. This binary search continues bit-by-bit until the LSB is decided.
5. The **Output Register** holds the final N-bit digital code.
6. The **EOC** flag signals the processor that the conversion is complete.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Dual Slope ADC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Switch[Input Switch SW] --> IntOp[Integrator Op-Amp with Capacitor]
    IntOp --> Comp[Comparator Zero Crossing Detector]
    Comp --> Counter[Counter with Gate Control]
    Counter --> OutReg[Output Register N-bit Binary]
    Clock[Reference Clock] --> Counter
    VrefN[Negative Reference Voltage] --> Switch
    VinPos[Positive Input Voltage] --> Switch
    Control[Control Logic] --> Switch
    Control --> Counter
```

**Two Phases of Operation:**

- **Phase 1 — Run-Up Integration:** Input $V_{in}$ is integrated for a fixed time $T_1$. The integrator output ramps up.
- **Phase 2 — Run-Down De-integration:** The switch flips to $-V_{ref}$. The integrator output ramps down at a fixed slope. The counter measures the time $T_2$ taken to reach zero.
- **Output Calculation:**

$$N = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n$$

Where $N$ is the count held in the output register, which is the digitized value.

> [!NOTE]
> The **dual slope technique inherently rejects** periodic noise (like 50/60 Hz power line interference) if $T_1$ is chosen as a multiple of the noise period. This is why it dominates **precision digital multimeters**.

---

## 4.5 Comparative Hierarchy of ADC Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ADC[ADC Main Categories] --> HighSpeed[High Speed]
    ADC --> HighRes[High Resolution]
    ADC --> Balanced[Balanced]
    HighSpeed --> Flash[Flash Parallel]
    HighRes --> DualSlope[Dual Slope]
    HighRes --> SigmaDelta[Sigma Delta]
    Balanced --> SAR[Successive Approximation SAR]
    Balanced --> Pipeline[Pipeline]
    Balanced --> Counter[Counter Type]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the term "resolution" of an ADC. A 10-bit ADC has a full-scale input of 5 V. Calculate its resolution in volts and as a percentage of full scale.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Resolution is the smallest change in the analog input that an ADC can detect and distinguish, equal to one Least Significant Bit (LSB) of the digital output.

**Step Size (Volts):**

$$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} = 4.8828 \text{ mV}$$

**Resolution (Percentage):**

$$\%R = \frac{1}{2^n} \times 100 = \frac{1}{1024} \times 100 = 0.0977\%$$

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a R-2R ladder DAC.**

**Model Answer:**

A R-2R ladder DAC uses only two resistor values, **R** and **2R**, in a repetitive ladder network. Each bit of the digital input drives a switch that connects the corresponding ladder node either to ground (logic 0) or to the op-amp's virtual ground (logic 1).

**Working Principle:**
- At each ladder node, the current divides by a factor of 2 due to the R-2R impedance pattern.
- The MSB switch controls the largest current contribution; the LSB switch controls the smallest.
- The op-amp sums all these currents and converts them to an output voltage proportional to the digital input.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Digital Input b3 b2 b1 b0] --> Switches[CMOS Switch Network]
    Switches --> Ladder[R-2R Ladder Network]
    Vref[Vref] --> Ladder
    Ladder --> OpAmp[Summing Op-Amp Inverting]
    OpAmp --> Vout[Vout Analog Output]
```

**Advantages:**
- Only two resistor values → easy to fabricate in ICs.
- Suitable for high-resolution (12–16 bit) converters.
- No wide range of resistor values required (unlike weighted resistor DAC).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3 (SAR ADC diagram).

**Working Principle:**
The SAR ADC operates on a **binary search algorithm**:

1. On receiving the **Start of Conversion (SOC)** signal, the SAR register sets the MSB = 1 and all other bits to 0.
2. The internal **DAC** converts this trial code into an analog voltage $V_{DAC}$.
3. The **comparator** checks: if $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, the MSB remains 1; otherwise, it is reset to 0.
4. The process repeats for the next bit (bit-by-bit) until the LSB is decided.
5. After **n clock cycles**, the output register contains the final n-bit digital word.
6. The **End of Conversion (EOC)** flag is raised.

**Advantages:**
- Good trade-off between speed and resolution.
- Moderate hardware (1 comparator + 1 DAC + 1 SAR).
- Low power consumption.
- Widely used in MCU-integrated ADCs.

**Disadvantages:**
- Slower than flash ADC.
- Requires a precise internal DAC.
- Susceptible to noise during the conversion window.

---

**(b)** A **successive approximation ADC** has a resolution of 12 bits and a clock frequency of 1 MHz. If the input voltage is 3.7 V and the reference voltage is 5 V, find:
   1. The digital output code.
   2. The conversion time. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $n = 12$ bits
- $f_{clk} = 1 \text{ MHz} \Rightarrow T_{clk} = 1 \mu s$
- $V_{in} = 3.7 \text{ V}$, $V_{ref} = 5 \text{ V}$

**(1) Digital Output Code:**

$$N = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n = \frac{3.7}{5} \times 4096 = 0.74 \times 4096 = 3031.04$$

Rounding to nearest integer: $N = 3031$

Converting to binary: $3031 = 101111010111_2$

**(2) Conversion Time:**

$$T_{conv} = n \times T_{clk} = 12 \times 1 \mu s = 12 \mu s$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **dual slope ADC** with a neat block diagram and waveform. Derive the expression for its digital output. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.4.

**Two Phases of Operation:**

**Phase 1 — Run-Up Integration:**
- The switch connects the input $V_{in}$ to the integrator for a fixed time $T_1 = 2^n \cdot T_{clk}$.
- The integrator output ramps up to:

$$V_{A} = -\frac{V_{in}}{RC} \cdot T_1$$

**Phase 2 — Run-Down De-integration:**
- The switch flips to $-V_{ref}$.
- The integrator output ramps down at a fixed rate: $\frac{V_{ref}}{RC}$.
- The counter measures the time $T_2$ for the output to reach zero:

$$V_{A} - \frac{V_{ref}}{RC} \cdot T_2 = 0 \Rightarrow T_2 = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot T_1$$

**Derivation of Digital Output:**

Since the counter counts clock pulses during $T_2$:

$$N = \frac{T_2}{T_{clk}} = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_{clk}} = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n$$

**Final Expression:**

$$N = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n$$

**Advantages:**
- Excellent noise rejection (especially 50 Hz).
- High accuracy; insensitive to component drift.
- Ideal for precision digital voltmeters.

**Disadvantages:**
- Slow conversion speed.
- Requires precision integrator components.

---

**(b)** A **dual slope ADC** uses a 16-bit counter and a 10 MHz clock. The integrator's full-scale voltage is 10 V. If the input voltage is 4.5 V and the reference voltage is 10 V, determine:
   1. The run-up time $T_1$.
   2. The de-integration time $T_2$.
   3. The digital output count $N$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $n = 16$ bits, $f_{clk} = 10 \text{ MHz} \Rightarrow T_{clk} = 0.1 \mu s$
- $V_{FS} = 10$ V, $V_{in} = 4.5$ V, $V_{ref} = 10$ V

**(1) Run-up Time:**

$$T_1 = 2^n \cdot T_{clk} = 2^{16} \times 0.1 \, \mu s = 65536 \times 0.1 \, \mu s = 6.5536 \text{ ms}$$

**(2) De-integration Time:**

$$T_2 = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times T_1 = \frac{4.5}{10} \times 6.5536 \text{ ms} = 0.45 \times 6.5536 \text{ ms} = 2.9491 \text{ ms}$$

**(3) Digital Output Count:**

$$N = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n = \frac{4.5}{10} \times 65536 = 29491$$

In binary: $29491 = 0111001100010011_2$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing DAC and ADC Formulas**: Students often write $q = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$ for an ADC. This is **WRONG** — the ADC uses $2^n$ in the denominator. The DAC uses $(2^n - 1)$. This is the **#1 most common error**.
> 
> 2. **Flash ADC Comparators**: Many students write $2^n$ comparators for a Flash ADC. The correct answer is $2^n - 1$. **Marks deducted for off-by-one errors.**
> 
> 3. **R-2R DAC Output Sign**: Forgetting that the op-amp inverts the output → writing positive voltages when the configuration is inverting. Always check the polarity.
> 
> 4. **SAR ADC Clock Cycles**: Some students write "$n$ clock cycles" but forget to add the **acquisition/sample time** in practical problems. The KTU paper may ask for total conversion time including the sample-and-hold phase.
> 
> 5. **Resolution in dB vs %**: Don't confuse the **6.02 dB/bit rule** (used for SNR) with the **percentage resolution**. They are different metrics for different contexts.
> 
> 6. **Unit Errors**: Always specify mV or μV for step size, and bits for resolution. Vague answers like "small" lose marks.

---

## 5.4 ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ADC and DAC (Module 3)**
> 
> **DAC Fundamentals:**
> - DAC converts digital code → analog voltage/current.
> - R-2R Ladder is preferred over Weighted Resistor for IC fabrication.
> - Step size: $q = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$ (DAC specific — note the $-1$).
> - Output formula: $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{N}{2^n}$ (for inverting R-2R with unity-gain op-amp).
> 
> **ADC Fundamentals:**
> - ADC performs **Sampling → Quantization → Encoding**.
> - Nyquist Theorem: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — aliasing occurs otherwise.
> - Step size: $q = V_{FS} / 2^n$ (ADC specific — note no $-1$).
> - Resolution: $\%R = 1/2^n \times 100$.
> 
> **Key ADC Architectures (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Flash**: $2^n - 1$ comparators, 1 clock cycle, ≤ 8 bits, fastest.
> - **SAR**: n clock cycles, binary search, 8–18 bits, balanced.
> - **Dual Slope**: 2 phases, high accuracy, noise rejection, slow.
> - **Counter Type**: Simple, slow, variable conversion time.
> 
> **Vital Numbers to Memorize:**
> - $2^8 = 256$
> - $2^{10} = 1024$
> - $2^{12} = 4096$
> - $2^{16} = 65536$
> - 6.02 dB per bit (SNR)
> 
> **Common KTU Exam Keywords:**
> - "Resolution" — always specify units.
> - "Conversion Time" — show calculation.
> - "Aliasing" — mention Nyquist and anti-aliasing filter.
> - "Quantization Error" — state $\pm q/2$.

---

🎓 **End of Module 3 — ADC and DAC Notes**

*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — B.Tech OECIT725 Sensors and Devices*
