topics.title: Temperature and Humidity Sensor DHT11
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temperature and Humidity Sensor DHT11

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **DHT11** is a **digital-output relative humidity and temperature sensor** manufactured using a calibrated digital signal acquisition technique. It integrates a **resistive-type humidity measurement component** (capacitive polymer sensor) and an **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) thermistor** for temperature sensing, coupled with a high-performance 8-bit microcontroller that converts the analog sensor signals into a fully calibrated digital serial output on a single-wire bidirectional bus.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework for *Sensors and Devices*, the DHT11 belongs to the family of **integrated smart sensors** (also called "sensor-plus-conditioning" modules), where the signal conditioning, linearization, and analog-to-digital conversion are all embedded inside a single chip package.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** DHT11 is treated as a representative case study for *Digital Environmental Sensors with Embedded Microcontroller* under the broader classification of **Integrated Sensors (Module 3)**. Examiners frequently test: working principle, pin configuration, communication protocol (single-wire), data format (40 bits), checksum verification, and conversion equations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Decoded for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Relative Humidity (RH):** The ratio of actual water vapor pressure to the saturation vapor pressure at a given temperature, expressed as a percentage.
> - **NTC Thermistor:** A thermally sensitive resistor whose resistance **decreases** as temperature **increases**.
> - **Single-Wire Protocol:** A serial communication standard where data, command, and clock signaling are all multiplexed on one data line.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a small, **two-in-one weather station** sitting on your desk.

One side of this tiny device has a **"sponge-like" polymer strip** that swells when the air becomes humid (think of a sponge absorbing water and getting heavier/wider). The device measures how swollen this sponge is and converts it into a humidity number.

The other side has a **"temperature-sensitive resistor"** — like the metal coil inside an electric kettle that changes its electrical resistance as it gets hot. Colder air → higher resistance; hotter air → lower resistance.

Inside the small black box, a tiny brain (an 8-bit microcontroller) reads both values, converts them into clean digital numbers, and sends them to your Arduino/Raspberry Pi over a single conversation line.

**In one sentence:** The DHT11 is the *Swiss Army knife* of entry-level environmental sensing — affordable, reliable, and pre-calibrated.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical/electrical constants are **intrinsic to the DHT11 datasheet parameters** and frequently asked in KTU examinations:

- Operating Voltage: **3.3 V to 5.5 V DC**
- Current consumption: **0.3 mA (measuring)**, **60 μA (standby)**
- Sampling rate: **1 Hz (1 sample per second)**
- Single-wire bus length: **< 20 m** (with 5 kΩ pull-up)
- Operating range: **0 °C to 50 °C**, **20 % RH to 90 % RH**
- Accuracy: **±2 °C**, **±5 % RH**
- Resolution: **1 °C**, **1 % RH**

> [!NOTE]
> The conversion coefficients in the data format are integral multiples of the resolution. For DHT11, the conversion factor is **1 count = 1 °C** and **1 count = 1 % RH**, unlike its higher-precision sibling DHT22 where the factors are 0.1.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Architecture

The DHT11 is internally organized into the following functional sub-blocks:

1. **Capacitive Humidity Sensor Element**
   - A polymer dielectric layer sandwiched between two electrodes.
   - The polymer absorbs/desorbs water vapor depending on ambient RH.
   - Capacitance $C$ varies with relative humidity $\phi$ approximately as:

$$C(\phi) = C_0 \left[ 1 + k_{\phi} \cdot \phi \right]$$

where $C_0$ is the dry capacitance and $k_{\phi}$ is the humidity sensitivity coefficient (in % per %RH).

2. **NTC Thermistor (Temperature Element)**
   - Resistance varies with temperature using the **Steinhart–Hart equation** or the simplified **β-parameter equation**:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

where $R_0$ is resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (usually 25 °C = 298.15 K), and $\beta$ is the material constant (typically 3950 K for the DHT11 NTC).

3. **Analog Front-End + ADC**
   - Two separate ADC channels digitize the capacitance-derived voltage and the thermistor-derived voltage.

4. **8-bit Microcontroller with Calibration LUT**
   - Contains a factory-calibrated lookup table (LUT) that compensates for non-linearity of both sensors.
   - The LUT converts raw ADC counts into engineering units (°C and % RH).

5. **Single-Wire Serial Interface**
   - Push-pull output stage with open-drain capability for bidirectional communication.
   - Requires external **pull-up resistor** (typically 4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ) on the DATA line.

## 2.2 Single-Wire Communication Protocol (Detailed)

The DHT11 uses a master-slave protocol:

- The host (MCU/Arduino) acts as the **master**.
- The DHT11 acts as the **slave**.
- Communication always begins with a **start signal** from the master.

### Step 1: MCU Start Signal
- MCU pulls DATA line **LOW for at least 18 ms** (recommended 20 ms).
- Then releases the line (sets as input) and waits **20–40 μs**.

### Step 2: DHT11 Response Signal
- DHT11 detects the LOW pulse and acknowledges by pulling DATA **LOW for 80 μs**, then **HIGH for 80 μs** (response preamble).

### Step 3: Data Transmission
- DHT11 transmits **40 bits** of data.
- Each bit begins with a **50 μs LOW pulse**.
- The duration of the following HIGH pulse determines bit value:
  - **26–28 μs HIGH → bit = 0**
  - **70 μs HIGH → bit = 1**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why single-wire is special:** In standard UART/SPI/I2C, clock and data are separate. In DHT11's single-wire protocol, the **bit duration itself** encodes the logic value — a form of *Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) encoding on the time axis*. This is a popular KTU short-answer question.

## 2.3 40-Bit Data Frame Format

The 40 bits are transmitted in the following order:

| Bit Position | Content | Range (DHT11) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0–7 (Byte 1) | Integral RH | 0–90 |
| 8–15 (Byte 2) | Decimal RH | Always 0 (DHT11) |
| 16–23 (Byte 3) | Integral Temperature | 0–50 |
| 24–31 (Byte 4) | Decimal Temperature | Always 0 (DHT11) |
| 32–39 (Byte 5) | Checksum | Sum of Bytes 1–4 (lower 8 bits) |

> [!NOTE]
> The DHT11 (unlike DHT22/AM2302) **does not report decimal places**. If your reading shows decimals, the firmware is artificially appending ".0". The KTU exam always uses integer values for DHT11.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all the formulas, constants, and conversions** that a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. Note the use of `\vert` (not `\vert\vert`) in any conditional/absolute-value expressions to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Humidity Conversion (DHT11) | $RH = $ Byte 2 (decimal) | Byte 2 = integer part of RH | % RH |
| 2 | Temperature Conversion (DHT11) | $T = $ Byte 4 (decimal) | Byte 4 = integer part of T | °C |
| 3 | Checksum Verification | $CS = B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4 \pmod{256}$ | $B_i$ = data bytes | Unitless |
| 4 | Capacitance vs RH | $C(\phi) = C_0 [1 + k_\phi \phi]$ | $C_0$ = dry capacitance, $k_\phi$ = sensitivity | F, % RH |
| 5 | NTC Resistance (β-equation) | $R_T = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | $R_0$ = resistance at $T_0$, $\beta$ = material constant | Ω, K |
| 6 | Pull-up Resistor Range | $4.7\ k\Omega \le R_{PU} \le 10\ k\Omega$ | $R_{PU}$ = pull-up resistor | Ω |
| 7 | Minimum Start LOW Pulse | $t_{LOW} \ge 18\ ms$ | Host start signal | s |
| 8 | DHT11 Response LOW | $t_{R\_LOW} = 80\ \mu s$ | DHT11 response low | s |
| 9 | DHT11 Response HIGH | $t_{R\_HIGH} = 80\ \mu s$ | DHT11 response high | s |
| 10 | Bit '0' HIGH Duration | $t_{B0} = 26\text{–}28\ \mu s$ | Logic-0 high pulse | s |
| 11 | Bit '1' HIGH Duration | $t_{B1} = 70\ \mu s$ | Logic-1 high pulse | s |
| 12 | Total Frame Time | $T_{frame} \approx 4\ ms$ | Complete 40-bit transmission | s |
| 13 | Sampling Frequency | $f_s \le 1\ Hz$ | $f_s$ = sampling rate | Hz |
| 14 | Saturated Vapor Pressure (Magnus) | $e_s(T) = 6.112 \cdot e^{17.67 T / (T + 243.5)}$ | $T$ in °C | hPa |
| 15 | Absolute Humidity from RH | $AH = 216.7 \cdot \frac{e_s(T) \cdot RH}{273.15 + T}$ | $AH$ in g/m³ | g/m³ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DHT11 is the **de-facto teaching sensor** in KTU-affiliated IoT, embedded systems, and product design labs. It is widely used in:

- **HVAC indoor climate monitors** (where ±5% RH accuracy is acceptable).
- **Smart agriculture** greenhouses (where sub-degree precision is unnecessary).
- **Weather stations** for educational projects.
- **Data logger prototypes** interfacing with Arduino UNO, ESP8266, ESP32, and Raspberry Pi Pico.
- **Server room temperature alarms** (above 35 °C triggering fan relays).

Its **industrial replacement** in production-grade applications is the DHT22 (AM2302) or the SHT31 from Sensirion.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting Raw DHT11 Bytes into Engineering Values

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
Given the following 5 bytes received from a DHT11 sensor over its single-wire bus, calculate the relative humidity, temperature, and verify the checksum.

**Received Bytes (in transmission order):**
- Byte 1 (Integral RH) = 45
- Byte 2 (Decimal RH) = 0
- Byte 3 (Integral Temp) = 26
- Byte 4 (Decimal Temp) = 0
- Byte 5 (Checksum) = 71

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the Data Frame Structure**

The DHT11 transmits data in 5 consecutive 8-bit bytes, totalling 40 bits. The frame is constructed as:

$$\text{Frame} = \underbrace{B_1}_{8\ \text{bits}} \, \vert \, \underbrace{B_2}_{8\ \text{bits}} \, \vert \, \underbrace{B_3}_{8\ \text{bits}} \, \vert \, \underbrace{B_4}_{8\ \text{bits}} \, \vert \, \underbrace{B_5}_{8\ \text{bits}}$$

where $\vert$ here denotes byte separation (not the modulus operator). Substituting the numerical values:

$$\text{Frame} = 45 \;\vert\; 0 \;\vert\; 26 \;\vert\; 0 \;\vert\; 71$$

**Step 2 — Extract the Relative Humidity**

For the DHT11, the integer part of relative humidity is contained in $B_1$, and the decimal part is in $B_2$ (which is always 0 for DHT11). The conversion rule is:

$$RH = B_1 \cdot 1 + B_2 \cdot 0.1$$

Substituting $B_1 = 45$ and $B_2 = 0$:

$$RH = 45 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0.1 = 45\ \%RH$$

**Step 3 — Extract the Temperature**

Similarly, the temperature is calculated from $B_3$ (integer part) and $B_4$ (decimal part, always 0 for DHT11):

$$T = B_3 \cdot 1 + B_4 \cdot 0.1$$

Substituting $B_3 = 26$ and $B_4 = 0$:

$$T = 26 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0.1 = 26\ ^\circ C$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Checksum**

The checksum rule states that the received $B_5$ must equal the lower 8 bits of the sum of $B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4$:

$$CS_{calc} = (B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4) \mod 256$$

Performing the arithmetic:

$$B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4 = 45 + 0 + 26 + 0 = 71$$

Then applying the modulo operation:

$$CS_{calc} = 71 \mod 256 = 71$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Received Checksum**

The received $B_5 = 71$. We compare:

$$CS_{calc} = 71 \quad\stackrel{?}{=}\quad B_5 = 71$$

Since the calculated checksum equals the received checksum, the data frame is **VALID**.

**Step 6 — Final Reported Values**

$$\boxed{RH = 45\ \%\text{RH} \quad , \quad T = 26\ ^\circ\text{C} \quad , \quad \text{Status} = \text{VALID}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Pulse-Width Discrimination for Bit Decoding

The DHT11 uses **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) on the time axis** to encode logic levels. The host MCU samples the duration of the HIGH pulse following each 50 μs LOW pulse.

### 3.2.1 Decision Boundary Derivation

Let $t_H$ be the duration of the HIGH pulse. The DHT11 datasheet specifies two nominal values: 26–28 μs for logic 0, and 70 μs for logic 1.

**Logical separation point:**

$$t_{threshold} = \frac{t_{B0,max} + t_{B1,min}}{2} = \frac{28 + 70}{2} = 49\ \mu s$$

Therefore, the decoder decision rule is:

$$\text{bit} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } t_H \le 49\ \mu s \\ 1 & \text{if } t_H > 49\ \mu s \end{cases}$$

In practice, embedded firmware sets $t_{threshold} = 50\ \mu s$ for simplicity.

### 3.2.2 Worked Example

Suppose the MCU times the high pulses and obtains the following sequence: [80 μs, 26 μs, 80 μs, 26 μs, 80 μs, 26 μs, 26 μs, 80 μs] (only 8 pulses shown for brevity, but the actual frame has 40 such pulses).

**Note:** The first two 80 μs pulses are the *response preamble* (not data bits). Data bits begin from the third 80 μs pulse onward.

Excluding preamble, the 8 data-bit durations are:
- Bit 1: $t_H = 80\ \mu s$ → Since $80 > 50$ → **bit = 1**
- Bit 2: $t_H = 26\ \mu s$ → Since $26 < 50$ → **bit = 0**
- Bit 3: $t_H = 80\ \mu s$ → **bit = 1**
- Bit 4: $t_H = 26\ \mu s$ → **bit = 0**
- Bit 5: $t_H = 80\ \mu s$ → **bit = 1**
- Bit 6: $t_H = 26\ \mu s$ → **bit = 0**
- Bit 7: $t_H = 26\ \mu s$ → **bit = 0**
- Bit 8: $t_H = 80\ \mu s$ → **bit = 1**

Concatenating the bits: $\text{MSB} \to \text{LSB} = 10101001_2$.

Converting to decimal:

$$N = 1 \cdot 2^7 + 0 \cdot 2^6 + 1 \cdot 2^5 + 0 \cdot 2^4 + 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0$$

$$N = 128 + 0 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 169$$

$$\boxed{\text{Byte value} = 169_{10} = 0\text{xA9}_{16}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Sampling Rate Limitation

The DHT11's datasheet specifies a maximum sampling rate of 1 Hz. We can justify this from the communication timing.

### 3.3.1 Communication Time Analysis

**Time taken by host start signal:**

$$t_{host} = t_{LOW} + t_{wait} = 18\ ms + 30\ \mu s \approx 18.03\ ms$$

**Time taken by DHT11 response signal:**

$$t_{resp} = t_{R\_LOW} + t_{R\_HIGH} = 80 + 80 = 160\ \mu s$$

**Time taken by 40-bit data transmission:**

Each bit takes $(50\ \mu s + t_{H})\ \mu s$. Assuming the worst case (all 1s, $t_H = 70\ \mu s$):

$$t_{bit,max} = 50 + 70 = 120\ \mu s$$

$$t_{data,max} = 40 \times 120\ \mu s = 4800\ \mu s = 4.8\ ms$$

**Total frame time (worst case):**

$$t_{frame,max} = t_{host} + t_{resp} + t_{data,max} = 18.03 + 0.16 + 4.8 = 22.99\ ms$$

**Maximum theoretical sampling rate:**

$$f_{s,max} = \frac{1}{t_{frame,max}} = \frac{1}{0.02299} \approx 43.5\ Hz$$

**Why the datasheet says 1 Hz:**

The internal capacitive humidity sensor requires **time to re-equilibrate** with the ambient humidity. The polymer dielectric's absorption/desorption time constant is in the range of several seconds, especially at high humidity. Therefore, although *electrical* sampling could be 43 Hz, the *physical* settling time limits $f_s$ to **1 Hz**. This is a classic KTU question testing the difference between electrical and physical bandwidth.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: DHT11 vs DHT22 vs SHT31

> [!NOTE]
> This comparative matrix is **frequently asked as a 14-mark question** in KTU ESE, where students are asked to *compare and contrast* DHT11 with at least one other environmental sensor. The following table covers the key distinguishing parameters.

| Parameter | DHT11 | DHT22 (AM2302) | SHT31 (Sensirion) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Temp Range | 0 to 50 °C | -40 to 80 °C | -40 to 125 °C |
| Humidity Range | 20 to 90 % RH | 0 to 100 % RH | 0 to 100 % RH |
| Temp Accuracy | $\pm 2\ ^\circ C$ | $\pm 0.5\ ^\circ C$ | $\pm 0.3\ ^\circ C$ |
| Humidity Accuracy | $\pm 5\ \%\text{RH}$ | $\pm 2\ \%\text{RH}$ | $\pm 2\ \%\text{RH}$ |
| Resolution | 1 °C, 1 % RH | 0.1 °C, 0.1 % RH | 0.1 °C, 0.1 % RH |
| Supply Voltage | 3.3 to 5.5 V | 3.3 to 5.5 V | 2.4 to 5.5 V |
| Interface | Single-wire | Single-wire | I2C |
| Sampling Rate | 1 Hz (min 1 s) | 0.5 Hz (min 2 s) | Up to 10 Hz |
| Cost (approx.) | ₹ 80–120 | ₹ 250–400 | ₹ 350–500 |
| Calibration | Factory calibrated | Factory calibrated | Field recalibration possible |
| Operating Temp of Sensor Die | -20 to 60 °C | -40 to 80 °C | -40 to 125 °C |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DHT11

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **internal functional blocks** of the DHT11 sensor, from the physical sensing elements through to the digital output. This satisfies the "Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow" requirement of the KTU 2024 scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AMBIENT AIR INLET] --> B1[CAPACITIVE HUMIDITY SENSOR]
    A --> B2[NTC THERMISTOR]
    B1 --> C1[OSCILLATOR AND CHARGE PUMP]
    B2 --> C2[VOLTAGE DIVIDER NETWORK]
    C1 --> D1[ANALOG MULTIPLEXER MUX1]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E[8 BIT SUCCESSIVE APPROXIMATION ADC]
    E --> F[CALIBRATION LOOKUP TABLE LUT]
    F --> G[8 BIT EMBEDDED MICROCONTROLLER]
    G --> H[SINGLE WIRE INTERFACE BUFFER]
    H --> I[OPEN DRAIN DATA PIN PIN2]
    J[HOST MCU] -->|START SIGNAL LOW 18 MS| H
    H -->|RESPONSE PREAMBLE 80 PLUS 80 US| J
    H -->|40 BIT DATA FRAME PWM ENCODED| J
    K[EXTERNAL PULL UP RESISTOR 4K7 TO 10K] --> I
    L[VDD 3V3 TO 5V5] --> G
    M[GND] --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram for Examination Use:**
> - **B1 (Capacitive Humidity Sensor):** Detects water vapor via polymer dielectric absorption.
> - **B2 (NTC Thermistor):** Detects temperature via resistance change.
> - **C1, C2:** Front-end signal conditioning circuits.
> - **E (ADC):** Converts analog sensor voltages to 8-bit digital counts.
> - **F (Calibration LUT):** Factory-stored compensation table for non-linearity.
> - **G (Embedded MCU):** Sequences the measurement, performs checksum, generates bit timing.
> - **H, I:** Single-wire bidirectional interface with open-drain output.
> - **J (Host MCU):** Initiates the transaction and decodes the incoming PWM.

## 4.2 Communication Protocol Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **chronological handshake sequence** between the host MCU and the DHT11 sensor. This is a *highly examinable diagram* in KTU 2024 ESE Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[HOST MCU POWER ON] --> P1[SET DATA PIN AS OUTPUT]
    P1 --> P2[PULL DATA LINE LOW FOR 18 MS]
    P2 --> P3[RELEASE LINE SET AS INPUT]
    P3 --> P4[WAIT 20 TO 40 US]
    P4 --> P5{DHT11 RESPONSE DETECTED}
    P5 -->|NO RESPONSE| P6[ERROR TIMEOUT RETRY AFTER 1 S]
    P5 -->|RESPONSE RECEIVED| P7[DHT11 SENDS LOW 80 US]
    P7 --> P8[DHT11 SENDS HIGH 80 US]
    P8 --> P9[TRANSMIT 40 BITS PWM ENCODED]
    P9 --> P10{BIT 0 DETECTED}
    P10 -->|HIGH 26 TO 28 US| P11[LOGIC BIT 0]
    P10 -->|HIGH 70 US| P12[LOGIC BIT 1]
    P11 --> P13[RECEIVE NEXT BIT]
    P12 --> P13
    P13 --> P14{BITS RECEIVED LESS THAN 40}
    P14 -->|YES| P10
    P14 -->|NO EQUALS 40| P15[ASSEMBLE 5 BYTES]
    P15 --> P16[CALCULATE CHECKSUM]
    P16 --> P17{CHECKSUM MATCHES}
    P17 -->|YES| P18[VALID DATA OUTPUT]
    P17 -->|NO| P19[DISCARD FRAME RETRY]
    P6 --> P1
    P19 --> P1
    P18 --> P20[WAIT 1 S BEFORE NEXT READ]
    P20 --> P1
```

## 4.3 External Pin Configuration Diagram

The DHT11 is available in two physical packages: a 4-pin single-row SIP and a 4-pin surface-mount variant. The following Mermaid block shows the **functional pin assignment** from a top-down perspective.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSOR[DHT11 TOP VIEW]
        direction LR
        PIN1[PIN 1 VDD POWER SUPPLY 3V3 TO 5V5]
        PIN2[PIN 2 DATA BIDIRECTIONAL SINGLE WIRE]
        PIN3[PIN 3 NC NOT CONNECTED]
        PIN4[PIN 4 GND GROUND REFERENCE]
    end
    PIN2 --> RES[PULL UP RESISTOR 4K7]
    RES --> VDD_EXT[VDD RAIL]
    PIN2 --> MCU[HOST MCU DATA PIN]
    PIN1 --> VDD_RAIL[VDD RAIL 3V3 OR 5V]
    PIN4 --> GND_RAIL[GROUND RAIL]
    PIN3 -.->|FLOATING| X[NC NO CONNECTION]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin Configuration Notes (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **Pin 1 (VDD):** Power supply input. Decoupling with a 100 nF ceramic capacitor close to the pin is mandatory.
> - **Pin 2 (DATA):** Bidirectional single-wire I/O. **Must have a pull-up resistor** (typically 4.7 kΩ for 5 V, or 10 kΩ for 3.3 V).
> - **Pin 3 (NC):** Leave floating. Do not connect to GND or VDD.
> - **Pin 4 (GND):** Reference ground.

## 4.4 Power-On Reset and Initialization Timing

The DHT11 requires a power-on stabilization time of **1 second** before the first valid reading. The following Mermaid block summarizes the timing constraints.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[POWER ON VDD RISES TO 3V3 OR 5V] --> B[WAIT 1 SECOND FOR INTERNAL CALIBRATION]
    B --> C[FIRST VALID READ TRANSACTION CAN BEGIN]
    C --> D[SUCCESSIVE READS MIN INTERVAL 1 SECOND]
    D --> E[HOLD DATA PIN HIGH DURING IDLE STATE]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]
**Q1.** Define the term **Relative Humidity (RH)** as measured by the DHT11 sensor. State the operating range of relative humidity specified in the DHT11 datasheet.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Relative Humidity (RH) is defined as the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor present in a given volume of air to the saturated vapor pressure of water at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage:

$$RH (\%) = \frac{p_{H_2O,actual}}{p_{H_2O,saturated}(T)} \times 100$$

**Operating Range (1 mark):** The DHT11 datasheet specifies the RH operating range as **20 % RH to 90 % RH** at a temperature of 0 °C to 50 °C.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is to state the range as "0 to 100 %". This is the **DHT22** range, not DHT11. The DHT11 cannot measure below 20 % RH. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this interchange.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]
**Q2.** With the help of a neat block diagram, list the **internal functional blocks** of the DHT11 sensor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The DHT11 consists of the following internal functional blocks (1½ marks for naming, 1½ marks for explaining connections):

1. **Capacitive humidity sensing element** (polymer dielectric)
2. **NTC thermistor** for temperature sensing
3. **Signal conditioning front-end** (oscillator + voltage divider)
4. **8-bit Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**
5. **Calibration Look-Up Table (LUT)** stored in non-volatile memory
6. **Embedded 8-bit microcontroller** for sequencing and data formatting
7. **Single-wire bidirectional interface** with open-drain output stage

The capacitive humidity sensor and NTC thermistor are multiplexed through a single ADC, and the resulting digital values are linearized using the factory calibration LUT before being formatted into the 40-bit frame and transmitted over the single-wire interface.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget the **calibration LUT** block, treating the DHT11 as a "raw" sensor. This is incorrect — the DHT11 is **factory-calibrated**, which is one of its principal advantages. Examiners specifically look for this point.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Modeled on KTU 2024 ESE Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE guidelines for *Sensors and Devices*, Part B Module-3 questions are worth 14 marks and typically have two sub-parts: **(a)** for 7 marks and **(b)** for 7 marks. Students are given an internal choice between two questions; we provide both choices below with complete model solutions.

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 3 Pattern]
**Q. (a)** Explain the **working principle of the DHT11 temperature and humidity sensor** with a block diagram. Discuss the role of the polymer capacitive sensor and the NTC thermistor in environmental measurement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The DHT11 is a calibrated digital-output sensor that measures both ambient temperature and relative humidity. It uses two distinct physical transduction principles and combines them through an internal microcontroller that delivers a single-wire serial digital output.

**Capacitive Humidity Sensing (3 marks):**
The humidity sensing element is a **capacitive polymer sensor** consisting of two conductive electrodes separated by a thin polymer dielectric film. The polymer is hygroscopic, meaning it absorbs and desorbs water molecules based on the surrounding humidity. As the polymer absorbs water, its dielectric constant changes (since water has a very high dielectric constant of ~80), which in turn changes the capacitance between the electrodes. The capacitance increases approximately linearly with relative humidity:

$$C(\phi) = C_0 \left[ 1 + k_\phi \phi \right]$$

where $C_0$ is the dry capacitance, $k_\phi$ is the sensitivity coefficient (in % per %RH), and $\phi$ is the relative humidity. This capacitance is converted into a voltage using a charge-pump or RC oscillator circuit, then digitized by the ADC.

**NTC Temperature Sensing (2 marks):**
The temperature is sensed by an **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) thermistor**, which is a semiconductor resistor whose resistance decreases as temperature rises. The resistance-temperature relationship is given by the β-parameter equation:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

where $R_0$ is the resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically 10 kΩ at 25 °C), $\beta$ is the material constant (~3950 K for the DHT11 NTC), and $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin. The thermistor is placed in a voltage divider, and the resulting voltage is digitized.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** [Refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram. Student must draw it in the answer sheet.]

**Conclusion (0 marks — implicit):**
The two digitized values are passed through the calibration LUT inside the embedded MCU, formatted into a 40-bit single-wire frame, and transmitted to the host. The DHT11 thus provides a *complete, calibrated, digital environmental reading* without any external signal conditioning.

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the **single-wire communication protocol** of the DHT11. With a clear timing diagram, explain how data bits 0 and 1 are distinguished on the bus. Show the format of the 40-bit data frame. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Single-Wire Protocol Overview (2 marks):**
The DHT11 uses a master-slave bidirectional single-wire protocol. The host MCU acts as the master, and the DHT11 acts as the slave. The bus is idle in the HIGH state (pulled up by an external 4.7 kΩ resistor). Each transaction consists of:
1. Host start signal
2. DHT11 response preamble
3. 40-bit data transmission
4. Bus returns to idle HIGH

**Start Signal (1 mark):**
The host pulls the DATA line LOW for **at least 18 ms** (typically 20 ms), then releases the line. The host then waits for 20–40 μs before the DHT11 begins responding.

**Response Preamble (1 mark):**
The DHT11 acknowledges by pulling the line LOW for 80 μs, then HIGH for 80 μs.

**Data Bit Encoding (2 marks):**
Each of the 40 data bits is encoded using pulse-width modulation on the time axis:
- The bit slot begins with a 50 μs LOW pulse.
- The bit slot ends with a HIGH pulse of variable duration:
  - **26–28 μs HIGH → bit = 0**
  - **70 μs HIGH → bit = 1**

**Data Frame Format (1 mark):**
The 40 bits are organized as:
- Byte 1: Integral RH
- Byte 2: Decimal RH (always 0 for DHT11)
- Byte 3: Integral Temperature
- Byte 4: Decimal Temperature (always 0 for DHT11)
- Byte 5: Checksum (sum of Bytes 1–4, lower 8 bits)

[Timing diagram must be hand-drawn in the answer sheet. See SECTION 4.2 for the textual representation.]

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 3 Pattern — Alternative Choice]
**Q. (a)** Draw the **pin configuration** of the DHT11 sensor. Explain the function of each pin and the role of the external pull-up resistor on the DATA line. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pin Diagram (2 marks):**
The DHT11 is a 4-pin device. Pin numbering is from left to right when viewed from the front (with the grille on top).

[Student should reproduce the SECTION 4.3 diagram in the answer sheet.]

**Pin Functions (3 marks):**
- **Pin 1 — VDD:** Power supply input (3.3 V to 5.5 V DC). A 100 nF decoupling capacitor must be placed as close to this pin as possible to suppress supply noise.
- **Pin 2 — DATA:** Bidirectional single-wire I/O. Used for both the host start signal and the DHT11 response/data transmission.
- **Pin 3 — NC (Not Connected):** Internally floating. Should not be connected to any net, especially not to VDD or GND, as it can act as an antenna and inject noise.
- **Pin 4 — GND:** Common ground reference, must be tied to the system ground.

**Role of the External Pull-up Resistor (2 marks):**
The DHT11 has an **open-drain** output stage on the DATA pin. This means the DHT11 can only pull the line LOW or release it; it cannot drive it HIGH actively. Therefore, an **external pull-up resistor** (typically 4.7 kΩ for 5 V operation, or 10 kΩ for 3.3 V) is required to pull the line HIGH during idle and after each pulse. Without this resistor:
- The line would float when released, causing spurious reads.
- The HIGH-level voltage may not reach the logic threshold of the host MCU.

The pull-up resistor value is a trade-off: smaller values give faster rise times (good for long cables) but increase current consumption; larger values reduce power but slow the rise time and limit cable length to < 20 m.

---

**Q. (b)** A DHT11 sensor returns the following 5-byte data frame on its single-wire bus:
- $B_1 = 55$, $B_2 = 0$, $B_3 = 28$, $B_4 = 0$, $B_5 = 83$

**Calculate:**
1. The relative humidity reported by the sensor.
2. The temperature reported by the sensor.
3. Verify the integrity of the received frame using the checksum.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Relative Humidity (2 marks):**
For the DHT11, the integer part of the relative humidity is $B_1$ and the decimal part is $B_2$:

$$RH = B_1 + B_2 \cdot 0.1$$

Substituting $B_1 = 55$ and $B_2 = 0$:

$$RH = 55 + 0 \cdot 0.1 = 55\ \%\text{RH}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature (2 marks):**
The integer part of the temperature is $B_3$ and the decimal part is $B_4$:

$$T = B_3 + B_4 \cdot 0.1$$

Substituting $B_3 = 28$ and $B_4 = 0$:

$$T = 28 + 0 \cdot 0.1 = 28\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Checksum Verification (3 marks):**
The checksum is calculated as the sum of the first 4 data bytes, taken modulo 256:

$$CS_{calc} = (B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4) \mod 256$$

Substituting the values:

$$CS_{calc} = (55 + 0 + 28 + 0) \mod 256$$

$$CS_{calc} = 83 \mod 256 = 83$$

The received checksum $B_5 = 83$. Comparing:

$$CS_{calc} = 83 \stackrel{?}{=} B_5 = 83 \quad \checkmark$$

Since the calculated checksum matches the received checksum, **the data frame is valid**.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{RH = 55\ \%\text{RH} \quad , \quad T = 28\ ^\circ\text{C} \quad , \quad \text{Frame is VALID}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Critical Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the modulo 256 operation:** Some students compute $B_1+B_2+B_3+B_4$ but skip the $\mod 256$. For DHT11, sums rarely exceed 255, so this is technically not an issue — but the **modulo operation is part of the protocol definition** and must be shown in your answer. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for omitting it.
> 2. **Confusing Bytes 1 and 2:** Many students think $B_2$ is the *decimal* humidity. While this is true in principle, the DHT11 **always sets $B_2 = 0$**. Treating $B_2$ as significant data will result in incorrect readings.
> 3. **Not stating the validity verdict explicitly:** The question asks to "verify" the checksum. You must explicitly write whether the frame is valid or not — do not just compute the number.
> 4. **Units:** Always append **%RH** and **°C**. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units, especially in numerical answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Entering the KTU Exam Hall**

- [ ] **DHT11** is a **digital-output**, single-wire, calibrated temperature and humidity sensor.
- [ ] Internal sensing elements: **capacitive polymer** for humidity + **NTC thermistor** for temperature.
- [ ] Operating voltage: **3.3 V to 5.5 V DC**.
- [ ] Operating ranges: **0–50 °C** for temperature, **20–90 % RH** for humidity.
- [ ] Accuracy: **±2 °C** and **±5 % RH**.
- [ ] Resolution: **1 °C** and **1 % RH** (no decimal places for DHT11).
- [ ] Pin count: **4 pins** (VDD, DATA, NC, GND).
- [ ] DATA line requires an **external pull-up resistor** (4.7 kΩ–10 kΩ).
- [ ] Communication: **single-wire, master-slave, bidirectional**.
- [ ] Data frame size: **40 bits = 5 bytes**.
- [ ] Byte order: **[RH_int, RH_dec, T_int, T_dec, Checksum]**.
- [ ] **RH_dec and T_dec are always 0 for DHT11**.
- [ ] Checksum: $(B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4) \mod 256$.
- [ ] Bit encoding uses **pulse-width modulation**: 26–28 μs = 0; 70 μs = 1 (after a 50 μs LOW preamble).
- [ ] Start signal from host: **LOW for ≥ 18 ms**.
- [ ] Response preamble from sensor: **80 μs LOW + 80 μs HIGH**.
- [ ] Minimum interval between two consecutive reads: **1 second**.
- [ ] DHT11 cannot measure humidity below **20 % RH** — do not confuse with DHT22.
- [ ] Cable length with pull-up: limited to **< 20 m**.
- [ ] Factory calibrated — **no external linearization required**.
- [ ] The internal LUT compensates for **non-linearity** of both sensing elements.
- [ ] The DHT11 is **slower** than I2C-based sensors (e.g., SHT31) but **simpler to interface** (only 1 data line).
- [ ] Sampling rate is limited by **physical polymer settling time**, not by electrical bandwidth.
- [ ] In KTU ESE, the most frequently asked sub-questions are: (i) working principle, (ii) pin diagram, (iii) checksum verification, (iv) single-wire timing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Note (Direct Quote Style):** *"The DHT11 question is the gateway question of Module 3. If you confuse DHT11 with DHT22, you will lose 2–3 marks instantly. Always cross-check: DHT11 = 1 °C resolution, 0–50 °C, 20–90 % RH, single-wire, no decimal."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Detection Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion Detection Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Motion Detection Sensor** is an electronic transducer that converts a physical change in the position, velocity, or acceleration of an object (or a person) within a defined surveillance zone into a corresponding measurable electrical signal. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for *Sensors and Devices*, motion sensors are broadly classified into **passive devices** (which only receive energy from the target, e.g., Passive Infrared) and **active devices** (which transmit a probing signal and observe its interaction with the target, e.g., Ultrasonic and Microwave Doppler sensors).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The three primary motion-sensing technologies covered in Module 3 are the **Pyroelectric Infrared (PIR) sensor**, the **Ultrasonic (acoustic) sensor**, and the **Microwave Doppler (radar) sensor**. Accelerometer-based vibration sensors are also covered as a complementary category.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Tripwire"

Imagine you are standing in a dark room and want to know if someone walks past. You have three options:

1. **Feel the heat they radiate** → This is exactly how a **PIR sensor** works. Every human body emits infrared energy centered near **9.4 µm** (peak blackbody wavelength at 310 K). The PIR sensor is like feeling warmth brush past your hand.
2. **Listen to an echo of your own shout** → This is how an **ultrasonic sensor** works. It "shouts" a **40 kHz** chirp and "listens" for the echo. The time delay tells you the distance, while a shift in echo pitch tells you the speed (Doppler effect).
3. **Use a flashlight of radio waves** → This is how a **microwave Doppler sensor** works. It sends a **2.4 GHz / 10.525 GHz** radio beam; when a person walks into the beam, the reflected wave's frequency is shifted. It is the same principle as the police radar gun.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light (vacuum) | c | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | Used for microwave/IR calculations |
| Speed of sound (air, 20 °C) | v_s | **343 m/s** | Used for ultrasonic calculations |
| Peak human body IR wavelength | λ_IR | **9.4 µm** | From Wien's displacement law at 310 K |
| Standard human skin temperature | T_body | **306.5 K** (≈ 33.5 °C) | Surface radiation temperature |
| PIR response window | λ | **8 – 14 µm** | Atmospheric IR transmission window |
| Common ultrasonic Tx frequency | f₀ | **40 kHz** | Above human hearing (~20 kHz) |
| Common microwave bands | f₀ | **2.45, 5.8, 10.525 GHz** | ISM and police radar bands |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Frequency of Human Hearing = 20 kHz.** Any ultrasonic sensor must therefore operate strictly above this value, with **40 kHz** being the industrial-standard choice because it balances absorption losses in air (low at 40 kHz) against beam directivity (sharper at higher frequencies).

## 1.4 Why Motion Detection is Different from Position Detection

A position sensor asks "**where** is the object?". A motion sensor asks "**has something changed position, and how fast?**". The fundamental signal of interest is therefore a **rate of change** (derivative) — a temperature gradient, a frequency shift, or an acceleration. This is why almost all motion sensors use either a **differential measurement** (PIR with two crystals) or a **Doppler-shift measurement** (ultrasonic, microwave, radar).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pyroelectric Infrared (PIR) Sensor

### 2.1.1 The Pyroelectric Effect
Certain crystals (commonly **Lithium Tantalate — LiTaO₃** or **Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT**) possess a permanent spontaneous electric polarization **P**. When the crystal temperature changes, the polarization changes proportionally:

$$\Delta P = p \cdot \Delta T$$

where *p* is the **pyroelectric coefficient** (C·m⁻²·K⁻¹). This polarization change appears as a surface charge that can be measured as a voltage across a high-impedance load.

### 2.1.2 Why a Dual-Element Design?

A single pyroelectric element would trigger on *any* temperature change — sunlight, air-conditioner draft, sunlight through a cloud. To reject these common-mode signals, commercial PIR sensors (e.g., HC-SR501, Panasonic EKMC) use **two identical crystals wired in series-opposing polarity**. The output is the **differential** of the two signals:

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{elem1}} - V_{\text{elem2}}$$

When ambient temperature changes uniformly, both elements respond identically, and the differential output remains **zero**. A walking person, however, illuminates element 1 first and element 2 a fraction of a second later, producing a **non-zero AC signal**.

### 2.1.3 The Fresnel Lens

A standard PIR lens is a **Fresnel lens** (segmented, low-cost aspheric lens) made of polyethylene, transparent to IR (8–14 µm). Its jobs are:

1. **Focus** the IR radiation from a wide field-of-view (typically 110°) onto the small pyroelectric element.
2. **Segment** the field of view into multiple alternating "zones" of high and low sensitivity. As a person walks across these zones, the differential signal **alternates in sign**, producing a clean AC waveform suitable for triggering a comparator.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common mistake is to call the Fresnel lens an "amplifier". It does not amplify the IR; it merely *concentrates* and *chops* the field of view to create motion signatures.

## 2.2 Ultrasonic Motion Sensor

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle
An ultrasonic sensor (e.g., HC-SR04) has a **piezoelectric ceramic transducer** that resonates at **40 kHz**. The device operates in two modes:

- **Pulse-Echo (Time of Flight) mode:** Measures **distance** to objects.
- **Continuous-Wave Doppler mode:** Measures **velocity** of moving objects.

### 2.2.2 Time of Flight
The transducer emits a short burst of ultrasound, which travels to the target and reflects. The round-trip time *t* gives the distance:

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$$

The factor **2** is the round-trip factor — sound travels to the target and back, so the total path is twice the distance.

### 2.2.3 Doppler Shift for Velocity
If the target is moving with velocity *v* (radial component), the reflected wave is shifted in frequency. The Doppler shift for a stationary transmitter and moving target is:

$$f_d = \frac{2 \, v \, f_0}{v_s}$$

This small shift (a few hundred Hz) is mixed with the transmitted frequency in a non-linear detector to produce an audible beat note, hence the name **"bat detector"**.

## 2.3 Microwave / Doppler Radar Motion Sensor

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
A Gunn diode or dielectric resonator oscillator (DRO) generates a microwave carrier at **f₀** (e.g., **10.525 GHz**). This is split into two paths: one goes to a transmitting antenna (microstrip patch or horn), the other serves as the local oscillator for a Schottky-diode mixer. The transmitted wave reflects off a moving target, and the received echo is mixed with the LO. The mixer output is the **Doppler beat frequency**:

$$f_d = \frac{2 \, v \, f_0}{c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Many students incorrectly write the radar Doppler equation as a *one-way* shift. The correct formula is **two-way** (factor of 2), because the wave undergoes *two* Doppler shifts — once when the moving target "observes" the transmitted wave, and once when the receiver "observes" the re-radiated wave from the moving target.

### 2.3.2 The Radar Range Equation
The maximum detection range of a Doppler radar is governed by the **Radar Range Equation**:

$$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, P_{r,\min}} \right]^{1/4}$$

where:
- **P_t** = transmitted power (W)
- **G_t, G_r** = transmit and receive antenna gains (linear, not dB)
- **λ** = wavelength (m)
- **σ** = radar cross-section of target (m²)
- **P_{r,min}** = minimum detectable received power (W)
- **(4π)³** = 4π from spherical spreading (one-way) × 4π from radar geometry × 4π from aperture-to-gain conversion

A typical adult human has σ ≈ **0.5 – 1.0 m²** at 10 GHz; at 2.4 GHz this drops to ≈ **0.15 m²** because the body is no longer electrically large.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Sensor | Governing Equation | Key Variables | Typical Numerical Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pyroelectric charge | $\Delta Q = p \, A \, \Delta T$ | p, A, ΔT | p ≈ 2 × 10⁻⁸ C/(cm²·K) for LiTaO₃ | IR power → voltage |
| PIR differential output | $V_{\text{out}} = G \, (V_1 - V_2)$ | amplifier gain G | G ≈ 1000 – 5000 | Motion/noise rejection |
| Ultrasonic time-of-flight | $d = v_s \, t \, / \, 2$ | sound speed, time | v_s = 343 m/s @ 20°C | Distance to object |
| Ultrasonic Doppler | $f_d = 2 \, v \, f_0 \, / \, v_s$ | target velocity, f₀ | f₀ = 40 kHz, v_s = 343 m/s | Speed of target |
| Microwave Doppler | $f_d = 2 \, v \, f_0 \, / \, c$ | target velocity, f₀ | f₀ = 10.525 GHz, c = 3×10⁸ m/s | Speed of target |
| Radar maximum range | $R_{\max} = \left[ P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma \, / \, ((4\pi)^3 P_{r,\min}) \right]^{1/4}$ | powers, gains, λ, σ | (4π)³ ≈ 1984 | Maximum detection distance |
| PIR detection range | $R \approx \sqrt{(A_{\text{lens}} \, f_{\text{number}}^{-2})}$ | lens area, f-number | typical 5–12 m | Lens design |
| Antenna beamwidth | $\theta \approx 70 \, \lambda \, / \, D$ | wavelength, antenna diameter | λ = 0.028 m @ 10.525 GHz | Beam focusing |
| Human Doppler @ 10.525 GHz (walking 1 m/s) | $f_d$ numerical | v = 1 m/s, c, f₀ | f_d ≈ **70.2 Hz** | Beat frequency in mixer |
| Human Doppler @ 40 kHz (walking 1 m/s) | $f_d$ numerical | v = 1 m/s, v_s, f₀ | f_d ≈ **233 Hz** | Audible beat note |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The human audible range is 20 Hz – 20 kHz. Both Doppler shifts computed above (70 Hz, 233 Hz) lie comfortably in the audible band, which is why motion-sensor output signals are often amplified and played through a speaker — a famous "ghost detector" effect.

## 2.5 Comparative Engineering Utility

| Application | Best Sensor Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Indoor security (home) | PIR | Cheap, low power, no radiation hazard |
| Outdoor perimeter (rain/fog) | Microwave | Penetrates fog, rain, dust |
| Car reversing / parking | Ultrasonic | High accuracy at short range, cheap |
| Highway speed gun | Microwave Doppler (24 / 35 GHz) | Long range, narrow beam, high velocity resolution |
| Open door automation | PIR + Ultrasonic hybrid | PIR detects presence; ultrasonic measures approach |
| Smartphone screen rotation | Accelerometer (MEMS) | Detects tilt/motion of the device itself, not environment |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **exhaustive and self-contained**. Every algebraic transition is justified. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Doppler Shift for a Moving Target (Electromagnetic / Microwave Case)

### Step 1 — Set up the problem

Consider a stationary transmitter sending a continuous wave of frequency **f₀** and wavelength **λ** toward a target moving away with radial velocity **v** (with **v ≪ c**). The receiver is co-located with the transmitter (monostatic radar).

### Step 2 — Frequency observed at the target

In the target's reference frame (moving at velocity *v* away from the source), the wave appears to have a stretched wavelength (the source is "running away" from the wavefronts it has just emitted). The wavelength observed by the target is:

$$\lambda' = \lambda + v \cdot T_0 = \lambda + \frac{v}{f_0}$$

The frequency in the target's frame is then:

$$f' = \frac{c}{\lambda'} = \frac{c}{\lambda + v/f_0} = \frac{c}{c/f_0 + v/f_0} = \frac{c \, f_0}{c + v} = f_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + v/c}$$

### Step 3 — Taylor expansion for v ≪ c

Use the binomial expansion $\dfrac{1}{1+x} \approx 1 - x$ for small *x*:

$$f' \approx f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v}{c}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Frequency of the re-radiated wave at the receiver

The target re-radiates the wave as if it were a transmitter moving *toward* the receiver (because it is moving away from the source, which is the same point as the receiver in monostatic radar). Applying the standard moving-source Doppler formula:

$$f'' = \frac{f'}{1 - v/c} \approx f' \left(1 + \frac{v}{c}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 4

$$f'' \approx f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v}{c}\right) \left(1 + \frac{v}{c}\right) = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v^2}{c^2}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Neglect the second-order term (v ≪ c)

Since v²/c² is negligible (for v = 1 m/s, v²/c² ≈ 10⁻¹⁷), we obtain:

$$f'' \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v}{c} - \frac{v}{c} - \frac{v^2}{c^2}\right) + \text{2nd order}$$

Wait — re-checking Step 6: $(1 - v/c)(1 + v/c) = 1 - v^2/c^2$. Therefore the leading-order (linear) terms *cancel*. We must retain the next order. Going back one step more carefully:

Re-doing Steps 3 and 4 to higher order:

$$f' = f_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + v/c} \approx f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v}{c} + \frac{v^2}{c^2} - \cdots\right)$$

$$f'' = f' \cdot \frac{1}{1 - v/c} \approx f' \left(1 + \frac{v}{c} + \frac{v^2}{c^2} + \cdots\right)$$

Multiplying:

$$f'' = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v}{c} + \frac{v^2}{c^2}\right)\left(1 + \frac{v}{c} + \frac{v^2}{c^2}\right)$$

To second order:

$$f'' = f_0 \left[1 - \frac{v^2}{c^2} + \frac{v^2}{c^2} - \frac{v^2}{c^2} + O(v^3/c^3)\right]$$

A more careful algebra gives, exactly:

$$f'' = f_0 \cdot \frac{1 + v/c}{1 - v/c} = f_0 \cdot \frac{1 - v^2/c^2 + 2v/c}{1 - v^2/c^2} \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2v}{c}\right)$$

### Step 7 — Final Doppler shift expression

$$\boxed{f_d = f'' - f_0 = \frac{2 \, v \, f_0}{c}}$$

This is the **two-way (radar) Doppler equation**, and it is the only form acceptable in KTU board answers for microwave / radar motion sensors.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Radar Range Equation

### Step 1 — Power density at the target

The transmitting antenna radiates isotropically, then concentrates energy into a solid angle determined by the gain G_t. The power density (W/m²) at range R is:

$$S_t = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2}$$

### Step 2 — Power intercepted and re-radiated by the target

The target re-radiates a fraction of the intercepted power, characterized by its radar cross-section σ (m²). The re-radiated power isotropically isotropically, treated as though the target were a point source:

$$P_{\text{scatter}} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}$$

### Step 3 — Power density at the receiver

The scattered wave spreads spherically. The power density back at the receiver (at distance R) is:

$$S_r = \frac{P_{\text{scatter}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)(4\pi R^2)} = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$$

### Step 4 — Effective aperture of the receiving antenna

The receiving antenna captures a fraction of this power. The relationship between antenna gain and effective aperture is:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

### Step 5 — Received power

Multiplying the power density at the receiver by the effective aperture:

$$P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the maximum range

For reliable detection, the received power must equal or exceed the **minimum detectable signal** $P_{r,\min}$ (set by the receiver's noise floor and required SNR). Setting $P_r = P_{r,\min}$ and solving for R:

$$R_{\max}^4 = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, P_{r,\min}}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, P_{r,\min}} \right]^{1/4}}$$

### Step 7 — Numerical illustration

Suppose a 10.525 GHz motion sensor has:
- P_t = 10 mW = 0.01 W
- G_t = G_r = 20 (linear, ≈ 13 dBi)
- σ = 1 m² (adult human)
- λ = c/f₀ = 3×10⁸ / 10.525×10⁹ = 0.0285 m → λ² ≈ 8.13×10⁻⁴ m²
- P_{r,min} = -90 dBm = 10⁻¹² W

Compute numerator: 0.01 × 20 × 20 × 8.13×10⁻⁴ × 1 = 3.25×10⁻³

Compute denominator: (4π)³ × 10⁻¹² ≈ 1984 × 10⁻¹² ≈ 1.98×10⁻⁹

Ratio: 3.25×10⁻³ / 1.98×10⁻⁹ ≈ 1.64×10⁶

Fourth root: (1.64×10⁶)^(1/4) = (1.64×10⁶)^0.25

Let *x* = 1.64×10⁶. log₁₀(x) = 6.215. log₁₀(x)/4 = 1.554. So x^(1/4) = 10^1.554 ≈ **35.8 m**.

So this modest 10 mW sensor can detect an adult at ≈ **36 m** — a respectable range for indoor/outdoor motion detection.

## 3.3 Derivation of Ultrasonic Doppler Equation

The acoustic case is non-relativistic. Apply the classical Doppler formula (target acts as moving observer, then as moving source):

$$f' = f_0 \, \frac{v_s + v}{v_s} \quad \text{(target as moving observer)}$$

$$f'' = f' \, \frac{v_s}{v_s - v} = f_0 \, \frac{v_s + v}{v_s - v} \quad \text{(target as moving source re-emitting)}$$

For v ≪ v_s, expand using the identity $\dfrac{1+a}{1-a} \approx 1 + 2a$ for small a:

$$f'' = f_0 \cdot \frac{1 + v/v_s}{1 - v/v_s} \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2v}{v_s}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{f_d = \frac{2 \, v \, f_0}{v_s}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | PIR | Ultrasonic | Microwave Doppler |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Passive (target's IR) | Active (40 kHz acoustic) | Active (2.4/10.5 GHz EM) |
| Penetration of walls | No | No | **Yes (thin walls, glass)** |
| Works in vacuum | No (IR is EM, so yes in principle, but no target heat) | **No** (no medium) | **Yes** (vacuum is transparent to microwaves) |
| Affected by sunlight | Slightly | No | No |
| Affected by fog/rain | No | **Yes (heavy absorption)** | No |
| Range (typical) | 5 – 12 m | 0.02 – 5 m | 10 – 50 m |
| Cost (relative) | Lowest | Low | Medium |
| Privacy concerns | None | None | **Some** (can sense through thin walls) |
| Power consumption | µA range | mA range | 10–100 mA |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Physical drawings of optical/acoustic systems cannot be rendered by Mermaid. The diagrams below are **block-level functional architecture flows** showing the precise signal path, stage-by-stage, from physical phenomenon to electrical output. These mirror the block diagrams typically expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

## 4.1 PIR Sensor — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stg1a[Human Body 310 K Emits IR at 9.4 micrometer] --> stg1b
    stg1b[Fresnel Lens Polyethylene 8 to 14 micrometer Passband] --> stg1c
    stg1c[Pyroelectric Element 1 LiTaO3 Crystal] --> stg1d
    stg1e[Pyroelectric Element 2 LiTaO3 Crystal Opposite Polarity] --> stg1d
    stg1d[Differential Buffer FET Source Follower] --> stg1f
    stg1f[Bandpass Filter 0.1 to 10 Hz Typical] --> stg1g
    stg1g[BJT Gain Stage 60 to 80 dB Total] --> stg1h
    stg1h[Window Comparator Schmitt Trigger] --> stg1i
    stg1i[Output Driver TTL HIGH for 2 to 5 seconds]
```

**Stage-by-stage function:**

1. **IR Source:** Human body acts as a blackbody at ~310 K; peak emission at 9.4 µm (Wien's law).
2. **Fresnel Lens:** Segments the field of view into alternating sensitivity zones; focuses IR onto the dual element.
3. **Pyroelectric Crystals (×2):** Convert incident IR (heat) into surface charge changes.
4. **Differential Buffer:** Rejects common-mode signals (sunlight, AC drift); passes only AC motion signals.
5. **Bandpass Filter:** Restricts response to walking-speed frequencies (0.1 – 10 Hz); rejects 50/60 Hz line noise.
6. **BJT Gain Stage:** Amplifies micro-volt-level signals to volt levels.
7. **Comparator + Output Driver:** Converts analog motion signal to clean digital HIGH for downstream microcontroller.

## 4.2 Ultrasonic Motion Sensor (HC-SR04 style) — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stg2a[Microcontroller Sends 10 microsecond Trigger Pulse] --> stg2b
    stg2b[Oscillator Generates 40 kHz Burst of 8 Cycles] --> stg2c
    stg2c[Piezo Transmitter Resonant at 40 kHz Emits Ultrasound] --> stg2d
    stg2d[Air Propagation at 343 m per second] --> stg2e
    stg2e[Strikes Object and Reflects Echo] --> stg2f
    stg2f[Piezo Receiver Resonant at 40 kHz Converts Echo to Voltage] --> stg2g
    stg2g[Low Noise Preamplifier 40 dB] --> stg2h
    stg2h[Bandpass Filter 38 to 42 kHz] --> stg2i
    stg2i[Envelope Detector or Comparator] --> stg2j
    stg2j[Timer Measures Echo Pulse Width t] --> stg2k
    stg2k[Output Distance d equals v sub s times t divided by 2]
```

**Stage-by-stage function:**

1. **Trigger:** Host microcontroller initiates ranging with a short TTL pulse.
2. **40 kHz Burst:** 8-cycle tone at the transducer's mechanical resonance maximizes acoustic power.
3. **Transmit:** Piezo ceramic vibrates at 40 kHz, producing a directional acoustic beam.
4. **Propagation:** Sound travels to the target at v_s = 343 m/s.
5. **Reflection:** Echo returns from the object (specular or diffuse).
6. **Receive:** The same transducer (or a second one) converts the acoustic echo back to a voltage.
7. **Preamplifier + BPF:** Boosts the weak echo and rejects ambient noise.
8. **Envelope Detector:** Recovers the baseband echo envelope.
9. **Timer:** Microcontroller measures the echo pulse width *t* and computes d.

## 4.3 Microwave Doppler Motion Sensor — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stg3a[Voltage Controlled Oscillator DRO at 10.525 GHz] --> stg3b
    stg3b[Power Splitter Wilkinson Divider] --> stg3c
    stg3b --> stg3d
    stg3c[Microstrip Patch Transmit Antenna] --> stg3e
    stg3e[Beam Propagates at 3 times 10 to 8 m per second] --> stg3f
    stg3f[Strikes Moving Person Doppler Shift f sub d] --> stg3g
    stg3g[Microstrip Patch Receive Antenna] --> stg3h
    stg3h[Low Noise Amplifier LNA 2.4 dB Noise Figure] --> stg3i
    stg3i[Schottky Diode Mixer Multiplies with LO from Splitter] --> stg3j
    stg3j[Low Pass Filter 10 Hz to 10 kHz Passband] --> stg3k
    stg3k[Audio Amplifier and Comparator] --> stg3l
    stg3l[Output Drives Relay Microcontroller or Alarm]
```

**Stage-by-stage function:**

1. **Oscillator (DRO/Gunn):** Generates a stable microwave carrier at 10.525 GHz.
2. **Power Splitter:** Divides the carrier into TX path and LO path for the mixer.
3. **TX Antenna:** Radiates a narrow beam (~30° typical) toward the surveillance zone.
4. **Beam Propagation:** Microwave beam travels to the target.
5. **Doppler Shift:** A walking person (v ≈ 1 m/s) produces f_d = 2(1)(10.525×10⁹)/(3×10⁸) ≈ **70.2 Hz**.
6. **RX Antenna:** Captures the shifted echo.
7. **LNA:** Boosts the weak echo above the mixer's noise floor.
8. **Schottky Mixer:** Multiplies RX and LO → produces sum and difference frequencies. The difference (Doppler beat at ~70 Hz) is the desired signal.
9. **LPF + Audio Amplifier:** Extracts the Doppler beat and amplifies it to a usable level.
10. **Comparator/Relay:** Triggers an alarm or light when beat is present.

## 4.4 Side-by-Side Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cmpA[PIR Passive No Tx Antenna Needed] --> cmpA1[Cheap Low Power]
    cmpB[Ultrasonic Active Acoustic Tx and Rx] --> cmpB1[Short Range Narrow Cone]
    cmpC[Microwave Active EM Tx and Rx] --> cmpC1[Long Range Penetrates Walls]
    cmpA1 --> cmpA2[Indoor Security Lights]
    cmpB1 --> cmpB2[Parking Sensors Robotics]
    cmpC2 --> cmpC3[Perimeter Security Speed Guns]
    cmpC1 --> cmpC2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a motion sensor and list any four different types. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

**Model Answer:**

A motion sensor is an electronic device that detects physical movement of an object or person and produces a corresponding electrical output. The four principal types are:

1. **Passive Infrared (PIR) sensor** — detects IR radiation emitted by a warm body.
2. **Ultrasonic sensor** — uses 40 kHz sound waves and measures echo time/frequency shift.
3. **Microwave / Doppler radar sensor** — uses GHz radio waves and Doppler shift.
4. **Accelerometer (MEMS)** — detects acceleration of the device itself, often used in motion-triggered devices.

> [!NOTE]
> Vibration sensors (piezoelectric) and LIDAR-based sensors are also valid answers.

---

### Q2. State and explain the principle of Doppler effect as applied to a microwave motion sensor. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Doppler effect** is the apparent change in frequency of a wave when there is relative motion between the source and observer. In a microwave motion sensor, a continuous wave of frequency **f₀** is transmitted toward the surveillance zone. If a person moves with radial velocity **v** (where v ≪ c), the reflected wave returns with a slightly different frequency **f₀ + f_d**, where:

$$f_d = \frac{2 \, v \, f_0}{c}$$

The factor of 2 accounts for the **two-way** Doppler shift (transmission and reflection). For example, a person walking at 1 m/s in a 10.525 GHz field produces f_d ≈ 70.2 Hz, which lies in the audible range and can be detected after mixing with the original carrier.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, each Part-B question in the End Semester Examination has two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

### ▸ Option A (14 Marks) — *PIR and Radar*

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Pyroelectric Infrared (PIR) motion sensor. Discuss the role of the Fresnel lens and the dual-element configuration. (7 marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** A PIR sensor is a *passive* device — it does not emit any energy of its own but senses the IR radiation emitted by a warm body. It is widely used in automatic lighting, security alarms, and energy-saving systems.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the architecture shown in Section 4.1 above. The signal flow is: *Human IR → Fresnel lens → Dual pyroelectric elements → Differential buffer → Bandpass filter → BJT gain stage → Comparator → Digital output.*

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- The human body at ~310 K emits blackbody radiation with peak wavelength λ_max ≈ 9.4 µm (Wien's law: λ_max T = 2.898×10⁻³ m·K).
- The **Fresnel lens** focuses this IR onto a small pyroelectric crystal (commonly LiTaO₃) and simultaneously segments the field of view into multiple zones.
- The crystal is split into **two series-opposing elements**. The output is the **difference** of the two signals. This common-mode rejection eliminates false triggers from uniform background changes (sunlight drift, AC drafts).
- As a person walks across the lens zones, the IR image sweeps from element 1 to element 2, generating an **alternating differential signal** in the 0.1–10 Hz range.
- This is amplified (60–80 dB), filtered, and fed to a comparator that produces a clean TTL HIGH for 2–5 seconds (adjustable).

**Role of the Fresnel lens (1 mark):** Focuses IR, segments the field of view into zones to convert slow motion into a clean AC signal, and provides a wide field of view (typically 110°) with a small, cheap lens.

**Role of the dual-element design (1 mark):** Common-mode rejection — eliminates false triggers from uniform ambient changes; only differential motion produces an output.

---

#### (b) Derive the radar range equation and explain the significance of each term. (7 marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (5 marks):** Refer to the step-by-step derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes. The final result is:

$$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, P_{r,\min}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Significance of each term (2 marks):**
- **P_t (transmitted power):** Higher P_t → larger range, but limited by FCC/regulatory power masks.
- **G_t, G_r (antenna gains):** Higher gain → narrower beam → more energy on target, but smaller coverage area.
- **λ (wavelength):** Shorter λ → better resolution but worse fog/rain penetration and lower RCS for humans.
- **σ (radar cross-section):** Depends on target's electrical size, shape, and material. Adult human ≈ 0.5 – 1.0 m² at 10 GHz.
- **P_{r,min} (minimum detectable power):** Set by receiver noise floor and required SNR. Lowering P_{r,min} (e.g., by cryogenic cooling, lower-noise LNAs) extends range.
- **(4π)³ factor:** Represents the two-way spherical spreading and the antenna gain–aperture conversion.

**Typical numerical example:** A 10 mW, 10.525 GHz sensor with 13 dBi antennas can detect a walking human at ≈ 36 m, illustrating the impressive sensitivity of microwave Doppler sensors.

---

### ▸ Option B (14 Marks) — *Ultrasonic and Microwave*

#### (a) Explain the working principle of an ultrasonic motion sensor. Derive the time-of-flight equation and the Doppler shift equation. (7 marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):** An ultrasonic sensor uses a piezoelectric ceramic transducer (typically lead zirconate titanate, PZT) that mechanically resonates at **40 kHz**, producing a directional acoustic beam. The transducer alternates between **transmit** and **receive** modes, measuring either the round-trip time of an echo (*time of flight, TOF*) or the frequency shift of the reflected wave (*Doppler mode*).

**Time-of-Flight Derivation (3 marks):**
Let *t* be the round-trip time for sound to travel to a target at distance *d* and return. The total path length is 2d, so:

$$2d = v_s \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{d = \frac{v_s \, t}{2}}$$

For v_s = 343 m/s and t measured in microseconds: **d (m) = 0.01715 × t (µs)**.

**Doppler Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.3:

$$f_d = \frac{2 \, v \, f_0}{v_s}$$

For f₀ = 40 kHz and a person walking at 1 m/s, f_d ≈ **233 Hz** (audible).

---

#### (b) With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a microwave Doppler motion sensor. Compare it with a PIR sensor. (7 marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 — VCO/DRO → Power splitter → TX antenna → Space → Target → RX antenna → LNA → Mixer → LPF → Audio amplifier → Comparator/relay.

**Working (3 marks):**
- A dielectric resonator oscillator (DRO) or Gunn diode generates a stable microwave carrier at f₀ (e.g., 10.525 GHz).
- A Wilkinson power splitter feeds the TX antenna and provides a local-oscillator reference to a Schottky-diode mixer.
- The TX antenna radiates a narrow beam. A moving person reflects the beam with a Doppler shift f_d = 2vf₀/c.
- The RX antenna receives the shifted echo. The mixer multiplies RX and LO, producing sum and difference frequencies. The difference (the Doppler beat at audio frequencies, e.g., 70 Hz for a walking person) is extracted by a low-pass filter and amplified.
- A comparator triggers an alarm/relay when the beat is present.

**Comparison with PIR (2 marks):**

| Aspect | PIR | Microwave Doppler |
|---|---|---|
| Energy emitted | None (passive) | Continuous microwave (active) |
| Penetration | No (line of sight) | Yes (thin walls, glass) |
| Weather immunity | Excellent | Excellent |
| Range | 5 – 12 m | 10 – 50 m |
| Cost | Very low | Moderate |
| Privacy concerns | None | Some (can sense through walls) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the Doppler equation.** Always write f_d = 2vf₀/c (or 2vf₀/v_s). Writing f_d = vf₀/c is a **3-mark deduction** in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Confusing passive and active sensors.** PIR is *passive* (no emission). Saying "PIR emits IR" is a fundamental conceptual error. Deduct **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Fresnel lens's role of *segmenting* the field of view.** Many students mention only the focusing role and miss the alternating-zones function that converts motion into AC signal. Deduct **1 mark**.
> 4. **Mixing up v_s (speed of sound) and c (speed of light).** Doppler shift formulas for ultrasonic use v_s = 343 m/s; for microwave, c = 3×10⁸ m/s. Interchanging these gives wrong numerical values; typical deduction **2 marks**.
> 5. **Not stating the standard reference conditions.** For v_s, always specify "at 20 °C in dry air" or you'll lose ½ mark on definition completeness.
> 6. **Omitting the radar cross-section units (m²) and the (4π)³ factor explanation** in the radar range equation. KTU examiners specifically check the (4π)³ derivation in long-answer questions; missing the geometric reasoning costs **2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Motion Detection Sensors**

- ☐ A **motion sensor** detects a *change* in position, velocity, or acceleration — not absolute position.
- ☐ Three primary technologies: **PIR (passive IR)**, **Ultrasonic (active acoustic)**, **Microwave Doppler (active EM)**.
- ☐ The **pyroelectric effect** is the change of spontaneous polarization with temperature; key material is **LiTaO₃**.
- ☐ PIR sensors use **two pyroelectric elements in series-opposing** configuration to reject common-mode signals.
- ☐ The **Fresnel lens** in a PIR sensor has *two* jobs: **focus** and **segment the field of view** into alternating zones.
- ☐ Standard ultrasonic frequency is **40 kHz** (well above 20 kHz human hearing threshold).
- ☐ The **radar Doppler equation** is **two-way**: f_d = 2v·f₀/c. Always include the factor of 2.
- ☐ The **ultrasonic Doppler equation** is f_d = 2v·f₀/v_s, where v_s = **343 m/s** at 20 °C.
- ☐ The **radar range equation** has a **fourth-root** dependence on power and a **fourth-root** dependence on σ.
- ☐ A walking human (1 m/s) at 10.525 GHz produces f_d ≈ **70.2 Hz** — in the audible band.
- ☐ A walking human (1 m/s) at 40 kHz ultrasonic produces f_d ≈ **233 Hz** — in the audible band.
- ☐ The **(4π)³ factor** in the radar range equation represents two-way spherical spreading + antenna gain–aperture conversion.
- ☐ **Microwave Doppler** is the only motion-sensing technology that penetrates thin non-metallic walls.
- ☐ **Ultrasonic** is the only motion-sensing technology that *fails in vacuum* (no medium for sound).
- ☐ **PIR** is the cheapest and lowest-power; ideal for battery-powered IoT motion detectors.
- ☐ MEMS **accelerometers** are *device-mounted* motion sensors, not surveillance sensors — different use case.
- ☐ Police **speed guns** operate at **24 GHz, 35 GHz, or 76–77 GHz** — all microwave Doppler variants.
- ☐ The **atmospheric IR window (8 – 14 µm)** is what makes PIR sensing of human body heat practical.
- ☐ **Wien's law**: λ_max · T = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K → λ_max for human body (310 K) ≈ 9.4 µm.
- ☐ **Schottky diode mixers** are the standard in microwave Doppler sensors due to their fast switching and low noise.
- ☐ **Fresnel zones** in a PIR lens create *alternating* high/low sensitivity areas — motion through these creates a clean AC waveform.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Bluetooth Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT725 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wireless Bluetooth Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Wireless Bluetooth Sensors** are intelligent, low-power transducer nodes that integrate a physical/chemical sensing element with a Bluetooth radio transceiver compliant with the IEEE 802.15.1 standard, enabling short-range, license-free wireless communication within the global **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific and Medical (ISM)** radio band. These sensors leverage **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** with **79 hop carriers** and a nominal hop rate of **1600 hops/second** to mitigate interference and provide robust connectivity for Internet-of-Things (IoT), healthcare, and industrial monitoring applications.

Under the KTU 2024 OECIT725 syllabus (Module 3 — *Wireless & Optical Sensors*), Bluetooth sensors form a critical bridge between physical-world measurement and the wireless cyber infrastructure. The modern implementation predominantly uses **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** — introduced in the Bluetooth 4.0 Core Specification (2010) — to satisfy the ultra-low-power demands of battery-operated sensor nodes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Walkie-Talkie in a Crowded Mall"

Imagine you and your friend trying to have a private conversation inside a noisy, crowded shopping mall. If both of you stay on a single fixed channel, dozens of other conversations will drown you out. The Bluetooth solution is the equivalent of **rapidly switching between 79 different corners of the mall 1600 times per second**, speaking just a tiny fragment of your sentence at each corner. Because no eavesdropper can follow you that fast, your conversation remains private and immune to interference. That is exactly what **FHSS** does for the sensor — its transmissions "hop" across 79 MHz-wide channels, dodging interference from Wi-Fi, microwaves, and other Bluetooth devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bluetooth for sensors?**
> - Operates in the **unlicensed 2.4 GHz ISM band** (no regulatory fees)
> - Native interoperability with **smartphones, tablets, and laptops** (no gateway hardware needed)
> - **Ultra-low-power** variants (BLE) enabling coin-cell battery lifetimes of **1–10 years**
> - Built-in **AES-128 encryption** and adaptive power control
> - Compact, low-cost System-on-Chip (SoC) implementations (e.g., Nordic nRF52, TI CC2650)

## 1.3 Standard Bluetooth Operating Parameters

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Frequency Band | **2.400 – 2.4835 GHz** (ISM) |
| Number of RF Channels (Classic) | **79** (1 MHz spacing) |
| Number of RF Channels (BLE) | **40** (2 MHz spacing) |
| Hop Rate | **1600 hops/s** (Classic), adaptive for BLE |
| Modulation (Classic) | GFSK, π/4-DQPSK, 8-DPSK |
| Modulation (BLE) | GFSK (1 Mbps) / LE 2M PHY (2 Mbps) |
| Nominal Range | **1 m – 100 m** (Class-dependent) |
| Max Data Rate (Classic) | **1 Mbps (Basic), 2 Mbps (EDR), 3 Mbps (HS)** |
| Max Data Rate (BLE) | **2 Mbps** |
| Encryption | **AES-128 (CCM mode)** |
| Power Classes | **Class 1 (100 mW, 100 m), Class 2 (2.5 mW, 10 m), Class 3 (1 mW, 1 m)** |

## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme — OECIT725 Module 3**, students must be able to: (i) describe the architecture of a Bluetooth sensor node, (ii) explain the piconet/scatternet topology, (iii) differentiate Classic Bluetooth from BLE, and (iv) discuss at least three real-world sensor applications. This section lays the conceptual foundation for all subsequent derivations and design problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Bluetooth Sensor Node

A Bluetooth sensor node is a **five-layer embedded system**:

1. **Sensing Layer** — Physical/chemical transducer (e.g., LM35 for temperature, MAX30100 for SpO₂).
2. **Signal Conditioning Layer** — Op-amp amplification, anti-aliasing filter, ADC conversion.
3. **Processing Layer** — Microcontroller/SoC (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4) running the Bluetooth Host stack.
4. **Radio Layer** — 2.4 GHz transceiver implementing FHSS, GFSK modulation, and link management.
5. **Application Layer** — GATT services, profile definitions, and user-facing mobile app/cloud gateway.

> [!NOTE] > **The "Why" of Layered Design:** Separation of concerns allows KTU engineers to swap out a faulty temperature sensor without redesigning the radio, or to migrate from a CC2640 to an nRF52840 SoC without rewriting the application logic — a classic **modular SoC design philosophy**.

## 2.2 Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS) — The Heart of Bluetooth

Bluetooth divides the 2.4 GHz ISM band into discrete hop channels. For **Classic Bluetooth (BR/EDR)**:

$$f_k = 2402 + k \text{ MHz}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 78$$

where $f_k$ is the carrier frequency of hop channel $k$. A pseudo-random hopping sequence (governed by the master's Bluetooth clock and address) determines the order in which these 79 channels are visited. Two key parameters:

- **Dwell time** $T_d$: time spent on each channel = $1/1600 = 625 \mu s$
- **Hop period** $T_h$: same as dwell time in Classic Bluetooth (625 μs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 79-channel hop plan was deliberately chosen so that 23 channels overlap with the 22 MHz-wide Wi-Fi channels. This overlap **forces** the Bluetooth hop sequence to skip occupied Wi-Fi channels via *adaptive frequency hopping (AFH)*, dramatically reducing co-channel interference in dense deployments.

## 2.3 Piconet and Scatternet Topologies

- **Piconet**: One **master** + up to **7 active slaves** (255 parked). All synchronization (clock, hop sequence) is dictated by the master.
- **Scatternet**: Interconnection of multiple piconets through shared slave nodes, enabling scalability beyond 8 devices.

The hop-sequence generation follows:

$$f_{n+1} = \left( f_n + k_1 \cdot p_1 + k_2 \cdot p_2 \right) \bmod 79$$

where $p_1 = 17$, $p_2 = 25$ are hop-increment constants and $k_1, k_2$ are derived from the master's address.

## 2.4 Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) — Optimized for Sensors

BLE re-architects the Classic Bluetooth protocol for ultra-low power. Key divergences:

| Parameter | Classic Bluetooth | BLE |
|---|---|---|
| Channels | 79 (1 MHz) | 40 (2 MHz) — 37 data + 3 advertising |
| Hop Rate | 1600 hops/s | Adaptive (1 hop per connection event) |
| Connection Interval | — | **7.5 ms – 4 s** |
| Modulation | GFSK/π/4-DQPSK/8-DPSK | GFSK only |
| Peak Current | ~25 mA | **~7.5 mA** |
| Sleep Current | ~50 μA | **~1 μA** |
| Encryption | E0/SAFER+ (legacy) / AES-128 | AES-128 CCM |
| Use Case | Audio streaming, file transfer | **Sensors, beacons, wearables** |

## 2.5 Power Budget for a Bluetooth Sensor Node

The average power consumption of a duty-cycled BLE sensor is:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{T_{tx} \cdot P_{tx} + T_{rx} \cdot P_{rx} + T_{sleep} \cdot P_{sleep}}{T_{period}}$$

where $T_{period}$ is the reporting interval. For a typical BLE temperature sensor:

- $T_{tx} = 3$ ms, $P_{tx} = 7.5$ mA @ 3 V
- $T_{rx} = 3$ ms, $P_{rx} = 7.5$ mA @ 3 V
- $T_{sleep} = 994$ ms, $P_{sleep} = 1$ μA @ 3 V
- $T_{period} = 1$ s

This yields $P_{avg} \approx 45 \, \mu W$, enabling multi-year operation on a CR2032 coin cell.

## 2.6 Friis Transmission Equation for Range Estimation

The maximum reliable range of a Bluetooth link (in free space) is governed by:

$$P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^2$$

Solving for distance $d$:

$$d_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{P_{r,min}}}$$

where $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(2.45 \times 10^9) \approx 0.1224$ m, $P_t$ is transmit power, $G_t, G_r$ are antenna gains, and $P_{r,min}$ is receiver sensitivity (typically **−80 to −97 dBm** for BLE).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_k = 2402 + k$ | Hop carrier frequency (Classic) | MHz |
| 2 | $T_d = 1/1600 = 625 \, \mu s$ | Dwell time per channel | seconds |
| 3 | $P_{avg} = \frac{\sum T_i P_i}{T_{period}}$ | Average power (duty-cycled) | W |
| 4 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi d)^2$ | Friis received power | W |
| 5 | $d_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{P_t G_t G_r / P_{r,min}}$ | Maximum range | meters |
| 6 | $R = \frac{N_{bytes} \cdot 8}{T_{tx}}$ | Effective data rate | bps |
| 7 | $E_n = k_B T_n B$ | Receiver noise floor energy | Joules |
| 8 | $SNR = P_r / (k_B T B)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_b/N_0 = SNR \cdot B/R_b$ | Energy per bit / noise spectral density | dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_{battery} = C_{mAh} / I_{avg}$ | Battery lifetime | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These formulas are the daily bread of KTU graduates working in IoT product companies (e.g., Bosch, Texas Instruments, Honeywell). The Friis equation (#4, #5) is used during **link-budget planning**, while the duty-cycled power formula (#3) directly drives **battery-life estimation** that appears in product datasheets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hop Sequence Generation (Master-Slave Synchronization)

**Problem:** A Bluetooth master device has the address $\text{BDA} = 0x1234567890AB$ and clock value $CLK_6$ (the 26-bit value) $= 0x1A2B3C$. Compute the first three hop frequencies in the connection state hopping sequence.

### Step 1 — Pre-conditioning
We need only the 27 least significant bits of the master clock, denoted $CLK_{27}$.

$$CLK_{27} = 0x1A2B3C \; \& \; 0x07FFFFFF = 0x1A2B3C$$
